q: A SysOps administrator must alert the operations team via email whenever an EC2 instance's CPU utilization exceeds 80% for five consecutive 1-minute periods. The alert must fire automatically without human intervention. Which configuration achieves this with the least operational overhead?
options: Configure an AWS Config rule with a compliance check that evaluates CPU utilization against an 80% threshold and sends Config compliance change notifications via SNS

Create a CloudWatch Alarm on the CPUUtilization metric with a threshold of 80%, a period of 60 seconds, and a datapoints-to-alarm of 5/5; configure the alarm action to publish to an SNS topic with the operations team email subscribed

Create an EventBridge rule that triggers a Lambda function every minute to call EC2 DescribeInstances and check CPU utilization, then publish to SNS if the value exceeds 80% for 5 consecutive Lambda invocations
Use Systems Manager OpsCenter to create an OpsItem when CPU utilization is high, then configure an OpsCenter notification to send email to the operations team
explain: CloudWatch Alarms are the native mechanism for metric-threshold alerting. Setting CPUUtilization > 80 with Period=60 seconds and DatapointsToAlarm=5 out of 5 evaluations means the alarm only fires after 5 consecutive minutes above 80% — preventing alerts on brief spikes. The alarm action publishes directly to SNS, which emails subscribed team members without any custom code. EventBridge with Lambda adds unnecessary complexity and cost for this straightforward threshold scenario. AWS Config evaluates resource compliance against configuration rules, not performance metrics. Systems Manager OpsCenter is for operational work item management, not automated metric alerting.

q: A SysOps administrator wants to use CloudWatch Logs Insights to analyze application errors. What must be configured FIRST before any queries can be run?
options: Configure EventBridge rules to forward events to CloudWatch
Create CloudWatch Alarms on relevant metrics
Enable a CloudWatch Logs log group and ensure the application streams logs to it
Create a CloudWatch dashboard to visualize log data
explain: CloudWatch Logs Insights queries are run against CloudWatch Logs log groups. Before any query can execute, the application must have a log group configured and must be actively streaming log events to that group. Without the log group and log data, Logs Insights has no data to analyze. EventBridge rules route events to targets but are not a prerequisite for log querying. CloudWatch Alarms and dashboards are useful after log collection is set up, not before.

q: A SysOps administrator needs to detect unauthorized API calls across the AWS account and create automated alarms. Which combination of services provides this capability?
options: EventBridge + CloudTrail
AWS Config + CloudWatch
CloudWatch + CloudTrail
CloudTrail + EventBridge
explain: AWS CloudTrail records all API calls made in an account and delivers them as log events to Amazon CloudWatch Logs. CloudWatch Logs metric filters can then match patterns (such as unauthorized API call event names or error codes like UnauthorizedOperation) and increment a custom metric. A CloudWatch Alarm on that metric triggers an SNS notification when unauthorized calls occur. AWS Config evaluates resource configuration compliance but does not alarm on individual API calls. EventBridge can also trigger on CloudTrail events in real time, but the standard SOA-C02 pattern for API call alarming is CloudWatch + CloudTrail.

q: A critical application requires monitoring of application-level metrics — such as active user sessions, queue depth, or business transaction counts — that are not available from standard CloudWatch infrastructure metrics. Which CloudWatch feature allows the application to publish these metrics for monitoring and alarming?
options: CloudWatch Synthetics
CloudWatch custom metrics
EventBridge rules
CloudWatch Logs Insights
explain: CloudWatch custom metrics allow any application or service to publish its own metrics to CloudWatch using the PutMetricData API or the CloudWatch agent. Application-level data such as business transaction counts, cache hit rates, or session counts can be tracked and alarmed on just like built-in AWS metrics. CloudWatch Synthetics creates scheduled canary scripts that test endpoints externally but does not enable applications to publish internal metrics. CloudWatch Logs Insights queries log data after the fact but does not create persistent, alarmable metrics. EventBridge rules route events but do not provide metric collection.

q: A SysOps administrator wants a single dashboard that displays the health status of EC2 instances, RDS databases, ELB load balancers, and active CloudWatch Alarms in one place. Operations team members should be able to view this dashboard in the AWS console without needing to navigate to each service separately. Which service provides this consolidated operational view?
options: AWS Health Dashboard, which shows the health of AWS services globally but does not display account-specific EC2, RDS, or ELB health status
Amazon CloudWatch Service Lens, which provides end-to-end application health views for X-Ray traced microservices but does not aggregate infrastructure-level dashboards across arbitrary services
Amazon CloudWatch Dashboards, which allow creating custom widgets displaying metrics, alarms, and logs from EC2, RDS, ELB, and other services on a single shareable console page
AWS Systems Manager OpsCenter, which aggregates OpsItems from multiple services into a unified operational view showing open issues, related resources, and incident history
explain: Amazon CloudWatch Dashboards allow SysOps administrators to build custom operational views by adding widgets for metrics (EC2 CPU, RDS connections, ELB latency), alarm status, and log insights queries from any AWS service. Dashboards are shareable via console URL and can be made public for read-only team access. Systems Manager OpsCenter aggregates operational work items (OpsItems) from multiple sources for incident management, not a general-purpose metrics dashboard. AWS Health Dashboard shows the health of AWS services globally, not account-specific resource health. CloudWatch ServiceLens is specifically for X-Ray traced distributed applications, not arbitrary infrastructure monitoring.

q: A SysOps administrator needs to monitor AWS Lambda function performance and errors, including invocation counts, duration, error rates, and throttles. Which CloudWatch feature automatically captures this data without any code changes to the Lambda function?
options: CloudWatch Logs
CloudWatch Synthetics
CloudWatch Events
CloudWatch custom metrics
explain: AWS Lambda automatically publishes invocation logs (including START, END, and REPORT lines with duration, memory used, and billed duration) to Amazon CloudWatch Logs, and also publishes built-in metrics such as Invocations, Errors, Duration, Throttles, and ConcurrentExecutions to CloudWatch Metrics — all without any code changes. CloudWatch Logs is the primary feature for capturing Lambda error details and performance data. CloudWatch Synthetics creates canaries to test endpoints externally, not internal Lambda performance. CloudWatch custom metrics require explicit PutMetricData API calls from the application. CloudWatch Events (now EventBridge) routes events to targets and does not collect performance data.

q: A SysOps administrator needs to detect anomalous patterns in AWS infrastructure metrics — such as unexpected CPU spikes or unusual network traffic volumes — without manually setting fixed thresholds. Which CloudWatch feature uses machine learning to identify deviations from expected behavior?
options: CloudWatch Events
CloudWatch Anomaly Detector
CloudWatch Logs Insights
CloudWatch Synthetics
explain: CloudWatch Anomaly Detector uses machine learning to model the expected behavior of a metric based on historical data, accounting for hourly, daily, and weekly patterns. It creates a dynamic band of expected values and can trigger alarms when the metric falls outside this band — enabling detection of unusual patterns without requiring manually tuned static thresholds. CloudWatch Logs Insights queries log data but does not perform predictive anomaly detection. CloudWatch Events (now EventBridge) routes events to targets and does not analyze metric anomalies. CloudWatch Synthetics runs canary scripts to test endpoints, not to detect metric anomalies.

q: A SysOps administrator configures CloudWatch Alarms for a production application. When an alarm fires, the on-call engineer must receive an SMS, the database team must receive an email, and a PagerDuty webhook must be called. All three notifications must be sent simultaneously from a single SNS topic. Which SNS configuration achieves this?
options: Create a single SNS FIFO topic and configure three subscriptions: one SMS endpoint for the on-call engineer, one email endpoint for the database team, and one HTTPS endpoint for the PagerDuty webhook — all subscribed to the same topic
Create three separate SNS topics (one per notification type) and configure the CloudWatch Alarm to publish to all three topics in sequence when the alarm state changes

Create a single standard SNS topic and configure three subscriptions on the topic: one for the SMS protocol to the on-call engineer's phone number, one for the email protocol to the database team's address, and one for the HTTPS protocol pointing to the PagerDuty webhook URL — SNS fans out the message to all subscriptions simultaneously

Use Amazon SES for the email notification, Amazon Pinpoint for the SMS notification, and Amazon EventBridge for the webhook call — SNS alone cannot handle multiple notification protocols simultaneously
explain: A standard Amazon SNS topic with multiple subscriptions is the correct pattern for fan-out notifications. When the CloudWatch Alarm publishes one message to the SNS topic, SNS simultaneously delivers it to all subscriptions regardless of protocol — email, SMS, HTTP/HTTPS, SQS, Lambda, or mobile push. One topic with three subscriptions is simpler and more cost-effective than three separate topics. SNS FIFO topics preserve ordering and deduplication but are designed for exactly-once processing with SQS FIFO subscribers, not for multi-protocol fan-out — FIFO topics do not support email or SMS subscriptions. Three separate topics require the CloudWatch Alarm to be configured with three separate action ARNs and create unnecessary management overhead.

q: A SysOps administrator needs to monitor an EBS gp2 volume attached to a heavily loaded EC2 instance. Users report intermittent storage latency spikes. Which CloudWatch metric is MOST USEFUL for identifying whether the gp2 volume is hitting its I/O credit balance limit?
options: BurstBalance — tracks the percentage of I/O credits remaining in the gp2 burst bucket; when it drops to 0% the volume is throttled to its baseline IOPS
VolumeQueueLength — measures the number of I/O requests waiting to be serviced and spikes indicate the volume cannot keep up with the request rate
VolumeThroughputPercentage — shows what percentage of provisioned throughput the volume is currently using relative to its maximum
VolumeReadLatency — measures average read latency per operation in seconds and is the best indicator of credit exhaustion
explain: EBS gp2 volumes earn I/O credits at a rate proportional to their size (3 IOPS/GB/s baseline) and can burst up to 3,000 IOPS by consuming accumulated credits. The BurstBalance metric tracks the remaining credit percentage. When BurstBalance reaches 0%, the volume is throttled to its baseline IOPS rate — causing latency spikes under high load. Monitoring BurstBalance and setting a CloudWatch alarm when it drops below a threshold (e.g., 20%) is the standard approach for diagnosing and proactively addressing gp2 credit exhaustion. VolumeQueueLength indicates backpressure but not the underlying credit cause. VolumeThroughputPercentage applies to provisioned throughput volumes (gp3/io1), not gp2 credit balance.

q: A SysOps administrator must ensure that all AWS API calls made by IAM users, roles, and services — including console logins, EC2 instance launches, and S3 bucket policy changes — are recorded for compliance auditing. Logs must be retained for 7 years and stored in a centralized, tamper-resistant location. Which service records these events and what configuration is required?
options: Enable Amazon CloudWatch Logs on each EC2 instance using the CloudWatch agent, which captures application-level events; configure log retention to 7 years using a log group retention policy
Enable AWS Config to record all configuration changes; Config records resource state snapshots at each change, providing a compliance history for all AWS API calls made against tracked resource types

Enable AWS CloudTrail with a multi-region trail that delivers logs to a dedicated S3 bucket with Object Lock in compliance mode configured for 7-year retention; enable CloudTrail log file integrity validation to detect tampering

Enable Amazon GuardDuty, which monitors CloudTrail events, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs for suspicious activity; GuardDuty findings retain the associated API call details for compliance auditing purposes
explain: AWS CloudTrail is the purpose-built service for recording AWS API calls from all sources: IAM users, roles, the AWS console, CLI, SDK, and AWS services acting on your behalf. A multi-region trail ensures that API calls in all regions (including global services like IAM) are captured. Delivering logs to S3 with Object Lock in compliance mode prevents deletion or modification before the 7-year retention period expires. Log file integrity validation (SHA-256 digest files) allows detecting any tampering with the log files. CloudWatch Logs records application-level events from EC2 workloads, not AWS API calls made to the control plane. AWS Config records resource configuration state, not the API calls themselves. GuardDuty analyzes events for threat detection, not for compliance audit logging.

q: A SysOps administrator is reviewing an AWS architecture for a new production workload that must handle 10,000 concurrent users and cannot tolerate more than 4 hours of downtime per year (99.95% uptime). The initial design uses a single EC2 instance in one Availability Zone. Which architectural improvement most directly addresses the uptime requirement?
options: Deploy at least two EC2 instances across two Availability Zones behind an Application Load Balancer with health checks; configure Auto Scaling with a minimum capacity of 2 to replace unhealthy instances automatically — this eliminates the AZ-level single point of failure
Upgrade the single EC2 instance to a larger instance type (e.g., from t3.medium to m5.2xlarge) to reduce the probability of performance-related failures that cause downtime
Enable EC2 termination protection on the single instance to prevent accidental termination, which is the most common cause of unexpected downtime in production EC2 workloads
Enable AWS Backup for the EC2 instance with daily snapshots so that in case of instance failure the previous day's snapshot can be restored within the 4-hour downtime budget
explain: A 99.95% uptime SLA allows only ~4.4 hours of downtime per year. A single EC2 instance in one AZ is a single point of failure — any instance failure, scheduled maintenance requiring stop/restart, or AZ disruption causes downtime. Deploying two instances across two AZs behind an ALB eliminates the single point of failure: if one AZ or instance becomes unhealthy, the ALB routes all traffic to the healthy instance within seconds (well within any reasonable RTO). Auto Scaling ensures a replacement instance launches automatically when one is terminated. Upgrading instance size reduces performance-related failures but does not prevent hardware failure, OS crashes, or AZ-level disruptions. Termination protection prevents manual API deletions but not system failures. Daily backup restoration typically takes 30-60 minutes (replacing instance, restoring snapshot, DNS failover) and still violates the 4-hour budget for frequent failures.

q: A company runs a batch processing workload on Amazon EC2 instances that processes financial reports every weekday from 6 AM to 10 AM. The workload requires 50 instances for exactly 4 hours each day and cannot be interrupted during processing. The company wants to minimize the cost of running these instances while ensuring the workload completes successfully every day. Which purchasing option should the SysOps administrator recommend?
options: Purchase 50 On-Demand Instances to ensure availability and run them only during the processing window each day.
Use a Compute Savings Plan covering 50 instances at the scheduled hours to reduce costs while maintaining availability.
Purchase 50 Reserved Instances with a Scheduled Reserved Instances plan for weekday mornings to match the exact usage pattern.
Launch 50 Spot Instances each morning and configure them with a Spot Instance interruption handler to retry failed jobs.
explain: A Compute Savings Plan provides up to 66% discount compared to On-Demand pricing and applies automatically to any EC2 instance usage, offering flexibility across instance families and sizes. Since the workload runs predictably for 4 hours daily and cannot be interrupted, committing to a Savings Plan for that consistent usage level delivers significant cost savings without risk. On-Demand pricing offers no discount for predictable, recurring workloads. Scheduled Reserved Instances have been retired by AWS and are no longer available for new purchases. Spot Instances are unsuitable because the workload cannot tolerate interruptions, and interruption handlers cannot guarantee completion within the required window.

q: A company's AWS bill has increased significantly over the past three months. A SysOps administrator suspects that several Amazon EC2 instances are over-provisioned relative to their actual workloads. The administrator needs to identify which instances should be resized and to what instance type, with the LEAST amount of manual analysis. Which approach should the administrator take?
options: Use AWS Cost Explorer's resource-level granularity to identify the most expensive EC2 instances and manually compare them against CloudWatch metrics.
Run AWS Trusted Advisor and review the Low Utilization Amazon EC2 Instances check to identify instances with consistently low CPU usage.
Review Amazon CloudWatch CPU and memory utilization metrics for each instance over the past 30 days and manually determine appropriate instance sizes.
Enable AWS Compute Optimizer and review its EC2 instance recommendations to identify over-provisioned instances and suggested right-sized instance types.
explain: AWS Compute Optimizer uses machine learning to analyze historical utilization metrics from Amazon CloudWatch and automatically generates specific rightsizing recommendations, including the exact target instance type and projected savings, requiring no manual analysis. This directly meets the requirement for the least manual effort. Manually reviewing CloudWatch metrics requires significant time to analyze each instance individually. AWS Cost Explorer identifies spending patterns but does not provide specific instance type recommendations. AWS Trusted Advisor's low utilization check flags instances below a CPU threshold but does not recommend specific target instance types or account for memory, network, and other dimensions.

q: A company is experiencing intermittent failures in a multi-tier web application hosted on AWS. Investigation reveals that an Amazon RDS MySQL database instance is intermittently running out of connections and CPU, causing application timeouts. The current RDS instance is db.m5.4xlarge. AWS Compute Optimizer shows the instance is over-provisioned for CPU but underprovisioned for connections. The company wants to resolve the failures and optimize cost simultaneously. Which solution best meets these requirements?
options: Enable Multi-AZ deployment on the existing RDS instance to distribute read traffic and reduce connection pressure on the primary instance.
Deploy Amazon RDS Proxy in front of the existing RDS instance to pool and reuse database connections, then downsize the instance based on Compute Optimizer recommendations.
Upgrade the RDS instance to a db.m5.8xlarge to provide more CPU and connection capacity, and enable RDS Auto Scaling to handle peak loads.
Create read replicas of the RDS instance and configure the application to distribute all database traffic across the primary and read replicas.
explain: Amazon RDS Proxy maintains a pool of established database connections and multiplexes application connections, directly resolving the connection exhaustion issue without requiring a larger instance. After deploying RDS Proxy, the SysOps administrator can then rightsize the instance to a smaller type as recommended by Compute Optimizer, simultaneously resolving failures and reducing cost. Upgrading to a larger instance increases cost and does not address the root cause of connection exhaustion efficiently. Enabling Multi-AZ provides high availability and failover but does not distribute read traffic or reduce connections on the primary. Read replicas can offload read traffic but require application-level changes and do not address connection pooling for the primary instance.

q: A company runs hundreds of AWS Lambda functions across multiple accounts in an AWS Organization. The company wants to understand which Lambda functions are the most expensive and identify opportunities to reduce costs by adjusting memory configurations. The solution must provide organization-wide visibility with the LEAST operational overhead. Which combination of services should the SysOps administrator use?
options: Use AWS Trusted Advisor across each account individually to identify Lambda functions with high costs and review memory settings manually.
Create a custom AWS Lambda function that queries CloudWatch Logs Insights across all accounts to calculate per-function costs and memory efficiency.
Deploy AWS Systems Manager Automation runbooks across all accounts to collect Lambda metrics and aggregate them in a central Amazon S3 bucket for analysis.
Enable AWS Cost Explorer with resource-level data and use AWS Compute Optimizer for Lambda function recommendations across the organization.
explain: AWS Cost Explorer provides resource-level granularity for Lambda function spending across an organization when enabled at the management account level, allowing identification of the most expensive functions without custom tooling. AWS Compute Optimizer analyzes Lambda function invocation patterns and memory utilization and recommends optimal memory configurations, directly enabling cost reduction. Together, these managed services provide organization-wide visibility with no infrastructure to manage. A custom Lambda-based solution requires significant development and ongoing maintenance. AWS Systems Manager Automation adds operational complexity with custom data aggregation pipelines. AWS Trusted Advisor must be reviewed per account and does not provide Lambda memory optimization recommendations at the required depth.

q: A company has been running production workloads on AWS for two years using On-Demand EC2 instances. A SysOps administrator has been asked to reduce compute costs by at least 30% without changing the instance types or families currently in use, and the company wants maximum flexibility to change instance families in the future if needed. Which purchasing strategy should the administrator implement?
options: Migrate all workloads to Spot Instances using Spot Fleet with diversified instance pools to achieve up to 90% savings while maintaining capacity.
Purchase Convertible Reserved Instances with a 1-year term, allowing instance type changes while still receiving approximately 31% discount over On-Demand pricing.
Activate a Compute Savings Plan with a 1-year commitment at a rate matching current On-Demand spend, providing up to 66% savings and full flexibility across instance families.
Purchase Standard Reserved Instances with a 1-year term for all currently running EC2 instances to receive up to 40% discount over On-Demand pricing.
explain: A Compute Savings Plan provides up to 66% discount over On-Demand pricing and automatically applies to any EC2 instance usage regardless of instance family, size, operating system, tenancy, or AWS Region, delivering both the required 30% minimum savings and maximum future flexibility. Standard Reserved Instances are locked to specific instance families and do not provide the flexibility requested. Convertible Reserved Instances allow changes but are limited to EC2 and offer lower discounts than Compute Savings Plans; they also have more administrative overhead when switching instance families. Spot Instances are inappropriate for production workloads that require consistent availability, as they can be interrupted with only a two-minute warning.

q: A company stores application log files in Amazon S3. The logs are actively queried using Amazon Athena for the first 30 days, occasionally accessed between 30 and 90 days, and rarely accessed after 90 days. Storage costs are increasing as log volume grows. The company wants to minimize storage costs while maintaining the ability to query all logs through Athena when needed. Which solution meets these requirements with the LEAST operational overhead?
options: Deploy an AWS Lambda function triggered by Amazon EventBridge on a weekly schedule to move logs older than 30 days to S3 Standard-Infrequent Access and older than 90 days to S3 One Zone-Infrequent Access.

Create an S3 Lifecycle policy to transition logs to S3 Standard-Infrequent Access after 30 days and S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval after 90 days, and update Athena queries to specify the correct storage class.

Configure an S3 Lifecycle policy to transition logs to S3 Intelligent-Tiering after 30 days, which automatically moves objects to lower-cost tiers based on access patterns while remaining queryable by Athena.
Create an S3 Lifecycle policy to transition logs to S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval after 30 days and S3 Glacier Deep Archive after 90 days, and use S3 Glacier Select for querying.
explain: S3 Standard-IA is cost-optimized for infrequently accessed data that still requires millisecond retrieval, making it ideal for the 30-90 day window. S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval provides the lowest-cost storage that supports millisecond access, making it queryable by Amazon Athena without restoration delays, while offering up to 68% lower cost than S3 Standard-IA. S3 Lifecycle policies automate all transitions with no operational overhead. Transitioning to S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval or Deep Archive would prevent direct Athena queries, as those tiers require restoration before access. S3 Intelligent-Tiering has per-object monitoring fees that can make it less cost-effective for large numbers of small log files. Using AWS Lambda for lifecycle management introduces unnecessary operational complexity compared to native S3 Lifecycle policies.

q: A company's SysOps team notices that their AWS monthly bill has unexpected charges. The team wants to set up proactive controls so that they are alerted before spending exceeds a defined budget threshold, and they want automated notifications sent to the finance team without requiring manual bill reviews each month. Which solution should the administrator configure?
options: Create an Amazon CloudWatch billing alarm on the EstimatedCharges metric with an SNS topic that notifies the finance team when the threshold is breached.
Configure an AWS Budgets budget with a cost threshold and set up email and Amazon SNS alert actions to notify the finance team when actual or forecasted spend reaches defined thresholds.
Enable AWS Cost Anomaly Detection with a monitor on the total AWS spend and configure an alert subscription to notify the finance team when anomalous spending is detected.
Schedule a monthly AWS Lambda function to query AWS Cost Explorer for the current month's spend and send a report to the finance team via Amazon Simple Email Service.
explain: AWS Budgets allows the team to set custom cost thresholds and configure alerts for both actual and forecasted spend, ensuring the finance team is notified proactively before the budget is exceeded, not just after the fact. Budgets support multiple notification channels including email and Amazon SNS, require no custom code, and update automatically each month without manual intervention. Amazon CloudWatch billing alarms only trigger on actual charges and cannot alert based on forecasted spend, meaning the team would only be notified after the threshold is already exceeded. AWS Cost Anomaly Detection identifies unexpected spending spikes relative to historical patterns but is not designed to enforce a specific budget threshold. A scheduled Lambda function adds unnecessary operational complexity and does not provide proactive forecasted spend alerts.

q: A company is running a containerized microservices application on Amazon ECS using AWS Fargate. The application experiences intermittent failures during peak traffic hours when tasks are unable to scale fast enough. The DevOps team has identified that the current task CPU and memory configurations are inconsistent — some tasks are over-provisioned while others are under-provisioned — contributing to both failures and unnecessary costs. The company wants to identify the correct resource configuration for each task with the LEAST effort. Which approach should the SysOps administrator take?
options: Install the Amazon CloudWatch agent on each Fargate task and create custom dashboards to manually analyze CPU and memory utilization over a two-week period.
Enable Amazon ECS Service Auto Scaling with target tracking policies based on CPU utilization to dynamically adjust the number of tasks during peak traffic.
Enable AWS Compute Optimizer for Amazon ECS on AWS Fargate and review the task-level CPU and memory recommendations to rightsize each task definition.
Use AWS Trusted Advisor to review the Fargate cost optimization checks and identify over-provisioned tasks that should be downsized.
explain: AWS Compute Optimizer supports Amazon ECS services on AWS Fargate and provides specific CPU and memory rightsizing recommendations for each task definition based on actual utilization data, directly solving both the over-provisioning and under-provisioning issues with minimal manual effort. Manually analyzing CloudWatch metrics requires significant effort across potentially dozens of task definitions and does not automate the recommendation process. AWS Trusted Advisor does not provide task-level Fargate rightsizing recommendations with specific target values. Amazon ECS Service Auto Scaling adjusts the number of running tasks but does not change the CPU and memory allocated to each individual task definition, so it does not resolve the misconfigured resource allocations causing the failures.

q: A company runs a critical e-commerce application on Amazon EC2 instances behind an Application Load Balancer. The instances are in a single Availability Zone. During a recent AZ outage, the application became completely unavailable for several hours, resulting in significant revenue loss. The company wants to redesign the architecture to achieve the highest availability while minimizing operational overhead. Which solution should the SysOps administrator implement?
options: Create an Auto Scaling group that spans multiple Availability Zones with a minimum of two instances per AZ, and configure the Application Load Balancer to route traffic across all AZs.
Launch additional EC2 instances in the same Availability Zone and configure the Application Load Balancer to distribute traffic across all instances.
Configure AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery to replicate EC2 instances to a secondary Availability Zone and trigger failover manually during an outage.
Deploy the application to a second AWS Region and use Amazon Route 53 with a failover routing policy to redirect traffic if the primary Region becomes unavailable.
explain: An Auto Scaling group spanning multiple Availability Zones with an Application Load Balancer provides automatic distribution of traffic and automatic replacement of unhealthy instances, ensuring the application remains available even if an entire AZ fails. This approach minimizes operational overhead because Auto Scaling and the ALB handle capacity and health management automatically. Adding more instances in the same AZ does not protect against an AZ-level outage, which was the root cause of the problem. Multi-Region failover with Route 53 is a valid high-availability approach but introduces significantly more operational complexity than multi-AZ. AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery is designed for on-premises to AWS scenarios and introduces manual failover overhead that contradicts the goal of minimizing operational complexity.

q: A company stores its transactional data in an Amazon RDS for MySQL Multi-AZ database instance. The company's recovery point objective (RPO) requires that no more than 5 minutes of data can be lost in the event of a failure. The company's recovery time objective (RTO) requires that the database must be available within 2 minutes of a failure. The current Multi-AZ configuration automatically fails over in approximately 60 to 120 seconds, but the company is concerned about data loss during unexpected failures. Which solution best meets both the RPO and RTO requirements?
options: Enable automated backups on the RDS instance with a backup retention period of 1 day and restore from the latest snapshot in the event of a failure.
Configure Amazon RDS Proxy in front of the Multi-AZ RDS instance to reduce failover time and maintain database connections during the failover event.
Create a read replica of the RDS for MySQL instance in a second Availability Zone and manually promote the read replica to a standalone instance during a failure event.
Convert the RDS for MySQL instance to an Amazon Aurora MySQL cluster with Multi-AZ enabled, which replicates data across six storage nodes in three Availability Zones with automatic failover.
explain: Amazon Aurora MySQL replicates data synchronously across six storage nodes in three Availability Zones, providing near-zero data loss (well within the 5-minute RPO) and automatic failover typically completing in under 30 seconds (well within the 2-minute RTO). Aurora's architecture is specifically designed to minimize both data loss and recovery time without manual intervention. Restoring from automated backups can result in up to 5 minutes of data loss and requires significant time to restore, failing the RTO requirement. RDS Proxy reduces connection overhead during failover but does not improve the underlying RPO of a standard Multi-AZ instance or guarantee faster failover for all failure types. Promoting a read replica is a manual process that can take several minutes and risks data loss since read replica replication is asynchronous.

q: A company runs a three-tier web application on AWS. The application tier consists of Amazon EC2 instances in an Auto Scaling group. During peak traffic events, new EC2 instances take 8 minutes to launch and complete their bootstrap configuration before they can serve traffic, causing degraded performance during scaling events. The company wants to reduce the time it takes for new instances to become healthy and serve traffic to under 2 minutes. Which solution meets this requirement with the LEAST operational overhead?
options: Create a golden AMI that includes the fully configured application and its dependencies, and update the Auto Scaling group launch template to use the golden AMI.
Use an AWS Lambda function triggered by an Auto Scaling lifecycle hook to install and configure the application on new instances before they are placed in service.
Increase the desired capacity of the Auto Scaling group to always maintain twice the number of required instances so that existing instances absorb traffic spikes without scaling.
Pre-warm the Auto Scaling group by manually launching additional EC2 instances before anticipated peak traffic events and terminating them afterward.
explain: Creating a golden AMI with the application and all dependencies pre-installed and pre-configured eliminates the bootstrap time, allowing new instances to start serving traffic almost immediately after launch and easily achieving the under-2-minute requirement. This approach requires creating the AMI once and updating the launch template, making it the lowest-overhead long-term solution. Pre-warming by manually launching instances adds significant operational overhead and human intervention before every peak event. Using Lambda with lifecycle hooks can reduce bootstrap time but adds architectural complexity with Lambda functions, IAM permissions, and hook management. Maintaining twice the capacity eliminates scaling delays but dramatically increases EC2 costs, which conflicts with the goal of reducing operational burden without mentioning cost increases.

q: A financial services company uses AWS Backup to protect its Amazon DynamoDB tables, Amazon RDS databases, and Amazon EFS file systems. A recent audit revealed that backup jobs are occasionally failing silently, and the operations team is unaware of these failures until they attempt a restore. The company wants to implement automated alerting so that the on-call team is notified within 5 minutes of any backup job failure. Which solution meets this requirement with the LEAST operational overhead?
options: Write an AWS Lambda function that queries the AWS Backup API every 5 minutes to check for failed jobs and sends an email notification using Amazon Simple Email Service (Amazon SES) when failures are detected.
Create an Amazon CloudWatch dashboard that displays AWS Backup job status metrics and configure the on-call team to review the dashboard every hour during business hours.
Enable AWS CloudTrail to log all AWS Backup API calls, create a CloudWatch Logs metric filter for backup failure log entries, and configure a CloudWatch alarm to notify via SNS.
Configure an Amazon EventBridge rule to match AWS Backup job failure events and route them to an Amazon Simple Notification Service (Amazon SNS) topic that sends email alerts to the on-call team.
explain: Amazon EventBridge natively integrates with AWS Backup and can match backup job state change events with a status of FAILED in near-real-time, routing them to an Amazon SNS topic for immediate notification with no polling delay and minimal configuration. This serverless, event-driven approach requires no ongoing infrastructure management and is the lowest-overhead solution. A Lambda function that polls the Backup API every 5 minutes adds unnecessary complexity, incurs invocation costs, and introduces up to a 5-minute detection delay. A CloudWatch dashboard requires manual human review and provides no automated alerting. Using CloudTrail with CloudWatch Logs metric filters is overly complex for this use case, as AWS Backup does not log job failures through CloudTrail in a way that is as reliable or immediate as native EventBridge events.

q: A company operates a media processing application that uses an Amazon SQS standard queue to decouple a fleet of Amazon EC2 worker instances from upstream producers. The worker instances run on On-Demand instances and process messages around the clock, but queue depth analysis shows that 70% of processing occurs during business hours and instances sit nearly idle overnight. The company wants to significantly reduce EC2 costs while ensuring that messages are always processed and the queue depth remains manageable. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Configure an Auto Scaling group for the worker instances using a target tracking scaling policy based on the SQS ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible metric, and use a mix of On-Demand and Spot Instances.
Replace the On-Demand EC2 instances with Reserved Instances to reduce the hourly cost, and keep the same number of instances running at all times.
Schedule the worker EC2 instances to stop every night at 10 PM and restart at 6 AM using AWS Systems Manager Automation, and keep On-Demand pricing for running hours.
Migrate the message processing logic to AWS Lambda with an SQS event source mapping so that Lambda functions process messages only when they are present in the queue.
explain: Configuring an Auto Scaling group with a target tracking policy based on the SQS ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible metric scales worker instances dynamically in response to actual queue depth, automatically reducing capacity during overnight low-traffic periods and scaling up during business hours. Using a mix of On-Demand and Spot Instances further reduces costs, as Spot Instances can provide up to 90% savings for the majority of the workload. Reserved Instances reduce the hourly rate but do not eliminate waste from idle overnight capacity, since the instances continue running at full count. Scheduled stop/start using Systems Manager does not respond to unexpected overnight traffic spikes that could allow queue depth to grow unbounded. Migrating to Lambda is a viable approach but requires significant refactoring of the application code, introducing development overhead rather than a simple infrastructure optimization.

q: A company runs a stateful web application on Amazon EC2 instances behind an Application Load Balancer. The application stores user session data in memory on each EC2 instance. When the Auto Scaling group scales in and terminates an instance, active users on that instance lose their session data and are forced to log in again. The company wants to eliminate session data loss during scale-in events while minimizing changes to the application code. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Enable sticky sessions (session affinity) on the Application Load Balancer target group so that each user's requests are always routed to the same EC2 instance.
Configure an Auto Scaling group lifecycle hook on the terminating state to delay instance termination and allow existing sessions to complete naturally before the instance is removed.
Take an Amazon Machine Image (AMI) of each EC2 instance before it is terminated so that session data can be recovered and restored to a new instance if needed.
Migrate session storage from instance memory to Amazon ElastiCache for Redis, and update the application to read and write session data to ElastiCache instead of local memory.
explain: Amazon ElastiCache for Redis provides a fast, in-memory data store that is external to the EC2 instances, allowing all instances to share session state. When an instance is terminated during a scale-in event, active user sessions remain intact in ElastiCache and are seamlessly picked up by other instances, eliminating session loss. This change is minimal — typically only requiring the session store configuration to point to an ElastiCache endpoint rather than local memory. Enabling sticky sessions on the ALB prevents a user from being redistributed to a healthy instance but does not protect session data if their sticky instance is terminated, only delaying the problem. Auto Scaling lifecycle hooks can delay termination to let sessions expire but do not actively protect session data and can significantly slow down scale-in operations. Creating AMIs before termination cannot capture in-memory session state and adds significant operational complexity with no practical benefit for session recovery.

q: A company hosts a business-critical API on Amazon API Gateway backed by AWS Lambda functions. The API serves customers globally and must maintain 99.99% availability. The company has identified that a regional AWS service disruption could make the API unavailable. The company wants to implement a multi-region active-active architecture so that the API continues to serve traffic with no manual intervention even if one AWS Region becomes unavailable. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Enable API Gateway Regional endpoint caching in the primary Region with a long TTL so that cached responses continue to be served during a Regional disruption.

Deploy the API Gateway and Lambda functions in two AWS Regions, configure Amazon Route 53 with a latency-based routing policy, enable Route 53 health checks on both Regional endpoints, and use Amazon DynamoDB global tables for shared data.

Deploy the API Gateway and Lambda functions in a second AWS Region, configure Amazon Route 53 with a failover routing policy, and use Route 53 health checks to detect Regional failures and redirect traffic to the secondary Region.
Deploy the API Gateway and Lambda functions in a second AWS Region and use AWS Global Accelerator with endpoint groups in both Regions to route traffic, without configuring health checks.
explain: Deploying API Gateway and Lambda in two Regions with Route 53 latency-based routing and health checks creates a true active-active architecture where both Regions serve live traffic simultaneously, and Route 53 automatically removes an unhealthy Region from DNS resolution if a disruption is detected, requiring no manual intervention. Amazon DynamoDB global tables ensure that data is replicated across both Regions so that either Region can handle any request independently. A Route 53 failover routing policy creates an active-passive setup where the secondary Region only serves traffic after the primary fails, which does not qualify as active-active. API Gateway response caching only serves cached data and cannot handle write operations or non-cacheable requests, making it unsuitable for a general-purpose API availability strategy. AWS Global Accelerator without health checks would continue routing traffic to an unhealthy Region endpoint, defeating the purpose of the multi-Region deployment.

q: A company runs an Amazon Aurora MySQL cluster with one writer instance and two reader instances in a single AWS Region. The application writes to the writer endpoint and reads from the reader endpoint. During a recent maintenance window, the writer instance was restarted, causing the application to experience connection errors for approximately 30 seconds while the application re-established database connections. The company wants to minimize application disruption during future database failover and maintenance events with the LEAST amount of application code changes. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Increase the number of Aurora reader instances from two to four so that the cluster has more capacity to absorb connections during a failover event.
Implement connection retry logic in the application code with exponential backoff so that the application automatically reconnects to the Aurora cluster endpoints after a brief pause.
Create an Amazon RDS Proxy in front of the Aurora cluster and update the application to connect to the RDS Proxy endpoint instead of the Aurora cluster endpoints directly.
Configure the application to connect to the individual Aurora instance endpoints rather than the cluster endpoint so that reads and writes can be distributed manually.
explain: Amazon RDS Proxy maintains a warm pool of persistent connections to the Aurora database and automatically routes connections to the new writer instance after a failover, reducing application-visible failover time from tens of seconds to just a few seconds. The only application change required is updating the connection string to use the RDS Proxy endpoint, which is a minimal code change. Connection retry logic with exponential backoff reduces errors but still requires the application to handle disconnections explicitly and adds development effort across potentially many application components. Adding more reader instances increases read capacity but does not shorten the writer failover or maintenance restart duration. Connecting to individual Aurora instance endpoints bypasses the cluster's automatic endpoint management, increasing application complexity and breaking the automatic failover routing that Aurora provides.

q: A company stores critical business data in Amazon S3 and needs to implement a disaster recovery strategy that meets a recovery point objective (RPO) of 1 hour and a recovery time objective (RTO) of 4 hours. The company wants the LOWEST cost solution that automatically replicates data to a secondary AWS Region without requiring manual intervention. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Deploy an AWS Lambda function triggered by Amazon S3 event notifications to copy each object to a destination S3 bucket in a secondary Region immediately after upload.
Use AWS DataSync to schedule hourly data transfer jobs from the source S3 bucket to a destination S3 bucket in a secondary Region. Monitor job completion with Amazon CloudWatch.
Enable S3 Cross-Region Replication on the source bucket to automatically replicate objects to a destination bucket in a secondary Region. Use S3 Replication Time Control to meet the RPO requirement.
Configure AWS Backup to create hourly snapshots of the S3 bucket and copy them to a secondary Region using a cross-Region backup copy rule.
explain: Amazon S3 Cross-Region Replication (CRR) automatically replicates objects to a secondary Region as they are written, with S3 Replication Time Control (S3-RTC) providing a guaranteed 15-minute replication SLA, which comfortably meets the 1-hour RPO. This is a fully managed, low-cost feature requiring no additional compute or orchestration. AWS DataSync introduces operational overhead in scheduling and monitoring jobs and may not meet the RPO consistently. AWS Backup is not designed to back up S3 bucket contents at the object level for CRR purposes and adds unnecessary complexity. A Lambda-based solution requires ongoing management of the function, error handling, and retry logic, increasing both cost and operational overhead.

q: A company runs a large fleet of Amazon EC2 instances across multiple AWS Regions to support its workloads. The company's cloud team suspects many instances are oversized and want to rightsize them to reduce costs. The team has no dedicated tooling in place and wants to identify rightsizing recommendations with the LEAST amount of operational effort. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Enable AWS Compute Optimizer and review the EC2 instance rightsizing recommendations in the Compute Optimizer console. Use the recommendations to downsize over-provisioned instances.

Install the Amazon CloudWatch agent on all EC2 instances to collect memory and disk metrics. Build a custom Amazon QuickSight dashboard to visualize utilization trends and manually identify candidates for rightsizing.
Use AWS Trusted Advisor to review the Low Utilization Amazon EC2 Instances check. Export the findings and schedule monthly reviews with the infrastructure team.
Use AWS Cost Explorer's rightsizing recommendations feature to identify over-provisioned EC2 instances. Apply the suggested instance type changes to reduce costs.
explain: AWS Compute Optimizer uses machine learning to analyze historical utilization metrics and provides specific EC2 instance type recommendations, including projected cost savings and performance risk ratings. It requires no additional agents or configuration and works across Regions with minimal setup, making it the lowest-effort rightsizing solution. AWS Cost Explorer's rightsizing feature provides recommendations but is less granular and doesn't account for memory utilization without the CloudWatch agent. Installing the CloudWatch agent and building QuickSight dashboards requires significant setup and ongoing maintenance. AWS Trusted Advisor's Low Utilization check is based on simple CPU thresholds and does not provide specific instance type recommendations.

q: A company runs production workloads on Amazon EC2 instances that require a consistent amount of compute capacity for the next 3 years. The company also runs variable, fault-tolerant background jobs that can be interrupted. A SysOps administrator must recommend a purchasing strategy that minimizes total EC2 costs while ensuring production workloads are never interrupted. Which combination of purchasing options should the administrator recommend? (Select the BEST single answer.)
options: Purchase 3-year Standard Reserved Instances for the production workloads and use Spot Instances for the background jobs.
Purchase 3-year EC2 Instance Savings Plans for the production workloads and use On-Demand Instances for the background jobs.
Use On-Demand Instances for the production workloads and purchase a 3-year Spot Instance savings commitment for the background jobs.
Purchase 3-year Compute Savings Plans for the production workloads and use On-Demand Instances for the background jobs.
explain: 3-year Standard Reserved Instances provide the highest discount (up to 72%) for stable, predictable production workloads, ensuring they are never interrupted. Spot Instances provide up to 90% savings compared to On-Demand and are ideal for fault-tolerant, interruptible background jobs. Compute Savings Plans offer flexibility across instance families and Regions but typically provide slightly lower discounts than Standard Reserved Instances for well-defined, stable workloads. EC2 Instance Savings Plans are less flexible than Compute Savings Plans but do not provide the maximum discount of Standard Reserved Instances for long-term commitments. Using On-Demand Instances for background jobs is significantly more expensive than Spot Instances when interruptions are acceptable.

q: A company wants to implement a disaster recovery strategy for its Amazon RDS for MySQL database. The company requires cross-Region replication with a recovery time objective (RTO) of less than 30 minutes. The company must also minimize ongoing storage and replication costs. Which solution meets these requirements at the LOWEST cost?
options: Use AWS Database Migration Service (AWS DMS) with ongoing replication to continuously replicate the RDS database to an Amazon Aurora MySQL-Compatible instance in a secondary Region.
Enable Amazon RDS Multi-AZ with two readable standby instances in a secondary Region to provide automatic failover with minimal RTO.

Enable automated backups on the RDS instance and use AWS Backup to copy daily snapshots to a secondary Region. In a disaster scenario, restore the snapshot to a new RDS instance in the secondary Region.
Create an Amazon RDS read replica in a secondary Region. In a disaster scenario, promote the read replica to a standalone primary instance.
explain: Amazon RDS cross-Region read replicas maintain a continuously replicated copy of the database in a secondary Region using asynchronous replication. In a disaster scenario, promoting the read replica to a standalone instance typically takes only a few minutes, meeting the 30-minute RTO. The cost is limited to the replicated instance and cross-Region data transfer, making it more economical than other options. Daily snapshot copies with AWS Backup would likely exceed the 30-minute RTO due to restore time, especially for large databases. Multi-AZ with two readable standby instances is a newer, higher-cost option designed for high availability rather than DR cost optimization. AWS DMS with continuous replication introduces additional service costs and operational complexity compared to a native read replica.

q: A company uses AWS Cost Explorer and notices that a significant portion of its monthly bill comes from underutilized Amazon EC2 Reserved Instances that no longer match current workload requirements. The company wants to reduce this wasted spend immediately without terminating existing reservations. Which action should the SysOps administrator take to address this MOST cost-effectively?
options: Purchase additional On-Demand Instances to run alongside the Reserved Instances, increasing overall utilization of the existing reservation commitment.
Modify the existing Convertible Reserved Instances to a different instance type, size, or tenancy using the AWS Management Console at no additional charge.
Sell the unused Standard Reserved Instances on the Reserved Instance Marketplace and purchase new Compute Savings Plans that better match current usage patterns.
Convert the existing Standard Reserved Instances to a different instance type using the Reserved Instance Marketplace to better match current workloads.
explain: Convertible Reserved Instances can be modified at no charge to change instance type, operating system, tenancy, or payment option, allowing the company to realign reservations with current workloads without losing the reservation discount. This is the most cost-effective and immediate way to reduce wasted Reserved Instance spend. Standard Reserved Instances cannot be converted to different instance types but can be sold on the Reserved Instance Marketplace; however, this assumes the existing reservations are Standard type, and the question implies they are underutilized Convertible Reserved Instances. Selling on the Marketplace and purchasing Savings Plans is a valid strategy but involves a waiting period for the sale to complete and incurs additional transaction steps. Purchasing additional On-Demand Instances increases total spend and does not address the root cause of underutilized reservations.

q: A company stores compliance-critical data in Amazon S3 and must ensure that all data can be recovered in the event of accidental deletion or ransomware encryption of S3 objects. The company requires that recovery points be retained for 90 days and that no user, including administrators, can delete backup copies during that retention period. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Enable S3 Versioning and S3 Object Lock in Compliance mode on the bucket. Configure a default retention period of 90 days so that no user can delete or overwrite protected object versions during the retention window.
Configure S3 Cross-Region Replication to replicate all objects to a secondary bucket in another Region. Enable MFA Delete on the primary bucket to prevent accidental deletions.
Enable S3 Versioning on the bucket and configure an S3 Lifecycle policy to expire previous versions after 90 days. Use an S3 bucket policy to restrict delete permissions to a dedicated IAM role.
Use AWS Backup to create daily S3 backup jobs with a 90-day retention policy. Enable AWS Backup Vault Lock with Compliance mode on the destination backup vault to prevent deletion of recovery points.
explain: S3 Object Lock in Compliance mode prevents any user, including the AWS root account, from deleting or overwriting protected object versions for the duration of the retention period. Combined with S3 Versioning, this ensures all object versions are retained for 90 days and are immutable, directly addressing both accidental deletion and ransomware scenarios. An S3 bucket policy restricting delete permissions can be circumvented by IAM administrators and does not provide true immutability. AWS Backup with Vault Lock is a valid alternative but introduces additional cost and complexity when S3 Object Lock natively provides the same protection. S3 Cross-Region Replication with MFA Delete does not guarantee immutability, as objects in the destination bucket can still be deleted by sufficiently privileged users.

q: A company runs a multi-tier web application with an Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL database that experiences high read traffic during business hours. The database is causing performance bottlenecks, and the company wants to reduce costs by offloading read traffic rather than upgrading to a larger instance type. The company also wants to minimize changes to the application code. Which solution meets these requirements at the LOWEST cost?
options: Migrate the RDS instance to Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL-Compatible Edition and enable Aurora Auto Scaling to automatically add Aurora Replicas during peak periods.
Deploy Amazon ElastiCache for Redis in front of the RDS instance. Update the application to check the cache before querying the database and write results to the cache after each query.
Enable Amazon RDS Proxy in front of the RDS instance to pool database connections and reduce the load on the primary instance during peak hours.
Create an Amazon RDS read replica in the same Region and update the application connection string to point read queries to the read replica endpoint.
explain: An Amazon RDS read replica offloads read traffic from the primary instance at a lower cost than upgrading the primary instance, with the application only needing to update the read endpoint — a minimal code change. This is the most direct and cost-effective way to scale read capacity for RDS PostgreSQL. Amazon ElastiCache for Redis requires significant application logic changes to implement cache-aside patterns, increasing development effort and adding a new service cost. Migrating to Aurora PostgreSQL-Compatible Edition involves a database migration effort, higher base costs, and Aurora Replicas add per-instance charges. Amazon RDS Proxy reduces connection overhead but does not offload read queries to separate compute resources, so it does not solve the read performance bottleneck.

q: A company runs a disaster recovery (DR) environment on AWS that mirrors its production workload. The DR environment consists of fully running Amazon EC2 instances and Amazon RDS databases that are never used except during a DR test once per quarter. The company wants to significantly reduce the cost of the DR environment without compromising its ability to recover within a 4-hour RTO. Which approach provides the GREATEST cost reduction while meeting the RTO?
options: Stop all DR EC2 instances and RDS databases when not in use. Before each quarterly DR test, start the instances and databases. Use Amazon EventBridge scheduled rules to automate the start and stop process.

Replace the fully running DR EC2 instances with AWS Auto Scaling groups set to a minimum capacity of zero. Replace the RDS databases with Amazon Aurora with Aurora Serverless v2 to scale to zero when idle.

Terminate all DR EC2 instances and RDS databases. Create Amazon Machine Images (AMIs) from the production EC2 instances and automated RDS snapshots daily. During a DR event, launch new instances from the AMIs and restore the database from the latest snapshot in the DR Region.

Use AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery (AWS DRS) to continuously replicate production servers to lightweight staging instances in the DR Region. During a DR event, launch full-capacity recovery instances within the RTO window.
explain: AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery (AWS DRS) continuously replicates production servers to low-cost staging instances in the DR Region, eliminating the need to run full-capacity EC2 instances and RDS databases at all times. During a DR event, full-capacity recovery instances can be launched within minutes, well within a 4-hour RTO. This approach provides the greatest cost reduction over a fully running DR environment. Stopping EC2 and RDS instances eliminates compute costs but still incurs storage costs for EBS volumes and RDS storage, and the start-up time during a real DR event may be unpredictable. Using AMIs and RDS snapshots provides the lowest cost but risks missing the 4-hour RTO for large, complex environments due to instance launch, configuration, and database restore times. Auto Scaling groups with zero minimum capacity and Aurora Serverless v2 reduce costs but introduce cold-start latency and complexity when scaling up during a DR event.

q: A company has a fleet of 500 Amazon EC2 instances running a web application across multiple AWS Regions. The operations team has noticed high CPU utilization on the instances and suspects that several instances are running outdated application versions that lack recent performance optimizations. The company wants to ensure all instances are consistently patched and running the latest application version with the LEAST operational overhead. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Create a new Amazon Machine Image (AMI) with the updated application and performance patches. Use AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager with a patch baseline and maintenance window to replace all existing instances with instances launched from the new AMI.

Use AWS CloudFormation StackSets to deploy updated launch templates referencing a new AMI across all Regions, then use an Auto Scaling group instance refresh to replace existing instances with the updated AMI.

Create a new Amazon Machine Image (AMI) with the updated application, then use AWS Systems Manager State Manager to associate a document that runs a shell script updating the application on all existing instances.
Manually connect to each EC2 instance using AWS Systems Manager Session Manager and run the update commands, then verify CPU utilization in Amazon CloudWatch.
explain: AWS CloudFormation StackSets enable centralized deployment of infrastructure changes across multiple AWS Regions and accounts from a single operation. By updating the launch template to reference a new AMI containing the performance-optimized application, and triggering an Auto Scaling group instance refresh, all existing instances are replaced in a controlled rolling manner with minimal manual effort. This provides the least operational overhead for a multi-Region fleet update. Using Systems Manager State Manager with a shell script is error-prone and does not guarantee image consistency. Systems Manager Patch Manager is designed for OS patch management, not full instance replacement with a new AMI. Manually connecting to 500 instances via Session Manager introduces significant operational overhead and human error risk.

q: A company is experiencing high CPU utilization on its Amazon EC2 instances running a three-tier web application deployed via AWS Elastic Beanstalk. The development team wants to test a new application version with performance improvements before rolling it out to all users. The company requires zero downtime during the deployment and wants the ability to instantly roll back if the new version causes issues. Which Elastic Beanstalk deployment policy meets these requirements?
options: Use the Blue/Green deployment policy by creating a separate Elastic Beanstalk environment with the new version and swapping the environment URLs.
Use the Immutable deployment policy to launch a new set of instances with the new version in the same Auto Scaling group, then swap them in.
Use the All at Once deployment policy to deploy the new version simultaneously to all instances for the fastest deployment speed.
Use the Rolling deployment policy to update instances in batches, keeping a portion of the fleet available during the update.
explain: The Blue/Green deployment in Elastic Beanstalk involves creating a completely separate environment running the new application version, then performing a CNAME swap to redirect traffic. This provides true zero downtime and allows instant rollback by swapping the CNAME back to the original environment if issues arise. The Immutable deployment policy launches new instances but does so within the same environment, making rollback slower and more complex. Rolling deployments reduce capacity during the update and do not support instant rollback. All at Once causes downtime as all instances are updated simultaneously, violating the zero-downtime requirement.

q: A SysOps administrator is managing a large number of Amazon EC2 instances and has noticed elevated CPU utilization caused by unpatched operating system vulnerabilities. The company's security policy requires that all EC2 instances in all AWS accounts within an AWS Organization receive critical OS patches within 48 hours of patch availability. The solution must provide a centralized report showing patch compliance status. Which solution meets these requirements with the LEAST operational overhead?
options: Deploy an AWS Config rule to detect unpatched instances and trigger an AWS Systems Manager Automation document to apply patches whenever a non-compliant instance is detected.

Create an AWS Lambda function that uses the AWS SDK to invoke the AWS Systems Manager Run Command on all EC2 instances across all accounts to apply patches on a scheduled basis using Amazon EventBridge.
Use AWS OpsWorks for Chef Automate to define recipes that apply OS patches across all EC2 instances and schedule the recipes to run every 24 hours.

Use AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager to create a patch baseline and maintenance window. Enable AWS Systems Manager Quick Setup with a Host Management configuration to deploy the SSM Agent and patch all instances across the AWS Organization from the management account.
explain: AWS Systems Manager Quick Setup with Host Management configuration automates the deployment of the SSM Agent and configures Patch Manager policies across an entire AWS Organization from the management account, providing centralized patch compliance reporting through the Systems Manager Compliance dashboard. This is purpose-built for organization-wide patching with minimal configuration overhead. Using Lambda and Run Command requires custom code, cross-account IAM role management, and does not natively provide compliance reporting. AWS Config rules with Automation documents address detection but add complexity and do not provide a native compliance dashboard. AWS OpsWorks requires significant additional infrastructure setup and management overhead compared to native Systems Manager capabilities.

q: A company runs a critical application on Amazon EC2 instances launched from an AWS CloudFormation stack. The SysOps administrator has updated the CloudFormation template to use a new AMI that resolves high CPU utilization issues from a poorly optimized system library. During a recent update attempt, the CloudFormation stack update failed midway, leaving the application in an inconsistent state. The company wants to ensure that future stack updates automatically roll back to the last known good state if an update fails. Which CloudFormation feature should the administrator configure?
options: Use a CloudFormation change set to preview all changes before applying them, and manually initiate a rollback if the change set produces unexpected results.
Configure a CloudFormation stack policy to prevent updates to critical resources, requiring manual approval before any resource replacement.
Enable rollback triggers by specifying Amazon CloudWatch alarms in the CloudFormation stack update configuration so the stack rolls back automatically if alarms breach during the update.
Enable CloudFormation drift detection to identify resources that have changed outside of CloudFormation and revert them automatically.
explain: CloudFormation rollback triggers allow administrators to specify Amazon CloudWatch alarms that are monitored during and after a stack update. If any specified alarm enters an ALARM state within the configured monitoring period, CloudFormation automatically rolls back the stack to the previously known good state without manual intervention. This directly addresses the requirement for automatic rollback. Drift detection identifies configuration drift but does not trigger automatic rollback during updates. Stack policies restrict which resources can be updated but do not provide automatic rollback capabilities. Change sets are a preview mechanism that still require manual action to initiate a rollback if problems are detected.

q: A company has observed high CPU utilization on Amazon EC2 instances and determined that the root cause is an application configuration file that is inconsistently applied across the fleet. The SysOps administrator needs to ensure that a specific configuration file is present and correctly set on all existing and future EC2 instances, and that any manual changes to this file are automatically corrected. Which solution meets these requirements with the LEAST operational overhead?
options: Use AWS Systems Manager State Manager to create an association that applies an AWS Systems Manager document distributing the correct configuration file to all EC2 instances on a recurring schedule.
Use AWS OpsWorks with a Chef recipe to manage the configuration file, and configure the recipe to run on all instances every 30 minutes.
Use AWS Systems Manager Run Command to distribute the configuration file to all existing instances. Configure an Amazon EventBridge rule to run the same command whenever a new instance launches.
Embed the configuration file in a custom AMI and use the AMI as the base for all EC2 instances launched via Auto Scaling groups.
explain: AWS Systems Manager State Manager is designed specifically to maintain a defined state on EC2 instances by applying associations that run on a recurring schedule. If a configuration file is manually changed or deleted, the next association execution automatically corrects it, ensuring continuous compliance with no manual intervention. Using Run Command with EventBridge only handles new instances and requires custom logic, not a purpose-built drift-correction mechanism. AWS OpsWorks with Chef Automate requires provisioning and managing an OpsWorks stack, adding significant operational overhead. Baking the configuration file into an AMI does not address drift correction on running instances and cannot dynamically update existing instances.

q: A company is using AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store to manage application configuration values for EC2 instances deployed via AWS CloudFormation. The operations team wants to update the application configuration on all running EC2 instances without replacing the instances or redeploying the CloudFormation stack. The company also wants to audit a history of all configuration changes. Which approach meets these requirements?
options: Store configuration values as AWS CloudFormation template parameters. Trigger a stack update with new parameter values and use the Rolling update policy to apply changes to instances without replacement.
Store configuration values in Amazon S3 as a JSON file. Configure a cron job on each EC2 instance to poll the S3 bucket every 5 minutes and apply updated configuration values.
Use AWS AppConfig to store and deploy configuration changes to EC2 instances, enabling managed rollouts and automatic rollback. Integrate with AWS CloudTrail for change auditing.

Use AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store with the Advanced tier to store configuration values. Use AWS Systems Manager State Manager associations referencing an SSM document that reads parameters and applies them to the instance, and enable parameter history for auditing.
explain: AWS AppConfig is purpose-built for deploying application configuration changes to compute resources including EC2 instances, with features including managed rollout strategies, validation, and automatic rollback if the configuration causes errors. AWS CloudTrail automatically records all AppConfig API calls, providing a complete audit history of configuration changes. Using CloudFormation stack updates with rolling policies replaces or restarts instances, which does not meet the no-replacement requirement. Systems Manager Parameter Store with State Manager can work but lacks the built-in rollout management and rollback capabilities that AppConfig provides. Using S3 with cron jobs adds operational overhead, lacks deployment management, and has no built-in audit trail for configuration changes.

q: A company is launching a new application on Amazon EC2 instances and wants to enforce that all instances must pass a series of custom configuration checks immediately after launch before they are considered healthy and placed behind an Application Load Balancer. High CPU utilization on prematurely registered instances has caused production incidents in the past. The company wants an automated solution that blocks traffic to instances until the checks pass. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Configure an Amazon CloudWatch alarm on the CPUUtilization metric and use an Amazon EventBridge rule to deregister any instance from the target group if CPU exceeds 80% within 5 minutes of launch.

Use EC2 Auto Scaling lifecycle hooks to pause instances in the Pending:Wait state after launch. Use AWS Systems Manager Automation to run the custom configuration checks, and then complete the lifecycle hook to move the instance to InService only after the checks pass.
Configure the Application Load Balancer health check to use a custom HTTP endpoint on each instance that returns a 200 status code only after all configuration checks are complete.

Use an Amazon EC2 user data script to run configuration checks during instance startup and signal the Auto Scaling group using the aws autoscaling complete-lifecycle-action CLI command when checks pass.
explain: EC2 Auto Scaling lifecycle hooks intercept instances during the Pending state before they are placed into service, allowing custom validation logic to run before traffic is sent to the instance. By integrating with AWS Systems Manager Automation, the configuration checks can be executed in a managed and auditable way, and the lifecycle hook is completed only upon successful validation. This prevents any premature registration with the load balancer. Using a CloudWatch alarm to deregister instances is reactive and still allows traffic to reach instances briefly before deregistration. ALB health checks are a good complementary control but rely on the application being able to self-report readiness, which may not cover infrastructure-level configuration checks. User data scripts can run checks but using CLI commands directly from user data is less reliable and auditable than Systems Manager Automation.

q: A company has a large fleet of Amazon EC2 instances across multiple AWS accounts and Regions. The SysOps administrator needs to create a standardized, pre-configured AMI with performance tuning settings that address high CPU utilization, and then distribute this AMI to all accounts and Regions automatically. The solution must track the AMI build pipeline and ensure only approved AMIs are used to launch instances. Which solution meets these requirements with the LEAST operational overhead?
options: Use AWS CodePipeline to orchestrate a pipeline that runs a Packer build on an AWS CodeBuild instance, produces an AMI, and then uses AWS Lambda to copy and share the AMI across accounts and Regions.

Use EC2 Image Builder to create an image pipeline that builds, tests, and distributes the AMI to all target AWS accounts and Regions. Use AWS Organizations service control policies (SCPs) to restrict instance launches to only approved AMIs.
Manually configure an EC2 instance with the required performance settings, create an AMI, then use the AWS CLI to copy the AMI to each target Region and share it with each account.
Use AWS Systems Manager Automation with the AWSEC2-CreateAndCopyAmi document to build and copy AMIs. Use AWS Config with a custom rule to flag any EC2 instance not launched from the approved AMI.
