Permanently provisioning for peak capacity is wasteful and expensive during normal operation. Exponential backoff helps the application handle throttling gracefully but doesn't prevent it.

q: A SysOps administrator needs to ensure that all Amazon EC2 instances in a production account always have the latest security patches applied within 72 hours of patch release. The solution must also generate a report showing patch compliance status for all instances. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Create an AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager patch baseline that approves patches 0 days after release. Configure a maintenance window to run the AWS-RunPatchBaseline document on all production instances within 72 hours. Enable patch compliance reporting in Systems Manager.
Enable AWS Config with the ec2-instance-managed-by-systems-manager managed rule and use Config compliance reports to track patching status.
Write a Lambda function that runs daily, checks patch availability via the SSM API, and installs patches on all instances using SSM Run Command. Store results in DynamoDB for compliance reporting.
Use Amazon Inspector to scan EC2 instances for missing patches and generate findings. Manually apply patches based on Inspector findings within the 72-hour window.
explain: AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager with a patch baseline set to auto-approve patches immediately upon release, combined with a maintenance window scheduled within 72 hours, provides automated patching that meets the time requirement. Systems Manager Compliance provides built-in patch compliance dashboards and reports without custom code. Lambda-based patching requires custom development and maintenance. Amazon Inspector identifies missing patches but doesn't automate remediation — patching must be manual. AWS Config with the managed rule tracks whether instances are managed by SSM, not their actual patch compliance status.

q: A company has a legacy application deployed on Amazon EC2 instances that generates configuration files at startup using instance metadata. When the Auto Scaling group launches new instances, they must retrieve the latest application configuration from a central location before starting. The company wants to decouple configuration management from the AMI. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Bake all configuration files into the EC2 AMI so instances have the correct configuration when they launch.
Use AWS Config to detect configuration drift and automatically push the latest configuration to instances.
Use an EC2 User Data script to retrieve the latest configuration from AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store at instance startup before launching the application.
Store configuration files in Amazon S3 and use an S3 trigger to push configuration updates to running EC2 instances.
explain: EC2 User Data scripts run at instance launch before the application starts. By retrieving configuration from Systems Manager Parameter Store in the User Data script, instances always get the latest configuration without requiring AMI updates. Parameter Store is centrally managed, versioned, and supports secure storage of sensitive values. Baking configuration into the AMI requires new AMI creation and instance replacement for every configuration change. AWS Config detects drift on existing instances but doesn't push configuration to new instances. S3 triggers respond to object changes but cannot push configuration to instances at startup.

q: A company operates a financial application that writes transaction data to an Amazon Aurora MySQL cluster. The application requires that no transaction data is lost even in the event of an Aurora primary instance failure. The recovery must be automatic with minimal downtime. Which Aurora feature meets these requirements?
options: Enable Aurora Backtrack to rewind the database to a point in time before the failure occurred without restoring from a backup.
Configure Aurora Global Database with a secondary Region to ensure data is replicated globally and can be recovered if the primary fails.

Enable Aurora Multi-AZ with at least one Aurora Replica in a different Availability Zone. Aurora automatically fails over to the replica in approximately 30 seconds if the primary instance fails, with no data loss due to synchronous storage replication.
Enable automated backups for the Aurora cluster with a retention period of 35 days to recover from any data loss event.
explain: Aurora Multi-AZ with Aurora Replicas uses synchronous storage replication at the storage layer across multiple AZs — writes are considered committed only after they are durable in the Aurora storage across all AZs. This means zero data loss on failover. Failover to an Aurora Replica is automatic and typically completes in under 30 seconds. Automated backups provide point-in-time recovery but require manual restoration time and have a potential RPO gap. Aurora Global Database provides cross-region replication but is designed for geographic distribution, not AZ-level high availability. Backtrack rewinds the database in time, which would lose recent transactions rather than preserve them.

q: A company runs an AWS Glue ETL job that processes 500 GB of data daily. The job currently uses 50 DPUs and takes 2 hours to complete. The data engineering team notices the job is idle for 40% of its runtime waiting for data shuffles. The company wants to reduce the cost of running this Glue job. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Split the 500 GB dataset into 10 smaller files and run 10 parallel Glue jobs with 5 DPUs each.
Increase the number of DPUs to 100 to complete the job faster and reduce the total runtime billing period.
Enable Glue job auto scaling to automatically add and remove workers based on actual workload demand, eliminating idle DPUs during data shuffle wait times.
Switch the Glue job from Spark to Python Shell to reduce the number of DPUs required.
explain: AWS Glue job auto scaling (available for Glue 3.0 and later) dynamically adds and removes worker nodes based on actual workload demand. During data shuffle phases where workers are idle, auto scaling reduces the DPU count, directly reducing cost. When processing resumes, it scales back up. This is purpose-built for addressing idle worker costs. Python Shell jobs support only 1 DPU and cannot process the Spark workloads efficiently. Increasing DPUs reduces runtime but total DPU-hour billing may be higher. Splitting and parallelizing with multiple jobs adds orchestration complexity and doesn't reduce idle time within each job.

q: A company stores application logs in Amazon CloudWatch Logs. The security team needs to search for specific patterns across all log groups in the account, including patterns like failed login attempts and access denied errors, and export matching log entries to Amazon S3 for SIEM ingestion daily. Which solution meets these requirements with the LEAST operational overhead?
options: Use CloudWatch Logs Insights to run scheduled queries across multiple log groups using the CloudWatch Logs Insights query scheduler. Export results to S3 using an EventBridge rule triggering a Lambda function that calls the CreateExportTask API.
Deploy Amazon OpenSearch Service and configure CloudWatch Logs to stream all logs to OpenSearch for pattern searching and export.
Create a CloudWatch Logs subscription filter for each log group that matches the patterns and sends matching events to an Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose delivery stream configured to deliver to S3.
Write a Lambda function that runs daily, retrieves all log groups, uses FilterLogEvents to search for patterns in each group, and uploads matching events to S3.
explain: CloudWatch Logs subscription filters with pattern matching send matching log events in near-real time to Kinesis Data Firehose, which can deliver to S3 with batching. This is a streaming, low-latency solution that requires no custom code and scales automatically. Setting up filters once per log group provides ongoing automated export. CloudWatch Logs Insights scheduled queries add Lambda code complexity and have query result size limits. Custom Lambda with FilterLogEvents requires iterating through all log groups and log streams, which is complex for large accounts. OpenSearch Service adds significant infrastructure cost and management overhead.

q: A company uses AWS CodePipeline for CI/CD. The pipeline deploys to a test environment and then to production. The production deployment must only proceed if automated integration tests in the test environment pass. The company does not want to manually approve each deployment. Which solution automates the quality gate between test and production deployments?
options: Add a manual approval action between the test and production stages and require a team member to verify test results before approving.

Use AWS CodeBuild to run integration tests as a separate pipeline stage. Configure the CodeBuild project to update an SSM parameter with the test result, and use a Lambda function to check the parameter before the production deployment.

Add an invoke action to the CodePipeline pipeline that runs an AWS Lambda function after the test deployment stage. The Lambda function executes integration tests and returns a success or failure status to CodePipeline, which proceeds or stops the pipeline accordingly.
Configure CodeDeploy to run integration tests as part of the deployment lifecycle hooks in the test environment and block production deployment on failure.
explain: A CodePipeline invoke action that calls a Lambda function can execute integration tests programmatically. The Lambda function runs the tests (or checks test results from a test execution system) and returns a success or failure result to CodePipeline via the PutJobSuccessResult or PutJobFailureResult API. CodePipeline proceeds to the next stage only on success. This is fully automated with no manual intervention. Manual approval requires human action for each deployment. CodeDeploy lifecycle hooks run during deployment but can't block a subsequent pipeline stage. Using SSM parameters as a handoff mechanism adds unnecessary complexity compared to direct Lambda integration.

q: A company needs to enable private connectivity between its Amazon ECS services and a third-party SaaS provider. The SaaS provider has made their service available through AWS PrivateLink. The company wants the ECS tasks to access the SaaS service without any traffic leaving the AWS network. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Configure a NAT gateway for the ECS tasks to access the SaaS provider's public API endpoint over the internet.
Create a VPC peering connection between the company's VPC and the SaaS provider's VPC to enable private routing.
Set up a Site-to-Site VPN connection to the SaaS provider's network and route ECS task traffic through the VPN tunnel.

Create an interface VPC endpoint in the company's VPC for the SaaS provider's endpoint service. Configure ECS task security groups to allow outbound traffic to the endpoint. ECS tasks access the SaaS service using the endpoint's private DNS name.
explain: AWS PrivateLink interface VPC endpoints allow consumers to access services offered by providers through private IP addresses within their VPC. Creating an interface endpoint for the SaaS provider's endpoint service establishes a private connection that keeps all traffic within the AWS network. ECS tasks use the endpoint's private DNS name to connect to the SaaS service transparently. VPC peering requires the SaaS provider to peer their VPC — PrivateLink is the standard mechanism for service providers. Site-to-Site VPN requires physical network infrastructure at the provider. NAT gateway routes traffic over the public internet.

q: A company uses multiple AWS accounts managed by AWS Organizations. The security team discovers that developers in member accounts are creating IAM users with console access and attaching the AdministratorAccess policy. The company wants to prevent this across all member accounts while still allowing developers to create IAM roles and service-linked roles. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Enable AWS IAM Identity Center and disable direct IAM user creation in member accounts through the Identity Center admin settings.
Create an IAM permission boundary that prevents creating IAM users and attach it to all developer IAM roles in each account.
Create an SCP that denies iam:CreateUser and iam:AttachUserPolicy when the policy ARN contains AdministratorAccess. Attach the SCP to the OU containing all developer accounts.
Use AWS Config with a custom rule to detect IAM users with AdministratorAccess and automatically delete those users.
explain: An SCP that denies iam:CreateUser prevents developers from creating IAM users entirely, while allowing iam:CreateRole for role creation. Adding a condition to deny iam:AttachUserPolicy when the AdministratorAccess policy ARN is specified provides an additional guard. SCPs apply to all principals in the account and cannot be overridden by account administrators. Permission boundaries apply per-role and require updating every developer role in every account. AWS Config with remediation is a detective and corrective control — users are created before detection. IAM Identity Center controls federation but doesn't prevent direct IAM API calls.

q: A company runs a critical API on AWS Lambda behind an Amazon API Gateway. During high traffic events, some requests receive 429 Too Many Requests errors because Lambda's account-level concurrency limit is reached by other functions. The company wants to guarantee a minimum number of concurrent executions for the critical API at all times. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Enable Lambda Provisioned Concurrency on the critical function to pre-warm function instances and eliminate cold starts during high traffic.
Implement API Gateway usage plans with throttling limits to prevent other clients from consuming Lambda concurrency.
Increase the AWS account-level Lambda concurrency limit by submitting a limit increase request to AWS Support.

Configure Reserved Concurrency on the critical Lambda function to guarantee a pool of concurrent executions exclusively for that function. Set the reserved concurrency to the minimum required to handle expected peak load.
explain: Reserved Concurrency allocates a dedicated pool of concurrency to a specific Lambda function that no other function can use. This guarantees that the critical API always has the configured number of concurrent executions available, even when other functions are consuming account-level concurrency. Reserved Concurrency also acts as a maximum concurrency limit for that function. Provisioned Concurrency pre-warms instances to eliminate cold starts but doesn't guarantee execution capacity relative to account limits. Increasing the account limit is a long-term improvement but doesn't protect the critical function from other functions. API Gateway usage plans throttle clients, not Lambda concurrency allocation.

q: A company's SysOps team needs to maintain an inventory of all EC2 instances, EBS volumes, and S3 buckets across 20 AWS accounts and three regions. The inventory must be updated in real time when resources are created or deleted, and the team needs to query the inventory using SQL. Which solution meets these requirements with the LEAST operational overhead?
options: Write a Lambda function that runs hourly, queries the EC2, EBS, and S3 APIs across all accounts using cross-account roles, and stores the results in Amazon DynamoDB for SQL querying.
Deploy AWS CloudFormation StackSets to tag all resources across accounts and use AWS Resource Groups to build a centralized inventory view.

Enable AWS Config in all accounts and regions. Set up a Config aggregator in a central account. Use Amazon Athena to query the Config resource inventory stored in S3 using the Config delivery channel.
Use AWS Systems Manager Inventory to collect resource data from EC2 instances and store results in an S3 bucket for querying with Amazon Athena.
explain: AWS Config continuously tracks resource configuration changes in real time across all accounts and regions. A Config aggregator consolidates this data from all 20 accounts and 3 regions into a central account. The Config delivery channel sends resource configuration data to S3, which can be queried using Amazon Athena with standard SQL. This covers EC2, EBS, and S3 natively without custom code. Custom Lambda requires building and maintaining API calls across all accounts. Systems Manager Inventory focuses on software inventory within EC2 instances. Resource Groups provide grouping but not queryable SQL inventory across accounts.

q: A company is migrating a monolithic application to microservices on AWS. As part of the migration, the team wants to use feature flags to gradually enable new microservice functionality for a subset of users before full rollout. The solution must integrate with their existing React frontend and Node.js backend without significant code changes. Which AWS service meets these requirements?
options: Use AWS CodeDeploy canary deployment to route 10% of traffic to the new microservice version and gradually increase the percentage.
Create an Amazon CloudFront distribution with Lambda@Edge functions that evaluate user attributes and route requests to the old or new implementation.

Use AWS AppConfig feature flags to define and manage feature flag configurations. Integrate the AppConfig SDK in the React frontend and Node.js backend to evaluate flag values at runtime. Use deployment strategies to gradually increase the percentage of users seeing the new feature.
Use Amazon API Gateway with a canary release deployment to route a percentage of requests to the new microservice endpoint.
explain: AWS AppConfig supports feature flag configurations natively with a dedicated feature flag configuration type. The AppConfig SDK integrates with any language or framework, including React and Node.js, with minimal code changes — just retrieve and evaluate flag values. Deployment strategies (linear, canary, all-at-once) control the rollout percentage. AppConfig is purpose-built for runtime configuration and feature flags. CodeDeploy canary deployment routes traffic at the infrastructure level, not at the feature level within an application. Lambda@Edge adds latency and deployment complexity. API Gateway canary releases route entire API traffic, not feature-level user targeting.

q: A company runs batch processing jobs using Amazon EC2 instances that are launched by Auto Scaling and terminated when jobs complete. The jobs take between 1 and 6 hours. The company uses On-Demand Instances and wants to reduce costs by 70% while accepting a small risk of job interruption. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Use AWS Batch with Fargate launch type to automatically manage container resources for batch processing at lower cost.
Switch from On-Demand to Scheduled Reserved Instances timed to match the batch processing windows.
Use EC2 Spot Instances for the batch processing fleet. Implement checkpointing in the application to save progress to Amazon S3, allowing jobs to resume from the last checkpoint if interrupted.
Use EC2 Reserved Instances with a 1-year commitment to receive a 40% discount on the batch processing costs.
explain: EC2 Spot Instances provide up to 90% savings compared to On-Demand pricing, exceeding the 70% target. For batch jobs that can tolerate interruption, Spot Instances are the optimal choice. Implementing application-level checkpointing to S3 allows jobs to resume from where they left off when a new Spot Instance is launched, minimizing the impact of interruptions. Reserved Instances provide 40-60% savings — below the 70% target — and require a 1-year commitment for variable batch workloads. AWS Batch with Fargate manages container infrastructure but Fargate pricing doesn't achieve 70% savings. Scheduled Reserved Instances require predictable timing and don't scale for variable workloads.

q: A company deploys containerized applications on Amazon ECS. The security team requires that container images are scanned for vulnerabilities before being deployed to production, and that images with critical vulnerabilities are blocked from deployment automatically. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Enable Amazon ECR enhanced scanning with Amazon Inspector on the ECR repository. Create an EventBridge rule that detects Inspector findings with critical severity and triggers a Lambda function to delete the image tag or add a block tag. Update the ECS task definition deployment process to check for blocked images.
Enable ECR basic scanning on the repository and manually review scan results before approving deployments.
Integrate a third-party container scanning tool in the CI/CD pipeline and store scan results in Amazon DynamoDB for deployment gate checking.
Use AWS Security Hub to aggregate ECR scan findings and create a manual workflow for security team review before ECS deployment.
explain: Amazon ECR enhanced scanning uses Amazon Inspector to automatically scan images on push and continuously as new vulnerabilities are discovered. EventBridge receives Inspector findings in real time, allowing a Lambda function to immediately act on critical findings — such as deleting the image tag, adding a 'quarantine' tag, or triggering an SNS alert. The ECS deployment pipeline can check for the quarantine tag before deploying. ECR basic scanning requires manual result review. Security Hub aggregates findings but manual review doesn't meet the automated blocking requirement. Third-party tools add vendor management and integration complexity.

q: A company's AWS environment has grown to 50 VPCs across multiple AWS accounts and regions. Network administrators are struggling to manage routing and connectivity between all VPCs. The company wants to centralize network management and enforce consistent routing policies across all VPCs. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Use AWS Direct Connect with a transit virtual interface to connect all VPCs through an on-premises router as a central hub.
Create VPC peering connections between all 50 VPCs in a full mesh topology and maintain routing tables in each VPC.
Deploy an EC2-based virtual router in each VPC and configure BGP routing between all VPCs across accounts and regions.

Deploy AWS Transit Gateway in each region and use Transit Gateway peering for cross-region connectivity. Use Transit Gateway route tables with route propagation and network segmentation policies. Manage everything from a central networking account using AWS Organizations.
explain: AWS Transit Gateway provides a centralized hub for connecting VPCs and on-premises networks. Using Transit Gateway in each region with Transit Gateway peering for cross-region connectivity creates a scalable, manageable network. Multiple route tables in the Transit Gateway enable network segmentation (e.g., separating production, development, and shared services). Managing from a central networking account with AWS Resource Access Manager for sharing Transit Gateways provides centralized control. Full mesh VPC peering requires O(n²) peering connections and doesn't scale to 50 VPCs. EC2-based routers require infrastructure management. Direct Connect through on-premises adds unnecessary latency and dependency on on-premises infrastructure.

q: A company hosts a web application on AWS that experiences sudden traffic spikes when promotional emails are sent to customers. These spikes cause the application to become slow or unresponsive for several minutes until Auto Scaling adds new instances. The company wants to ensure the application remains responsive immediately when spikes occur. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Increase the Auto Scaling maximum capacity and reduce the cooldown period so new instances launch faster when traffic spikes.
Pre-scale the Auto Scaling group manually before each promotional email is sent by increasing the desired capacity.
Configure predictive scaling for the Auto Scaling group based on the email send schedule, and maintain a warm pool of pre-initialized instances that can be quickly activated during traffic spikes.
Use Amazon CloudFront with caching enabled for all application content to absorb traffic spikes at the CDN layer.
explain: A warm pool maintains a pool of pre-initialized EC2 instances in a stopped or hibernated state. When traffic spikes and Auto Scaling needs to scale out, instances from the warm pool launch in seconds rather than minutes because they skip the initialization phase (OS boot, application startup). Predictive scaling anticipates the promotional spike based on schedule patterns and prepares the warm pool in advance. Reducing cooldown periods still requires full instance initialization time. CloudFront helps for cacheable content but doesn't help with dynamic application requests. Manual pre-scaling requires human intervention for each email campaign.

q: A company wants to implement cost allocation for AWS resources shared between multiple teams. Each team's resources are tagged with a Team tag. The finance team needs a monthly report showing AWS costs broken down by team and service type. Which solution meets these requirements with the LEAST operational overhead?
options: Activate the Team cost allocation tag in the AWS Billing and Cost Management console. Use AWS Cost Explorer with Group By Tag filters to view and export monthly cost reports grouped by Team and service.
Write a Lambda function that queries the AWS Cost and Usage Report data in S3 using Athena, groups costs by the Team tag, and emails a monthly report to the finance team.
Use AWS Cost Anomaly Detection to monitor per-team spending and generate monthly anomaly reports for the finance team.
Enable AWS Budgets for each team with cost alerts and have teams self-report their monthly costs to the finance team.
explain: Activating a cost allocation tag in AWS Billing and Cost Management allows that tag to appear as a dimension in Cost Explorer reports. The finance team can filter and group costs by the Team tag and service type in Cost Explorer's UI without any custom code. Reports can be exported to CSV on a monthly schedule. Custom Lambda with Athena requires developing and maintaining a cost reporting pipeline. AWS Budgets tracks budget adherence but doesn't provide the detailed cost breakdown report needed. Cost Anomaly Detection identifies unusual spending patterns, not structured cost allocation reporting.

q: A company runs a machine learning training workload on Amazon EC2 using GPU instances (p3.8xlarge). Training jobs run for 4-8 hours and are typically submitted 3-4 times per week with unpredictable scheduling. The data scientist team wants to reduce EC2 costs for these training jobs by at least 60% while accepting occasional job delays if capacity is unavailable. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Use EC2 Spot Instances for GPU training jobs. Configure EC2 Spot Fleet with multiple Spot pools (different instance types and AZs) to increase availability. Implement checkpointing to S3 every 30 minutes so interrupted jobs can resume.
Use AWS Batch with EC2 launch type and configure a compute environment with Spot Instances for GPU training jobs.
Purchase 1-year Reserved Instances for the p3.8xlarge instances to receive a 40% discount on training costs.
Use Amazon SageMaker Training Jobs with Managed Spot Training enabled to automatically use Spot capacity and handle interruptions.
explain: Amazon SageMaker Managed Spot Training automatically uses EC2 Spot Instances for training jobs and handles Spot interruptions natively — SageMaker checkpoints training state and resumes automatically when Spot capacity becomes available. This provides up to 90% cost savings with no custom checkpoint implementation. The team accepts delays (waiting for Spot capacity), which aligns with Managed Spot Training behavior. Using raw EC2 Spot Instances requires custom checkpoint implementation. Reserved Instances provide only 40% savings, below the 60% target. AWS Batch with Spot is valid but requires containerizing the ML workload and managing the training framework, whereas SageMaker provides native ML training management.

q: A company's SysOps team needs to execute an operational runbook that involves 15 sequential steps across multiple AWS services when a specific incident type is detected. The runbook includes API calls, approval steps, and Lambda function invocations. The team wants to automate this runbook while maintaining the ability to require human approval at critical steps. Which service meets these requirements?
options: Use AWS Step Functions to orchestrate the 15 steps with task states for API calls and human activity states for approval steps.
Use AWS Systems Manager Automation with a custom runbook document that defines the 15 steps including invoking Lambda functions and aws:approve steps for human approval gates.
Write a single AWS Lambda function that executes all 15 steps sequentially and sends an SNS notification for steps requiring approval.
Create an AWS CodePipeline with 15 stages, adding manual approval actions at critical steps in the pipeline.
explain: AWS Systems Manager Automation is purpose-built for operational runbooks. It supports a wide range of action types including aws:executeAwsApi for AWS service API calls, aws:invokeLambdaFunction for Lambda integration, and aws:approve for human approval workflows with SNS notifications. Runbook documents are versioned and can be triggered by EventBridge rules when incidents are detected. Step Functions also provides orchestration with human approval via callback tasks but is more complex to set up for operational runbooks. Lambda as a monolith for 15 sequential steps lacks visibility and granular approval capability. CodePipeline is designed for CI/CD workflows, not operational runbooks.

q: A company wants to implement a zero-trust security model for its AWS workloads. Applications running on EC2 instances must authenticate and authorize every API call between services, with certificates rotated automatically without downtime. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Configure security groups to allow traffic only between specific EC2 instances and use VPC Flow Logs to audit all inter-service communications.

Deploy AWS Certificate Manager Private CA to issue short-lived TLS certificates to EC2 instances. Use AWS Private CA connector for instance identity and configure applications to perform mutual TLS (mTLS) authentication for all inter-service API calls. Automate certificate renewal using the ACM SDK.
Use AWS Secrets Manager to store and rotate TLS certificates for each service and configure applications to retrieve certificates on startup.
Use IAM roles for EC2 instances and require that all inter-service API calls use IAM Signature Version 4 signing to authenticate requests.
explain: AWS Certificate Manager Private CA enables issuance of private TLS certificates for internal services. Short-lived certificates (e.g., 24-hour validity) combined with mutual TLS (mTLS) require both the client and server to present valid certificates, implementing zero-trust authentication for every API call. ACM automates certificate renewal, and with the Private CA connector, instances can request certificates programmatically. This implements true zero-trust at the transport layer. IAM Signature V4 provides authentication but is AWS-specific and doesn't implement mTLS. Security groups restrict network access but don't authenticate individual API calls. Secrets Manager stores credentials but doesn't issue PKI certificates.

q: A company runs a three-tier web application on AWS. After a recent infrastructure change, the application response time increased from 200ms to 2 seconds for some users but not others. The team wants to identify the exact component causing the slowdown and which user requests are affected. Which solution provides the MOST granular visibility?
options: Create CloudWatch dashboards with response time metrics for each tier and compare them to identify the degraded component.
Use Amazon CloudWatch Synthetics to run canary scripts that test the application from multiple regions and report response times.

Enable AWS X-Ray on the application. Instrument each tier (web, app, and database) with X-Ray SDK. Use the X-Ray service map and trace analysis to identify the slow component and filter traces by response time.
Enable VPC Flow Logs and analyze the network latency data to determine which tier has increased communication latency.
explain: AWS X-Ray provides end-to-end distributed tracing for each individual request through all application tiers. The X-Ray service map shows latency at each segment, and filtering traces by response time exceeding a threshold identifies the exact requests that are slow and which component (web, app, or database) is the bottleneck for those requests. This answers both questions: which component and which requests. CloudWatch metrics show aggregate performance but can't correlate specific slow requests to components. VPC Flow Logs capture network metadata without application-layer timing. CloudWatch Synthetics tests from specific endpoints on a schedule, not real user traffic.

q: A company has a fleet of Amazon EC2 instances that run a stateless microservice. The instances are in an Auto Scaling group behind an ALB. The DevOps team notices that during deployments, some in-flight requests to instances being terminated return errors to clients. The company wants to eliminate these errors during deployments. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Increase the Auto Scaling health check grace period to give instances more time to complete in-flight requests before being terminated.
Use blue/green deployments via AWS CodeDeploy so traffic is only shifted to new instances after they pass health checks.
Configure the ALB to use HTTP/2 multiplexing to keep connections open and retry failed requests automatically.

Enable connection draining (deregistration delay) on the ALB target group. When an instance is deregistered, the ALB stops sending new requests to it but allows existing in-flight requests to complete before the connection is closed.
explain: Connection draining (called deregistration delay in ALB target groups) ensures that when an instance is removed from the target group during a deployment, the ALB stops routing new requests to it but allows existing in-flight connections to complete within the configured timeout (default 300 seconds). This eliminates errors for requests that were in progress when the instance was deregistered. Health check grace period relates to when health checks start after instance launch, not termination. HTTP/2 multiplexing doesn't handle request completion during termination. Blue/green deployment is a broader strategy that also solves this, but deregistration delay is the simpler targeted fix.

q: A company's AWS account has been flagged by AWS Trusted Advisor for having an IAM root account access key that is still active. The security team wants to immediately remediate this finding and implement monitoring to detect if a root access key is ever created in the future. Which combination of actions should the administrator take?
options: Delete the root account access key immediately from the IAM console under the root account credentials. Create a CloudWatch metric filter on CloudTrail logs to detect CreateAccessKey events for the root user and configure a CloudWatch alarm with SNS notification.
Rotate the root access key by creating a new one and deleting the old one to ensure continuity, then monitor for root access key usage in CloudTrail.
Change the root account password to revoke existing root access key permissions and enable MFA on the root account.
Deactivate the root access key and configure AWS Config to detect active root access keys using the iam-root-access-key-check managed rule.
explain: AWS best practices require deleting (not rotating or deactivating) the root account access key since root should never have programmatic access keys. Deleting immediately remediates the Trusted Advisor finding. Creating a CloudWatch metric filter for CreateAccessKey events where userIdentity.type = Root provides continuous monitoring for future root access key creation, triggering immediate alerts via SNS. Rotating creates a new key (still a violation). AWS Config's iam-root-access-key-check detects active keys with periodic evaluation, which is slower than real-time CloudTrail monitoring. Changing the root password doesn't revoke access keys.

q: A company uses Amazon Route 53 to manage DNS for its SaaS application. Different customers are routed to different application environments (tenant isolation) based on their subdomain (customer1.app.com, customer2.app.com). The company has hundreds of customers and new customers are onboarded frequently. The solution must automatically create DNS records for new customers without manual DNS updates. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Create a wildcard DNS record (*.app.com) in Route 53 pointing to a single ALB, and use the ALB listener rules to route traffic based on the Host header to customer-specific target groups.

Use the Route 53 API or SDK in the customer onboarding workflow to programmatically create a Route 53 CNAME or A record for each new customer subdomain pointing to their dedicated ALB or CloudFront distribution.
Use Amazon CloudFront with multiple origins and configure a Lambda@Edge function to route requests based on the Host header.
Manually create Route 53 records for each customer during onboarding and document the process in the operations runbook.
explain: Integrating Route 53 API calls into the automated customer onboarding workflow eliminates manual DNS updates. When a new customer is provisioned, the workflow creates the subdomain record programmatically using the Route 53 SDK. This scales to hundreds or thousands of customers without operational overhead. Manual record creation doesn't scale and is error-prone. A wildcard DNS record with ALB host-based routing works for directing traffic but still requires per-customer ALB listener rules and target groups. Lambda@Edge routing is complex and doesn't eliminate the need for DNS records per customer subdomain.

q: A company stores 10 TB of data in Amazon S3 Standard. The data is accessed once when uploaded, processed within 24 hours, and then never accessed again. The company wants to minimize storage costs for the data after processing while ensuring it is retained for 1 year for compliance. Which solution minimizes costs?
options: Create an S3 Lifecycle policy to transition objects to S3 Standard-IA after 30 days and delete them after 365 days.
Create an S3 Lifecycle policy to transition objects to S3 Glacier Deep Archive after 24 hours and configure a lifecycle expiration rule to delete objects after 365 days.
Use S3 Intelligent-Tiering to automatically move the data to the lowest cost tier based on access patterns.
Store all data in S3 Standard for the entire 1-year retention period and use server-side encryption to ensure compliance.
explain: S3 Glacier Deep Archive is the lowest-cost S3 storage class at approximately $0.00099/GB/month. Since the data is never accessed after the first 24 hours and only needs to be retained for compliance (not quick retrieval), Glacier Deep Archive is ideal. Transitioning after 24 hours and expiring at 365 days minimizes total storage costs. S3 Standard for the full year is the most expensive option. S3 Standard-IA is cheaper than Standard but significantly more expensive than Glacier Deep Archive for data that is never accessed. Intelligent-Tiering incurs a per-object monitoring fee and won't move rarely accessed data to Glacier Deep Archive automatically without configuration.

q: A company runs a Node.js application on AWS Elastic Beanstalk. The application needs to run a database migration script before each deployment to update the database schema. The migration must run exactly once per deployment, before the new application version starts serving traffic. Which Elastic Beanstalk feature meets these requirements?
options: Use an Elastic Beanstalk environment hook to trigger a Lambda function that runs the migration script during deployment.
Add the migration script to the application's startup code so it runs when each EC2 instance starts the Node.js application.
Configure an EC2 User Data script to run the migration before the Elastic Beanstalk application is installed.

Create an .ebextensions configuration file with a container command that runs the migration script. Container commands run before the application is started and run only on one instance using the leader_only option.
explain: Elastic Beanstalk .ebextensions container_commands run during the deployment process, after the application source is extracted but before the application starts. The leader_only: true option ensures the command runs on only one instance in the environment (the leader), making it suitable for database migrations that must run exactly once. This is the Elastic Beanstalk native pattern for pre-deployment tasks. Running migrations in application startup code runs on every instance on every start, risking concurrent migration execution. Elastic Beanstalk doesn't have native environment hooks for Lambda. EC2 User Data runs once at instance launch, not on each deployment.

q: A company's application uses Amazon SQS to decouple its components. A producer service sends messages to SQS. A consumer Lambda function processes messages and writes results to Amazon DynamoDB. During a DynamoDB outage, the Lambda function fails, and messages are returned to the queue and retried continuously, consuming Lambda concurrency. The company wants to handle this gracefully without losing messages. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Increase the Lambda function timeout to allow it to wait for DynamoDB to recover before failing.
Enable SQS FIFO queues to prevent duplicate message processing during the outage.
Configure Lambda Reserved Concurrency to zero to stop processing messages during the DynamoDB outage.

Configure a Dead Letter Queue on the SQS queue with an appropriate maxReceiveCount. Set the SQS visibility timeout longer than the Lambda timeout. When DynamoDB recovers, process messages from the DLQ.
explain: A Dead Letter Queue with a configured maxReceiveCount (e.g., 3) automatically moves messages that fail multiple times to the DLQ, preventing the endless retry loop that consumes Lambda concurrency. Setting the SQS visibility timeout longer than the Lambda timeout prevents the message from becoming visible again while Lambda is still processing it. When DynamoDB recovers, the team can redrive messages from the DLQ back to the source queue for reprocessing. Increasing Lambda timeout wastes concurrency waiting for DynamoDB. Setting Reserved Concurrency to zero stops all processing but doesn't handle the messages gracefully. FIFO queues ensure ordering but don't handle downstream service failures.

q: A company uses AWS CloudTrail to monitor API activity. The security team wants to detect when S3 bucket policies are modified to grant public access and automatically remove the public access within 5 minutes of the change. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Create an Amazon EventBridge rule that matches CloudTrail PutBucketPolicy events. Trigger an AWS Lambda function that evaluates the new bucket policy and removes public access by enabling S3 Block Public Access settings on the affected bucket.
Enable AWS Config with the s3-bucket-public-read-prohibited rule and configure automatic remediation to run an SSM Automation document that enables S3 Block Public Access.
Use Amazon Macie to detect when S3 buckets become public and trigger an SNS notification to the security team for manual remediation.
Enable S3 server access logging on all buckets and use CloudWatch Logs metric filters to detect public access policy changes.
explain: EventBridge can detect CloudTrail PutBucketPolicy events in near real time (within seconds). The Lambda function evaluates the new policy for public access statements and enables S3 Block Public Access settings on the bucket, effectively overriding any public access grants in the bucket policy. This entire response chain typically completes within 1-2 minutes, well within the 5-minute requirement. AWS Config evaluation runs every few minutes on configuration changes and then triggers SSM Automation — slightly slower but also valid. Macie focuses on data discovery and doesn't monitor policy changes in real time. S3 access logging captures data plane events, not management API calls.

q: A company uses Amazon EKS to run containerized workloads. The platform team wants to monitor Kubernetes control plane logs (API server, scheduler, controller manager) and pod-level application logs from a central location. They also need the ability to search logs using full-text queries. Which solution meets these requirements with the LEAST operational overhead?
options: Configure EKS pod logs to write to a shared EFS volume and run a search application on an EC2 instance to index and query the logs.

Enable EKS control plane logging to Amazon CloudWatch Logs. Deploy the Amazon CloudWatch Container Insights agent (Fluent Bit) as a DaemonSet to collect pod logs and send them to CloudWatch Logs. Use CloudWatch Logs Insights for full-text log search.
Deploy an ELK stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) on EC2 instances to collect, store, and search all EKS logs.
Use Amazon Kinesis Data Streams to collect EKS logs and process them with Lambda functions that store searchable logs in DynamoDB.
explain: EKS supports native control plane log export to CloudWatch Logs with a single configuration toggle. AWS provides the CloudWatch Container Insights DaemonSet (based on Fluent Bit) as a managed add-on that automatically collects pod-level logs and ships them to CloudWatch Logs without manual Fluent Bit configuration. CloudWatch Logs Insights provides full-text search with SQL-like query syntax. ELK stack requires provisioning, configuring, and maintaining three components. Kinesis with Lambda requires custom processing logic. Shared EFS with a custom search application adds significant operational overhead.

q: A company needs to migrate 8 TB of data from an on-premises NFS server to Amazon EFS. The migration must minimize impact on the production NFS server and complete within 5 days. The on-premises environment has a 1 Gbps internet connection. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Set up an AWS Direct Connect connection and use rsync to copy data from the NFS server to Amazon EFS over the dedicated connection.
Use the aws s3 cp command to copy data from the NFS server to Amazon S3 and then use an AWS Lambda function to move data from S3 to Amazon EFS.

Deploy AWS DataSync on-premises agent and configure a DataSync task to transfer data from the NFS source to the Amazon EFS destination. DataSync uses parallel transfers and automatic retries, and can saturate available bandwidth while throttling NFS server impact.
Order an AWS Snowball Edge device, copy 8 TB of NFS data to it, and ship it to AWS for import to Amazon EFS.
explain: AWS DataSync is purpose-built for data migration between NFS servers and AWS storage services including Amazon EFS. A DataSync on-premises agent connects to the source NFS server and transfers data in parallel to EFS using the DataSync service. DataSync has built-in throttling controls to minimize impact on the source NFS server during business hours. An 8 TB transfer over a 1 Gbps connection with DataSync optimization is achievable within 5 days. S3 to EFS migration via Lambda is not a native capability and requires custom code. Snowball Edge ships physical devices taking 1-2 weeks. Direct Connect setup takes weeks and rsync is single-threaded.

q: A company hosts static website content in Amazon S3 and serves it through Amazon CloudFront. After reviewing the AWS bill, they notice CloudFront origin request charges are high because the cache hit ratio is only 20%. The website serves different content for authenticated users based on their user type (standard vs premium). Which change will improve the cache hit ratio for unauthenticated content?
options: Increase the CloudFront default TTL from 24 hours to 7 days to keep objects cached longer.

Separate the CloudFront behaviors: create one cache behavior for unauthenticated static content (no cookie forwarding, high TTL) and another for authenticated API requests (forward auth cookies, dynamic TTL). This allows static content to be cached effectively.
Enable CloudFront real-time logs and analyze request patterns to identify which objects are not being cached.
Enable CloudFront Origin Shield to add a centralized caching layer and reduce origin requests for all content.
explain: The low cache hit ratio is likely caused by a cache behavior that forwards authentication cookies for all requests. Since cookies are part of the cache key, each unique cookie value creates a separate cache entry — making static content unique per user and eliminating caching. Separating into two behaviors fixes this: unauthenticated static content gets a no-cookie cache behavior with high TTL (99%+ cache hit ratio for static assets), while authenticated API requests forward cookies with dynamic TTLs. Origin Shield helps reduce origin requests but doesn't fix the root cause of cache fragmentation. Increasing TTL helps for currently-cached objects but not for the miss caused by unique cookies. Log analysis identifies the problem but doesn't fix it.

q: A company runs Amazon Aurora Serverless v2 to support a multi-tenant SaaS application. Some tenants run scheduled batch jobs at the same time, causing the Aurora cluster to scale rapidly and then become underutilized between jobs. The company wants to reduce costs by optimizing Aurora capacity without impacting batch job performance. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Switch from Aurora Serverless v2 to a provisioned Aurora cluster with a fixed instance size that meets peak demand.

Configure Aurora Serverless v2 minimum ACU to a low value (e.g., 0.5 ACU) and maximum ACU to handle peak batch load. Aurora will scale up automatically when batch jobs run and scale down during idle periods, reducing costs between jobs.
Enable Aurora Auto Scaling to add reader instances during batch job peaks and remove them when not needed.
Configure separate Aurora clusters for each tenant to isolate batch job impact and optimize per-tenant capacity.
explain: Aurora Serverless v2 is designed for variable workloads — it scales in fine-grained 0.5 ACU increments based on actual demand. Setting a low minimum ACU reduces the cost floor during idle periods while the high maximum ACU ensures batch jobs have sufficient capacity. The scaling happens in seconds, so even rapid batch job spikes are handled automatically. Aurora Auto Scaling adds and removes reader instances, which helps for read workloads but doesn't address write capacity scaling. A provisioned cluster with fixed capacity wastes money when underutilized. Separate clusters per tenant multiply infrastructure costs significantly.

q: A company uses AWS Organizations with 30 member accounts. The security team needs to ensure all member accounts have Amazon GuardDuty enabled and that it cannot be disabled by member account administrators. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Designate an administrator account for GuardDuty in AWS Organizations. From the GuardDuty delegated administrator account, enable GuardDuty across all existing member accounts and enable auto-enablement for new accounts. Use an SCP to deny the guardduty:DisableOrganizationAdminAccount and guardduty:DeleteDetector actions in member accounts.
Use AWS CloudFormation StackSets to deploy a CloudFormation stack in all member accounts that enables GuardDuty.
Create an AWS Config rule that detects when GuardDuty is disabled and uses automatic remediation to re-enable it.
Enable GuardDuty manually in each of the 30 member accounts by logging in individually and enabling the service.
explain: GuardDuty Organizations integration supports centralized management through a delegated administrator account. The admin can enable GuardDuty in all member accounts simultaneously and configure auto-enablement for accounts added in the future. An SCP that denies guardduty:DeleteDetector in member accounts prevents member administrators from disabling GuardDuty. This combination ensures continuous coverage. Manual enablement doesn't scale and can be reversed. CloudFormation StackSets enable GuardDuty but don't prevent member administrators from disabling it later. AWS Config remediation re-enables GuardDuty after it's disabled — there's a gap between disablement and re-enablement.

q: A company runs multiple microservices on Amazon EKS. The team wants to implement canary deployments for individual microservices, routing 5% of traffic to a new version and gradually increasing to 100% based on error rates. The solution must not require changes to the microservice code. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Use Amazon CloudFront with Lambda@Edge to route 5% of requests to the canary environment based on a random header value.
Use Kubernetes native Deployment objects with two deployments (stable and canary) and adjust the replica count ratio between them to approximate 5% canary traffic.

Deploy AWS App Mesh as the service mesh for the EKS cluster. Configure App Mesh virtual routers with weighted routes to split traffic between the stable and canary versions of each microservice. Use CloudWatch metrics to monitor error rates and adjust weights programmatically.
Configure an Application Load Balancer with weighted target groups for each microservice version and use ALB weighted routing to split traffic.
explain: AWS App Mesh provides a service mesh that intercepts all microservice traffic using Envoy proxy sidecar containers without any application code changes. Virtual routers with weighted routes allow precise traffic splitting (e.g., 95% to stable, 5% to canary). Weights can be updated programmatically via the App Mesh API based on error rate metrics from CloudWatch. This enables automated canary progression. Kubernetes replica-count-based traffic splitting is imprecise (e.g., 1/20 replicas = 5%) and scales all pods. ALB weighted target groups work at the load balancer level, not service mesh level, requiring separate target groups per version. Lambda@Edge introduces latency and requires complex routing logic.

q: A company's AWS environment spans 5 accounts and 3 regions. The security team wants a single pane of glass view of all security findings including GuardDuty findings, AWS Config non-compliance, IAM Access Analyzer findings, and Inspector vulnerabilities across all accounts and regions. Which service provides this aggregated view with the LEAST operational overhead?
options: Write an AWS Lambda function that queries each security service API in each account and region and stores normalized findings in a central DynamoDB table.
Deploy Amazon OpenSearch Service in a central account and configure all security services to stream findings to OpenSearch for unified search and visualization.
Create a central Amazon CloudWatch dashboard that queries metrics from all accounts and regions using cross-account and cross-region dashboards.

Enable AWS Security Hub with AWS Organizations integration. Designate a Security Hub administrator account to aggregate findings from all member accounts and regions. Enable relevant security standards and integrate GuardDuty, Config, Inspector, and Access Analyzer as finding providers.
explain: AWS Security Hub is the native AWS security posture management service designed exactly for this use case. With Organizations integration, a designated admin account automatically receives findings from all member accounts and regions. Security Hub natively integrates with GuardDuty, AWS Config, IAM Access Analyzer, and Amazon Inspector as finding providers, aggregating findings in the ASFF (Amazon Security Finding Format) for consistent querying and filtering. CloudWatch dashboards aggregate metrics, not security findings. OpenSearch requires significant setup and data pipeline development. Custom Lambda requires building and maintaining a custom aggregation pipeline.

q: A company runs production workloads on Amazon EC2 using a mix of On-Demand and Reserved Instances. At the end of the year, the finance team notices that 30% of the Reserved Instance capacity was unused because workloads were migrated to containers on Amazon ECS Fargate. The company wants to optimize costs for the remaining EC2 workloads and avoid wasted RI capacity in the future. Which approach should the SysOps administrator recommend?
options: Convert the unused Standard Reserved Instances to Convertible Reserved Instances if possible, or list them on the EC2 Reserved Instance Marketplace for sale. For future commitments, use Compute Savings Plans instead of Reserved Instances for flexibility across EC2, Fargate, and Lambda.
Migrate all workloads back to EC2 to maximize Reserved Instance utilization before the RI term expires.
Cancel the unused Reserved Instances and switch entirely to On-Demand pricing for flexibility.
Purchase additional Reserved Instances to cover the Fargate workloads.
explain: Listing unused Standard RIs on the Reserved Instance Marketplace allows recovery of some upfront cost. Convertible RIs offer more flexibility but cannot be sold on the Marketplace. For future planning, Compute Savings Plans automatically apply to EC2, Fargate, and Lambda based on actual compute usage — eliminating the risk of RI stranding when workloads shift between services. This provides commitment-based discounts without being tied to specific instance types or services. Purchasing more RIs for Fargate wastes more money. Migrating back to EC2 defeats the purpose of containerization. Reserved Instances cannot be cancelled.

q: A company uses Amazon API Gateway to expose a REST API. The API is only for internal use by the company's mobile app and must not be accessible from the internet. The company wants to ensure no external traffic can reach the API while keeping the implementation simple. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Use an API Gateway usage plan with an API key that the mobile app includes in all requests, ensuring only the mobile app can access the API.

Create a private API in Amazon API Gateway. Create a VPC endpoint for execute-api in the mobile app's VPC. Add a resource policy to the API that denies access from any principal not using the VPC endpoint.
Configure Amazon CloudFront in front of the API Gateway and set geographic restrictions to block all countries except the company's country.
Deploy the API behind an Application Load Balancer in a private subnet and configure the ALB listener to reject requests from outside the VPC.
explain: A private API Gateway endpoint is only accessible from within a VPC through a VPC endpoint for execute-api. The resource policy that denies access from any principal not using the specified VPC endpoint ensures the API cannot be accessed over the internet, even if the endpoint URL is known. This is the purpose-built solution for internal-only APIs. An ALB in a private subnet requires additional load balancer infrastructure. CloudFront geographic restrictions block by country, not by network — the API would still be internet-accessible to users in the allowed country. API keys are authentication tokens that control usage, not network-level access control.

q: A company stores critical application data in Amazon S3 with versioning enabled. The company wants to protect the data from ransomware attacks that could overwrite or delete all object versions. The solution must prevent any user, including AWS administrators, from deleting data within a 30-day period after upload. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Create an S3 bucket policy that denies s3:DeleteObject for all IAM principals for 30 days after object creation.

Enable S3 Object Lock on the bucket in Compliance mode with a default retention period of 30 days. All objects uploaded will be automatically protected for 30 days. Neither users nor AWS can delete the objects during the retention period.
Enable S3 Replication to copy all objects to a second bucket with different access controls as a ransomware-resistant backup.
Enable MFA Delete on the S3 bucket to require multi-factor authentication for permanent object deletion.
explain: S3 Object Lock in Compliance mode with a 30-day default retention period provides immutable object storage that prevents deletion or overwriting by any user, including the root account and AWS. Once set in Compliance mode, the retention settings cannot be changed or deleted before expiration. This directly protects against ransomware that compromises AWS credentials. MFA Delete requires MFA for deletion but can be bypassed if the MFA device is compromised. A bucket policy can be modified by a sufficiently privileged user. S3 Replication creates a copy but if the same compromised credentials have access to both buckets, both can be deleted.

q: A company manages a large fleet of Amazon EC2 instances across multiple AWS accounts using AWS Systems Manager. The SysOps team needs to distribute a new software agent to all instances. The agent package is stored in an S3 bucket in the management account. EC2 instances in member accounts must be able to download the package from S3. Which solution meets these requirements with the LEAST operational overhead?
options: Create an S3 bucket policy on the management account's S3 bucket that allows access from all AWS accounts in the Organization using the aws:PrincipalOrgID condition. Use Systems Manager Distributor to package and deploy the agent to all instances across accounts.
Share the S3 bucket with each member account individually by adding explicit account ARNs to the bucket policy.
Create IAM roles in each member account with S3 read permissions and use Systems Manager Run Command with cross-account access to install the agent.
Copy the software agent package to an S3 bucket in each member account and use Systems Manager Distributor to deploy from local buckets.
explain: Using aws:PrincipalOrgID in the S3 bucket policy automatically grants access to all current and future member accounts in the Organization without listing individual account IDs. Systems Manager Distributor provides a native mechanism for packaging and deploying software to managed instances across all accounts using Systems Manager automation, supporting multi-account and multi-region deployments. This combination requires minimal configuration. Cross-account IAM roles require creating roles in each account. Copying packages to each account's S3 bucket multiplies storage costs and maintenance. Individual account ARNs in the bucket policy require updating when accounts are added.

q: A company runs a web application on Amazon EC2 instances behind an Application Load Balancer. The security team discovers that attackers are sending large volumes of HTTP requests with malformed headers to exhaust server resources. The solution must block these requests before they reach EC2 instances and automatically adapt to new attack patterns. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Update EC2 security group rules to block the source IP addresses of the attackers as they are identified.
Deploy Amazon Shield Advanced on the ALB to protect against network-layer DDoS attacks.

Enable AWS WAF on the ALB with AWS Managed Rules for common HTTP threats and add rate-based rules. Enable WAF Bot Control to detect and block automated clients. Review WAF logs in Amazon CloudWatch to monitor blocked requests.
Enable Amazon GuardDuty and configure automated remediation to update ALB security groups when malicious IPs are detected.
explain: AWS WAF with AWS Managed Rule Groups provides pre-built rules against common web threats including malformed headers, SQL injection, and known bad inputs. These rules are maintained by AWS and automatically updated to address new attack patterns. Rate-based rules limit request volume per IP. WAF Bot Control detects and blocks automated attack tools. All inspection happens at the ALB level before requests reach EC2. Security group IP blocking requires manual updates and cannot match adaptive attackers. GuardDuty detects threats in AWS activity but requires custom Lambda to update security groups, introducing latency. Shield Advanced protects against network-layer DDoS but not application-layer malformed HTTP requests.

q: A company recently migrated its on-premises infrastructure to AWS. After 3 months, the SysOps team uses AWS Cost Explorer and finds that data transfer costs are the second-largest expense. Investigation reveals that EC2 instances are frequently transferring data between different Availability Zones within the same region. Which solution reduces these data transfer costs?
options: Enable VPC endpoint for all AWS services to reduce data transfer costs between EC2 and AWS services.

Redesign the application to ensure services that frequently communicate are deployed in the same Availability Zone. Use Auto Scaling group Availability Zone rebalancing carefully to avoid moving instances to different AZs unnecessarily.
Move all EC2 instances to a single AWS Region where data transfer between AZs is cheaper.
Purchase an AWS support plan that includes data transfer cost optimization recommendations.
explain: Data transfer between Availability Zones within the same region incurs charges per GB in both directions. Co-locating services that communicate heavily in the same AZ eliminates inter-AZ data transfer charges. Using AZ affinity for related microservices (e.g., deploying app and database tiers in the same AZ) is a key cost optimization for high-bandwidth inter-service communication. AWS doesn't offer cheaper cross-AZ transfer in any specific region — rates are the same across regions. VPC endpoints reduce costs for EC2 to AWS service transfers (like S3), not EC2 to EC2 cross-AZ traffic. AWS Support plans don't directly reduce costs.

q: A company's SysOps team wants to automatically receive daily reports of all newly created AWS resources (EC2 instances, S3 buckets, RDS databases, Lambda functions) across all accounts in an AWS Organization. The report should include the resource type, account, region, and creator identity. Which solution meets these requirements with the LEAST operational overhead?
options: Enable AWS CloudTrail in all accounts and deliver logs to a central S3 bucket. Write an AWS Lambda function triggered by EventBridge daily to parse CloudTrail logs and email a resource creation report via SES.

Enable AWS Config in all accounts with an organization-level aggregator. Create an Amazon Athena query that joins the Config resource inventory with CloudTrail logs to identify newly created resources and their creators. Schedule the query using Amazon EventBridge to run daily and deliver results via SNS.
Use AWS Resource Groups Tagging API to list all resources across accounts and identify ones created in the last 24 hours by checking creation time tags.
Enable Amazon GuardDuty in all accounts to detect new resource creation events and generate daily findings reports.
explain: AWS Config continuously tracks resource configuration changes including creation events across all accounts via the organization aggregator. Athena can query Config snapshots in S3 to find new resources. Joining with CloudTrail logs provides the creator identity (userIdentity) for each resource. EventBridge scheduling automates daily report generation. SNS delivers the report without custom email infrastructure. Custom Lambda parsing raw CloudTrail logs requires complex log parsing across many accounts. Resource Groups Tagging API lists tagged resources but requires creation time tags and doesn't include creator identity. GuardDuty monitors for threats, not routine resource inventory reporting.

q: A company has a multi-tier application running in a VPC. The web tier is in public subnets, the application tier in private subnets, and the database tier in isolated subnets. The security team wants to ensure that the database tier instances can only receive connections from the application tier, and any future changes to security groups are automatically detected and alerted. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Deploy AWS Network Firewall between the application and database tiers to inspect and filter all traffic.

Configure the database security group to only allow inbound traffic from the application tier security group ID. Enable AWS Config with the restricted-ssh and vpc-sg-open-only-to-authorized-ports managed rules. Create EventBridge rules for Config compliance change notifications via SNS.
Configure VPC Flow Logs on the isolated subnets and create CloudWatch alarms to detect traffic from unauthorized sources.
Use network ACLs on the isolated subnets to allow traffic only from the application tier subnet CIDR range.
explain: Security group referencing (allowing inbound from the application tier SG ID) provides precise control that automatically adapts if application tier instances are added or replaced. AWS Config continuously monitors security group changes and the managed rules detect overly permissive configurations. EventBridge with Config compliance notifications provides real-time alerting when security groups are modified outside of policy. NACLs use CIDR ranges, not security group IDs, and are harder to maintain as IP ranges change. Network Firewall adds cost and infrastructure for a scenario well-handled by security groups. VPC Flow Logs detect actual traffic but don't prevent unauthorized connections from occurring.

q: A company uses AWS CloudFormation to manage its entire infrastructure. A SysOps administrator needs to update a production stack that includes 50 resources. The administrator wants to validate that the stack update will succeed before applying it to production, without impacting the production environment. Which approach should the administrator use?
options: Use CloudFormation Stack Policies to prevent updates to critical resources and test the update on the remaining resources.
Create a CloudFormation change set in production and review it in the console before executing it in a maintenance window.

Deploy the CloudFormation template to a separate staging environment with production-equivalent configuration to validate the update. Review the CloudFormation change set in the staging environment. Then create and execute a change set in production.
Enable CloudFormation drift detection on the production stack to identify any differences before applying the update.
explain: Deploying to a staging environment with production-equivalent configuration validates that the CloudFormation update succeeds end-to-end without impacting production. The staging deployment catches issues like resource conflicts, IAM permission errors, and service limits. After successful staging validation, creating and executing a change set in production provides a final preview before execution. Drift detection identifies manual changes to existing resources but doesn't validate that a template update will succeed. Stack Policies restrict which resources can be updated but don't validate the update. A change set in production previews changes but executing it still risks failure — staging validation should come first.

q: A company uses Amazon Cognito User Pools for authentication in its web application. The security team wants to add a layer of protection that blocks login attempts from known malicious IP addresses and bot networks, without impacting legitimate users. Which solution meets these requirements with the LEAST operational overhead?
options: Enable Amazon Cognito advanced security features (user pool add-on) with Cognito Threat Protection. Configure adaptive authentication to block high-risk login attempts automatically based on risk scores calculated from IP reputation, device fingerprinting, and user behavior.
Add AWS WAF to the Cognito User Pool endpoint and configure IP reputation managed rule groups to block known malicious IPs.
Enable Amazon GuardDuty to detect compromised Cognito credentials and automatically invalidate suspicious user sessions.
Create a Lambda trigger for the Cognito pre-authentication event that checks the source IP against a threat intelligence list stored in DynamoDB and blocks the login if the IP is flagged.
explain: Amazon Cognito advanced security features include built-in threat protection that calculates risk scores for each authentication attempt based on IP reputation, device fingerprint, and anomalous behavior patterns. High-risk logins can be automatically blocked or challenged with MFA without any custom code. This is a native Cognito capability requiring only configuration. AWS WAF can be applied to Cognito user pool endpoints and also provides IP-based blocking, but Cognito's advanced security provides more context-aware protection. Lambda pre-authentication triggers require maintaining threat intelligence lists and add latency. GuardDuty detects compromised accounts after the fact but doesn't block login attempts proactively.

q: A company runs a containerized application on Amazon ECS with Fargate. Cost analysis shows that Fargate costs are 40% higher than expected because tasks are provisioned with 4 vCPU and 8 GB memory, but monitoring shows average utilization of 0.5 vCPU and 1 GB memory. The company wants to right-size the Fargate tasks without impacting application performance. Which approach should the SysOps administrator take?
options: Enable ECS Service Auto Scaling based on CPU utilization to scale the number of tasks rather than adjusting task size.
Switch from Fargate to EC2 launch type with smaller EC2 instances to reduce infrastructure costs.
Reduce the task CPU and memory to the minimum Fargate configuration (0.25 vCPU, 0.5 GB) immediately to maximize savings.

Use Amazon CloudWatch Container Insights to analyze CPU and memory utilization patterns for the ECS tasks over 4 weeks. Reduce the task CPU and memory configuration to match the 95th percentile utilization with a 20% buffer. Test in a staging environment before applying to production.
explain: Right-sizing Fargate tasks requires analyzing actual utilization patterns over a representative period to understand peak usage. CloudWatch Container Insights provides CPU and memory utilization metrics for ECS tasks. Using the 95th percentile with a 20% buffer ensures the task handles traffic spikes without under-provisioning. Staging environment testing validates the configuration before production. ECS Auto Scaling adjusts the number of tasks but doesn't change per-task sizing — over-provisioned tasks still waste money. Switching to EC2 launch type reduces Fargate benefits and adds instance management. Immediately setting minimum configuration risks under-provisioning without data-driven validation.

q: A company's development team uses feature branches in AWS CodeCommit. The team wants each feature branch to automatically deploy to an isolated test environment when a branch is pushed, and the test environment should be automatically destroyed when the branch is deleted. Which solution automates this workflow?
options: Require developers to manually create CodePipeline pipelines for each feature branch and delete them when merging.
Use a single AWS CodePipeline that deploys all branches to the same test environment sequentially, using branch-specific configuration files.

Create an AWS CodePipeline for each feature branch using an EventBridge rule that detects CodeCommit branch creation events and triggers a Lambda function to create the pipeline and deploy to a new CloudFormation stack named after the branch. Use another EventBridge rule on branch deletion to trigger pipeline and stack deletion.
Configure AWS CodeBuild to deploy feature branches to a shared test environment and use environment variables to isolate configuration per branch.
explain: EventBridge can detect CodeCommit repository events (createBranch, deleteBranch) and trigger automation. A Lambda function for branch creation automatically creates a CodePipeline configured for that branch and deploys to a new CloudFormation stack with a branch-specific name — providing complete environment isolation. A second EventBridge rule on branch deletion triggers Lambda to delete the pipeline and CloudFormation stack. This provides fully automated environment lifecycle management. Manual pipeline management doesn't scale for active development teams. A shared test environment has configuration conflicts between branches. CodeBuild alone doesn't provide full environment isolation.

q: A company wants to track changes to IAM policies across all accounts in an AWS Organization. Whenever an IAM policy is created, modified, or deleted, the security team needs to receive a notification within 2 minutes containing the policy ARN, the action taken, and who made the change. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Write an AWS Lambda function that queries IAM policy versions daily and compares them to a stored baseline, sending SNS alerts for any differences.
Enable AWS Config with the iam-policy-in-use managed rule across all accounts and configure SNS notifications for Config compliance changes.
Use AWS Security Hub to detect IAM policy changes and generate findings that trigger SNS notifications.

Enable AWS CloudTrail with an organization trail. Create an Amazon EventBridge rule with the pattern source=aws.iam and detail-type=AWS API Call via CloudTrail filtering on events CreatePolicy, PutRolePolicy, DeletePolicy, etc. Configure the EventBridge rule to trigger an SNS topic with the event details formatted in the notification.
explain: AWS CloudTrail captures all IAM API calls within seconds. An organization trail captures events across all accounts. EventBridge receives CloudTrail events in near real time (typically within 2 minutes). Filtering on specific IAM API events (CreatePolicy, DeletePolicy, PutRolePolicy, AttachRolePolicy, etc.) and routing to an SNS topic provides the required notification with full event context including the policy ARN (from the resources field), action (eventName), and actor (userIdentity). AWS Config detects configuration changes with slightly longer delays and tracks compliance, not real-time IAM change notifications. Daily Lambda polling misses the 2-minute requirement. Security Hub aggregates findings but doesn't provide sub-2-minute IAM change notifications.

q: A company has a critical order processing system that uses an Amazon SQS queue to receive orders and AWS Lambda to process them. During peak shopping events, Lambda is throttled due to concurrency limits, and messages stay in the queue for hours before being processed. The company wants to ensure orders are processed within 5 minutes even during peak events. Which solution meets these requirements?
