options: Add an Amazon Kinesis Data Stream between the order system and Lambda to buffer requests at higher throughput than SQS.

Request a Lambda concurrency limit increase from AWS Support for the order processing function. Set Reserved Concurrency on the Lambda function to guarantee a minimum concurrency pool. Configure an SQS maximum message retention period and adjust the visibility timeout to match the Lambda processing time.
Increase the SQS queue visibility timeout to prevent messages from being reprocessed while Lambda processes them during peak load.
Deploy the order processing logic on Amazon EC2 instances with Auto Scaling to handle peak load without Lambda concurrency constraints.
explain: Lambda throttling during peak events is a concurrency limit problem. Requesting a concurrency limit increase from AWS Support addresses the root cause. Setting Reserved Concurrency ensures the order processing function always has dedicated concurrency even when other Lambda functions are running. Adjusting the visibility timeout ensures messages remain invisible while being processed (preventing duplicate processing) and returning to the queue if Lambda fails. Increasing only the visibility timeout doesn't solve the concurrency throttling. Kinesis is a data streaming service that doesn't improve Lambda concurrency limits. EC2 with Auto Scaling is a valid alternative but requires significant application refactoring.

q: A company's security audit finds that several Amazon RDS databases in a production account have the 'publicly accessible' setting enabled. The company wants to immediately remediate all publicly accessible RDS databases and ensure no RDS databases are ever made publicly accessible in the future. Which combination of actions meets these requirements?
options: Manually log in to each AWS account, identify publicly accessible RDS instances in the console, and modify them one by one.

Use the AWS CLI to modify all publicly accessible RDS instances to set PubliclyAccessible to false. Create an AWS Config rule using the rds-instance-public-access-check managed rule. Configure automatic remediation using an AWS Systems Manager Automation document to disable public access on any non-compliant instance.
Create an SCP that denies the rds:CreateDBInstance and rds:ModifyDBInstance actions when the PubliclyAccessible parameter is true.
Enable AWS Macie to scan RDS databases for sensitive data and automatically disable public access for databases containing PII.
explain: The AWS CLI command to modify all RDS instances immediately resolves the existing violations. The AWS Config managed rule rds-instance-public-access-check provides continuous compliance monitoring for both existing and future RDS instances. Automatic SSM Automation remediation ensures that any future publicly accessible RDS instance is automatically corrected without manual intervention. This covers both remediation and prevention. Manual remediation doesn't scale. An SCP denying RDS actions with PubliclyAccessible=true prevents new public instances but doesn't remediate existing ones. Macie focuses on data classification, not database network configuration.

q: A company stores its AWS Lambda function code in Amazon S3. The security team requires that all Lambda function deployments include a checksum verification to ensure code has not been tampered with between upload to S3 and deployment. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Enable S3 server-side encryption with KMS to protect deployment packages from unauthorized access.
Enable S3 versioning on the deployment bucket to maintain a history of all Lambda packages uploaded.

Calculate the SHA256 hash of the Lambda deployment package before uploading to S3. Store the hash in AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store. In the deployment pipeline, download the package, recalculate the hash, compare with the stored value, and proceed with deployment only if they match.

Use Lambda code signing with AWS Signer. Configure the Lambda function to only accept deployment packages signed with an approved signing profile. Upload packages through the AWS Signer signing process.
explain: AWS Lambda code signing uses AWS Signer to cryptographically sign deployment packages. When code signing is configured on a Lambda function, Lambda validates the signature before deployment and rejects packages that are not signed by an approved signing profile. This provides tamper-detection that is managed by AWS without custom hash comparison code. If someone modifies the package after signing, the signature is invalid and deployment fails. Manual SHA256 comparison requires custom code in the pipeline and can be bypassed. S3 versioning preserves history but doesn't detect tampering. KMS encryption protects data at rest but doesn't verify integrity.

q: A company runs an Amazon Redshift cluster with 10 ra3.4xlarge nodes for a business intelligence application. The cluster is used by analysts during business hours (8 AM to 6 PM) and is idle overnight and on weekends. The company wants to minimize Redshift costs without losing data or requiring significant operational effort. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Pause the Redshift cluster every evening using an AWS Lambda function triggered by Amazon EventBridge and resume it each morning. Data is preserved when the cluster is paused.

Use Amazon Redshift Serverless instead of a provisioned cluster. Redshift Serverless automatically scales to zero when not in use and charges only for compute when queries are running, eliminating idle cluster costs.
Reduce the cluster to 2 nodes during off-hours and scale back to 10 nodes during business hours using Redshift Elastic Resize.
Purchase 1-year Reserved Instance pricing for the Redshift nodes to receive a discount on the 24/7 cluster costs.
explain: Amazon Redshift provisioned clusters support pause and resume functionality. When paused, compute billing stops while storage (data) is retained. An automated Lambda function on an EventBridge schedule pauses the cluster at 6 PM and resumes it at 8 AM, with no manual intervention. This eliminates approximately 65% of compute hours (16 hours overnight + weekends). Redshift Serverless scales to zero but requires migrating from provisioned clusters, which may affect workload-specific optimizations. Reserved Instances reduce hourly cost but charges continue during idle hours. Elastic Resize changes the node count but doesn't stop billing when idle.

q: A company needs to monitor the health and performance of its AWS infrastructure and receive predictive alerts before issues occur, rather than reacting to problems after users are affected. The company uses EC2, RDS, and Lambda across multiple accounts. Which AWS service provides ML-powered anomaly detection and predictive alerting?
options: Use AWS Trusted Advisor to receive weekly recommendations for performance and reliability improvements across the infrastructure.
Create static CloudWatch alarms with fixed thresholds on all key metrics and set conservative thresholds to catch issues early.
Enable AWS Compute Optimizer to generate ML-based performance recommendations for EC2 and Lambda configurations.

Use Amazon CloudWatch Anomaly Detection on key metrics (EC2 CPU, RDS connections, Lambda error rate). Create CloudWatch alarms with anomaly detection bands that alert when metrics deviate significantly from their expected pattern, enabling early warning before thresholds are breached.
explain: CloudWatch Anomaly Detection uses ML to learn the normal baseline and seasonal patterns for each metric, then creates dynamic bands of expected values. Alarms based on anomaly detection bands trigger when metrics deviate significantly from expected patterns — before breaching fixed thresholds. This provides earlier warning and adapts to changing patterns without manual threshold tuning. Static threshold alarms require manual tuning and generate false positives for normal traffic variations. Trusted Advisor provides weekly best practice recommendations, not real-time predictive alerting. Compute Optimizer provides configuration recommendations, not real-time anomaly detection.

q: A company runs a web application using Amazon EC2 instances in an Auto Scaling group behind an Application Load Balancer. The application serves user-uploaded images stored on the EC2 instance's local disk. When Auto Scaling terminates an instance during scale-in, the images on that instance are permanently lost. Users report missing images after scale-in events. Which solution resolves this issue?
options: Modify the application to store user-uploaded images in Amazon S3 instead of local EC2 instance storage. Update the application to retrieve images from S3 using the S3 SDK or pre-signed URLs.
Increase the Auto Scaling scale-in protection threshold to prevent instances from being terminated when they contain user images.
Configure Auto Scaling lifecycle hooks to copy images from terminating instances to a shared EBS volume before the instance is terminated.
Attach an Amazon EBS volume to each EC2 instance and store images on the EBS volume, which persists independently of the instance.
explain: Amazon S3 is the correct storage layer for user-uploaded files in a stateless, auto-scaled architecture. S3 provides durable, scalable object storage that is accessible from all instances and persists independently of EC2 lifecycle. Migrating from local disk to S3 storage eliminates the data loss problem at the architectural level. Auto Scaling lifecycle hooks can delay termination but copying images is complex and unreliable. Scale-in protection prevents termination but defeats the purpose of auto scaling. EBS volumes are AZ-specific and cannot be shared across instances in multiple AZs.

q: A company uses AWS Lambda functions that are triggered by API Gateway for a REST API. The Lambda functions are deployed in a VPC to access an RDS database. The team notices Lambda cold start times have increased to 8-10 seconds since adding VPC connectivity, causing poor API response times for the first request after periods of inactivity. The company wants to eliminate cold start latency without incurring unnecessary costs for rarely used functions. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Enable Lambda SnapStart for the functions (for Java runtimes) or configure Provisioned Concurrency with Application Auto Scaling scheduled scaling to pre-warm instances during expected active hours and scale down during inactive periods.
Remove the Lambda function from the VPC and use an RDS Proxy endpoint with public accessibility for database connectivity.
Increase the Lambda function memory allocation to maximum to reduce cold start initialization time.
Deploy a constantly running EC2 instance with the same application logic to handle requests during Lambda cold starts.
explain: Lambda SnapStart (for Java) or Provisioned Concurrency eliminates cold starts by keeping initialized Lambda environments ready to handle requests. Using Application Auto Scaling with scheduled scaling policies pre-warms instances during known active hours (e.g., business hours) and scales back to zero during inactive periods, balancing performance and cost. VPC cold starts are longer because Lambda must attach ENIs, making Provisioned Concurrency especially valuable for VPC-attached functions. Increasing memory reduces execution duration but doesn't eliminate the cold start initialization phase. Removing VPC access breaks the private RDS connection. EC2 adds infrastructure management and costs more.

q: A company uses Amazon CloudFront to serve a web application. The security team wants to ensure all HTTP requests are automatically redirected to HTTPS and that the application uses TLS 1.2 or higher. The solution must be implemented without modifying the application code. Which CloudFront configuration meets these requirements?
options: Enable Amazon Certificate Manager to issue a certificate for the domain and configure HTTP to HTTPS redirect in the ACM console.

Set the CloudFront distribution viewer protocol policy to Redirect HTTP to HTTPS. Configure the CloudFront security policy to TLSv1.2_2021 or higher to enforce TLS 1.2 minimum for all viewer connections.
Create an AWS WAF rule that blocks all HTTP requests and allow only HTTPS connections to the CloudFront distribution.
Configure the EC2 origin servers to redirect HTTP to HTTPS at the application level and install TLS certificates on each instance.
explain: CloudFront viewer protocol policy set to 'Redirect HTTP to HTTPS' automatically redirects all HTTP requests to the equivalent HTTPS URL at the CDN level without any application code changes. The CloudFront security policy (e.g., TLSv1.2_2021) enforces the minimum TLS version for all connections between viewers and CloudFront. Both settings are configured in the CloudFront distribution settings with no code changes required. Configuring the origin servers adds complexity and only affects requests that reach the origin. WAF cannot distinguish between HTTP and HTTPS at the application layer for redirect purposes. ACM manages certificates but doesn't handle protocol redirects.

q: A company has 500 Amazon EC2 instances that need to be updated with a new configuration file. The SysOps administrator needs to distribute the file to all instances simultaneously and verify that the update was applied successfully on each instance. The instances are managed by AWS Systems Manager. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Log in to each of the 500 instances via SSH and manually copy the configuration file.
Create an AMI with the new configuration file and use rolling replacement in the Auto Scaling group to update all instances.
Use AWS Systems Manager State Manager to create an association that applies the configuration file to all instances on a schedule.

Use AWS Systems Manager Run Command with the AWS-RunShellScript document to distribute the configuration file from S3 to all 500 instances simultaneously. Use the Run Command output and CloudWatch Logs integration to verify success on each instance.
explain: AWS Systems Manager Run Command can target all 500 instances simultaneously using instance tags or instance IDs without requiring SSH access. The AWS-RunShellScript document can download the file from S3 and place it at the correct path. Run Command provides real-time output per instance and integrates with CloudWatch Logs for persistent verification records. SSH to 500 instances is impractical. AMI replacement requires instance termination and restart, causing downtime. State Manager associations are for ongoing compliance enforcement (recurring), not immediate one-time distribution with immediate verification.

q: A company runs a microservices application on Amazon ECS. Services need to discover each other dynamically using DNS-based service discovery. When a new ECS task starts, it must be automatically registered in DNS and removed when it stops. The solution must work without a load balancer for direct service-to-service communication. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Enable Amazon ECS Service Discovery using AWS Cloud Map. Each ECS service registers its tasks as instances in a Cloud Map service, which automatically creates and removes DNS records as tasks start and stop. Services query the Cloud Map service using the DNS name.
Create an Amazon Route 53 private hosted zone and write Lambda functions triggered by ECS task state change events to create and delete DNS records.
Use Amazon ECS service links to establish fixed DNS names for each ECS service and configure inter-service communication using the service link DNS names.
Deploy a Consul service mesh on EC2 instances to handle service registration and discovery for all ECS tasks.
explain: AWS Cloud Map integrates natively with Amazon ECS Service Discovery. When configured, ECS automatically registers task IP addresses and ports with Cloud Map as instances when tasks start, and deregisters them when tasks stop. Cloud Map creates Route 53 private hosted zone records for each registered instance, enabling DNS-based service discovery. Services use the Cloud Map DNS name (e.g., service.namespace.local) for direct communication. Custom Lambda for DNS management requires complex ECS event handling. Consul is a third-party tool requiring separate infrastructure. ECS service links are not a real AWS feature.

q: A company uses Amazon Route 53 for DNS with weighted routing across two identical application stacks in us-east-1 and eu-west-1, each with a 50/50 weight. During a deployment, the company wants to shift 100% of traffic to us-east-1 while updating eu-west-1, then shift back to 50/50 after the update is complete. The switch must happen within seconds without DNS propagation delay. Which approach minimizes the switchover time?
options: Change the weight of the eu-west-1 Route 53 record to 0. With a weight of 0, Route 53 stops routing traffic to that record immediately. After the eu-west-1 update completes, restore the weight to 50.
Delete the eu-west-1 Route 53 record during the update and recreate it after the update completes.
Reduce the TTL on the Route 53 records to 0 seconds before the deployment and update the weights to 100/0 during the update.
Use Amazon CloudFront with origin failover to temporarily route all traffic to the us-east-1 origin during the eu-west-1 update.
explain: Setting the Route 53 weighted record weight to 0 is the correct mechanism for removing a record from routing without deleting it. Route 53 immediately stops routing traffic to records with weight 0, and restoring the weight instantly re-enables routing. The change takes effect within the TTL of cached responses, but new DNS queries immediately use the updated weights. TTL of 0 is not a valid Route 53 setting (minimum is 0 but many resolvers ignore it). Deleting and recreating records is operationally risky and introduces the DNS propagation delay for the new record. CloudFront origin failover is for resilience, not planned traffic shifting.

q: A company wants to implement proactive notifications when AWS is planning maintenance on EC2 instances, RDS databases, or other resources that will require restarts or brief unavailability. The operations team must be notified at least 24 hours before any scheduled maintenance. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Subscribe to AWS Health events using Amazon EventBridge. Create a rule matching AWS Health scheduled maintenance events for EC2, RDS, and other services. Route the events to an SNS topic that notifies the operations team email distribution list.
Create CloudWatch alarms that monitor EC2 instance health checks and notify the team when instances fail health checks during maintenance.
Enable AWS Trusted Advisor and subscribe to weekly email reports that include upcoming maintenance notifications.
Check the AWS Health dashboard in the AWS console manually every morning to look for upcoming maintenance notifications.
explain: AWS Health sends scheduled maintenance notifications through Amazon EventBridge with advance notice of 24-72 hours for most maintenance events. An EventBridge rule that matches AWS Health events (source: aws.health, detail-type: AWS Health Event) filtered for UPCOMING or SCHEDULED event type codes automatically routes notifications to SNS, which delivers email notifications to the operations team. This provides automated, real-time notification without manual monitoring. Manual dashboard checks introduce human error and timing gaps. Trusted Advisor provides weekly best practice reports, not maintenance event notifications. CloudWatch health check alarms fire during maintenance, not before it.

q: A company has 200 Amazon S3 buckets created over several years. Many buckets contain data that has not been accessed in over 2 years. The company wants to identify which S3 buckets and objects are unused and reduce storage costs. Which solution provides the data needed to make cost-optimization decisions with the LEAST operational overhead?
options: Manually check the Amazon S3 console for each of the 200 buckets to review last access time and storage usage.
Enable S3 server access logging on all 200 buckets and write a custom script to parse the logs and identify unused buckets.
Use Amazon Athena to query S3 access logs stored in a central bucket and calculate access frequency for each bucket.

Enable Amazon S3 Storage Lens with advanced metrics including activity metrics. S3 Storage Lens provides aggregate visibility into storage usage and access patterns across all 200 buckets, identifying buckets with no recent access and objects that are candidates for tiering or deletion.
explain: Amazon S3 Storage Lens provides organization-wide visibility across all S3 buckets with a single configuration, showing storage size, object count, and activity metrics (request counts, bytes downloaded) per bucket. Advanced metrics include object access patterns that identify buckets with zero activity over configurable periods. This is a native S3 analytics feature requiring a single Storage Lens configuration, not per-bucket setup. Server access logging requires enabling on each bucket and custom log parsing. Manual console review doesn't scale to 200 buckets. Athena log analysis also requires logging to be enabled per bucket and custom queries.

q: A company's DevOps team needs to grant temporary AWS console access to a contractor for 8 hours to troubleshoot an issue. The contractor does not have an AWS account. The company uses AWS IAM Identity Center with an on-premises Active Directory identity provider. Which solution provides the contractor temporary access with the LEAST permanent infrastructure changes?
options: Generate temporary AWS STS credentials using the AssumeRole API and share them with the contractor for console access.

Create a temporary user in the on-premises Active Directory with membership in the appropriate AD group mapped to an IAM Identity Center permission set. Set the AD account expiry to 8 hours. The contractor logs in through the IAM Identity Center portal using the temporary AD credentials.
Create a temporary IAM user in the AWS account with console access and attach the minimum required IAM policies. Delete the user after 8 hours.
Create a new IAM Identity Center user directly in Identity Center and assign a permission set. Remove the user after 8 hours.
explain: Creating a temporary Active Directory user that maps to an existing IAM Identity Center permission set leverages the existing federation infrastructure without any permanent AWS changes. The AD account expiry automatically terminates access after 8 hours without requiring manual cleanup. The contractor uses the existing IAM Identity Center portal for access. Temporary IAM users require creating AWS credentials that must be manually tracked and deleted. STS temporary credentials provide CLI/API access but not console access without federation. Creating an IAM Identity Center user is valid but requires changes in both IAM Identity Center and group assignments, with manual cleanup.

q: A company is implementing Infrastructure as Code using AWS CloudFormation. The team wants to reuse common CloudFormation components (such as VPC configuration, security groups, and IAM roles) across multiple stacks without duplicating the code. Changes to a shared component should automatically update all stacks that use it. Which CloudFormation feature meets these requirements?
options: Use CloudFormation Nested Stacks or CloudFormation StackSets. Define shared components in parent or nested stack templates. Reference child stack outputs using the Fn::GetAtt function to share values between stacks.
Store shared CloudFormation snippets in Amazon S3 and use custom Lambda functions to merge them into each stack template at deployment time.
Copy and paste the shared CloudFormation YAML code into each stack template that needs it, updating all copies when changes are made.
Use AWS Service Catalog to publish shared infrastructure products that teams can provision independently without dependency updates.
explain: CloudFormation Nested Stacks allow defining common infrastructure components in separate templates stored in S3. Parent stacks reference nested stacks using AWS::CloudFormation::Stack resources with the TemplateURL pointing to the shared template. When the shared template is updated, updating the parent stack automatically propagates changes to all consumers of the nested stack. This provides true code reuse with centralized management. Copy-paste duplication violates DRY and requires manually updating all copies. Custom Lambda-based merging adds complexity without CloudFormation's built-in dependency tracking. Service Catalog allows independent provisioning without propagating updates to existing deployments.

q: A company needs to allow its on-premises data center to access Amazon S3 without traffic traversing the internet. The company has an AWS Direct Connect connection to AWS. Which solution enables private S3 access over Direct Connect?
options: Establish a Site-to-Site VPN over the Direct Connect connection and use a VPC endpoint for S3 to route S3 traffic privately.

Create a public virtual interface (VIF) on the Direct Connect connection. Configure the on-premises firewall to only allow traffic to Amazon S3 IP ranges. S3 is accessible over the public VIF using public IP addresses without traversing the internet.

Create a private virtual interface (VIF) on the Direct Connect connection and a gateway VPC endpoint for S3 in the VPC. Route S3 traffic from on-premises through the private VIF to the VPC, then to S3 via the gateway endpoint.
Deploy an S3 Transfer Acceleration endpoint that routes traffic from on-premises through Direct Connect to the nearest edge location.
explain: A public VIF on Direct Connect provides private, dedicated network access to AWS public services including Amazon S3 using their public IP addresses, without the traffic transiting the internet. AWS advertises S3 public IP prefixes over the public VIF via BGP, so on-premises traffic to S3 uses the Direct Connect physical connection rather than the internet. A private VIF connects to VPCs; gateway VPC endpoints for S3 work within VPCs and require traffic to enter the VPC first. VPN over Direct Connect adds encryption overhead but is used for private service access. S3 Transfer Acceleration is for accelerating internet transfers, not Direct Connect private access.

q: A company runs a critical payment processing application on AWS. The application must be able to detect and automatically recover from EC2 instance failures within 2 minutes without data loss. The application is stateless and stores all state in Amazon DynamoDB. Which solution meets the recovery time requirement?
options: Enable Amazon EC2 Auto Recovery using an AWS Systems Manager automation document triggered by a CloudWatch alarm on StatusCheckFailed_System.
Deploy the application on two EC2 instances behind an ALB. When one instance fails, the ALB automatically routes traffic to the healthy instance.

Configure Amazon CloudWatch to monitor the EC2 instance status check. Create a CloudWatch alarm that triggers an EC2 instance recovery action when system or instance status checks fail for 2 consecutive minutes. The recovery action restores the instance on healthy hardware preserving the same instance ID, IP addresses, and attached EBS volumes.
Configure an Auto Scaling group with a minimum of 1 instance. When the instance fails a health check, Auto Scaling terminates and launches a replacement instance.
explain: CloudWatch EC2 instance recovery is specifically designed for recovering failed instances within minutes. When the StatusCheckFailed_System alarm triggers after 2 minutes of failed checks, the recovery action automatically migrates the instance to healthy underlying hardware while preserving the instance ID, private IP address, Elastic IP, and attached EBS volumes. Recovery typically completes within 2-3 minutes. Auto Scaling terminates and launches a new instance, which takes longer than recovery and changes the instance ID. Two instances with ALB provides redundancy but doesn't auto-recover — traffic is rerouted, not the instance recovered. Systems Manager automation adds complexity when native CloudWatch recovery is available.

q: A company uses AWS CloudFormation to manage infrastructure. The SysOps team accidentally deployed a CloudFormation stack with an IAM policy that grants overly broad permissions (s3:* on all resources). The company needs to immediately restrict these permissions across all affected resources. Which approach should the administrator take FIRST?
options: Identify all IAM roles and users that have the overly broad policy attached. Create a new restrictive IAM policy with minimum required permissions. Update the CloudFormation template with the corrected policy and run a stack update to replace the overly broad policy. Verify that no existing permissions exist outside of CloudFormation management.
Delete the CloudFormation stack immediately to remove all deployed resources including the IAM policy.
Contact AWS Support to revoke the IAM policy from all affected principals on the company's behalf.
Enable AWS Config with the iam-user-no-policies-check rule to detect the overly broad policy and automatically remediate it.
explain: The correct remediation is to first identify the blast radius (all principals using the policy), then create a properly scoped replacement policy, and update the CloudFormation stack to deploy the corrected policy. CloudFormation stack updates replace the IAM policy in place. Identifying all attached principals ensures no shadow access remains. Deleting the stack removes all resources (not just the IAM policy), causing unnecessary disruption to production infrastructure. AWS Support cannot modify customer IAM policies. AWS Config detects violations but iam-user-no-policies-check targets inline policies on users, not managed policy permissions — and remediation doesn't auto-correct policy permissions.

q: A company's application team reports that their AWS Lambda functions are experiencing higher-than-expected costs. The SysOps administrator reviews the Lambda metrics and sees that the duration of invocations varies significantly — sometimes completing in 100ms and sometimes taking 15 seconds. The administrator suspects the variance is caused by downstream API calls. Which tool provides the MOST detailed insight into which downstream call causes the latency?
options: Review the Lambda function source code to identify all external API calls and add timing logs around each call to measure individual durations.
Create CloudWatch metric filters on Lambda CloudWatch Logs to identify invocations with duration over 10 seconds and manually analyze the log output.

Enable AWS X-Ray active tracing on the Lambda functions. X-Ray captures subsegment timing for each external API call, AWS SDK call, and database query within each Lambda invocation, showing exactly which downstream call causes the 15-second latency.
Use Amazon CloudWatch Contributor Insights to identify which Lambda invocations have the longest duration and correlate them with request parameters.
explain: AWS X-Ray active tracing automatically instruments Lambda functions and captures timing data for every downstream call including HTTP requests (external APIs), AWS SDK calls (DynamoDB, S3, etc.), and database queries. X-Ray subsegments show the exact duration of each downstream call within a trace, allowing the administrator to identify which specific API call takes 15 seconds. The X-Ray trace timeline visualization makes the bottleneck immediately visible. CloudWatch metric filters find slow invocations but require manual log analysis. CloudWatch Contributor Insights analyzes log patterns, not distributed trace timing. Adding timing logs requires code changes and manual analysis.

q: A company uses Amazon DynamoDB for a high-traffic e-commerce application. Cost analysis shows that DynamoDB costs have increased 300% over the past quarter, driven by read capacity. Further investigation shows that 80% of reads are for the same 100 product items that are frequently viewed but rarely updated. The company wants to reduce DynamoDB read costs significantly. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Enable DynamoDB Auto Scaling to reduce the read capacity units during periods of lower traffic.
Enable DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) in front of the DynamoDB table to cache read results at the DynamoDB API layer.

Implement Amazon ElastiCache for Redis as a caching layer in front of DynamoDB. Cache the 100 frequently read product items with an appropriate TTL. Application reads hit the cache first and only go to DynamoDB on cache misses.
Switch the DynamoDB table from provisioned capacity to on-demand mode to pay only for actual reads.
explain: Since 80% of reads target the same 100 items, an application-level caching layer with ElastiCache for Redis dramatically reduces DynamoDB read requests. Cached items are served at microsecond latency from ElastiCache, with DynamoDB reads only occurring on cache misses or cache expiration. This can reduce DynamoDB read capacity consumption by 80%+. DAX (option 4) also caches DynamoDB reads and is also a valid answer, but ElastiCache provides more flexibility for caching logic, supports more data structures, and can cache across multiple DynamoDB tables. Auto Scaling reduces capacity when load is lower but doesn't reduce the number of DynamoDB reads. On-demand mode eliminates capacity management but doesn't reduce per-read costs.

q: A company runs its application on a fleet of 1,000 Amazon EC2 instances using a custom AMI. After a security vulnerability is discovered, the security team needs all instances to be replaced with a new AMI that includes the security patch within 4 hours. The application is stateless and data is stored in Amazon RDS. Which solution meets the time requirement with the LEAST manual effort?
options: Create a new AMI with the security patch. Update the Auto Scaling group launch template to reference the new AMI. Use an EC2 Instance Refresh on the Auto Scaling group with a percentage-based replacement rate, configuring it to replace all instances within 4 hours.
Create a new AMI and manually update each of the 1,000 instances to use the new AMI using rolling replacement.
Terminate all 1,000 instances simultaneously and wait for Auto Scaling to launch new instances with the updated launch template.
Use AWS Systems Manager Automation to run a runbook that creates a new AMI, updates the launch template, and replaces instances one-by-one using Run Command.
explain: EC2 Auto Scaling Instance Refresh automates the process of replacing all instances in an Auto Scaling group with a new launch template version. The administrator configures the minimum healthy percentage and instance warmup time to balance speed and availability. With 1,000 instances and a 4-hour window, configuring a replacement rate that terminates and launches many instances simultaneously achieves the deadline. The process is automated once initiated. Terminating all 1,000 instances simultaneously causes complete outage. Systems Manager runbook automation is more complex than the built-in Instance Refresh feature. Manual replacement of 1,000 instances is not feasible within 4 hours.

q: A company uses multiple AWS accounts and stores sensitive data in Amazon S3 across all accounts. The data classification team needs to identify which S3 buckets contain sensitive data (PII, financial data, credentials) across all accounts automatically. New buckets and objects should be scanned continuously. Which AWS service meets these requirements?
options: Enable Amazon Inspector on all EC2 instances that have access to the S3 buckets to scan for sensitive data patterns.
Use Amazon GuardDuty to detect when sensitive data in S3 is accessed by unusual principals and generate findings for the security team.

Enable Amazon Macie across all accounts using AWS Organizations integration. Macie uses ML to automatically discover and classify sensitive data including PII, financial information, and credentials in S3 buckets. Configure Macie to send findings to AWS Security Hub for centralized visibility.
Write custom AWS Lambda functions triggered by S3 object creation events to scan new objects for sensitive data patterns using regular expressions.
explain: Amazon Macie is purpose-built for sensitive data discovery and classification in Amazon S3. It uses ML models and pattern matching to identify PII (names, addresses, SSNs), financial data (credit card numbers), credentials, and other sensitive data types. With AWS Organizations integration, Macie can be enabled across all accounts from a central administrator account and continuously monitors new and existing buckets. Findings include the specific sensitive data type, object location, and severity. Amazon Inspector performs software vulnerability scanning on EC2, not S3 data classification. GuardDuty detects suspicious access patterns but doesn't classify data content. Custom Lambda pattern matching requires maintaining regex patterns and processing infrastructure.

q: A company runs a multi-tier application with web servers in public subnets and application servers in private subnets. The security team notices that some application servers are making outbound connections to unexpected external IP addresses. The team wants to implement DNS-based filtering to block application servers from accessing unauthorized external domains. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Configure the security groups on application servers to deny all outbound traffic except to explicitly allowed service endpoints.

Create an Amazon Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall rule group with deny rules for unauthorized domains or domain lists. Associate the DNS Firewall rule group with the VPC to filter DNS queries from application servers in private subnets.
Update the network ACLs on the private subnets to block outbound traffic to the IP addresses of unauthorized domains.
Deploy AWS Network Firewall in the VPC with stateful rules that match and block traffic to unauthorized external IP addresses.
explain: Amazon Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall allows or blocks DNS queries based on domain names. Creating deny rules for unauthorized domains prevents application servers from resolving those domains, blocking access at the DNS level before any network connection is established. Rule groups with domain lists can block entire categories of domains. The DNS Firewall is associated at the VPC level, affecting all resources in the VPC. Network ACLs use IP addresses, not domain names, and require maintaining static IP lists that change frequently. Network Firewall provides domain-based stateful inspection but at higher cost. Security group outbound rules use IP/port, not domain names.

q: A company's SysOps team manages an Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL cluster that serves a high-traffic OLTP application. The team observes that during peak business hours, the primary instance's CPU utilization reaches 95%, causing query latency to increase. Analysis shows that 70% of database queries are read operations. The team wants to scale read capacity without modifying the application connection strings. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Add Aurora read replicas to the cluster and configure an Aurora Reader endpoint. Update the application to use the Aurora Reader endpoint for read queries and the Cluster endpoint for write queries. Aurora automatically load balances connections across all read replicas.
Create an Amazon RDS Proxy in front of the Aurora cluster to pool connections and reduce the load on the primary instance.
Enable Aurora Serverless v2 for the read replicas to automatically scale read capacity based on demand.
Increase the primary Aurora instance to a larger instance class to handle both read and write operations.
explain: Aurora provides a built-in Reader endpoint that automatically distributes read connections across all available Aurora read replicas. Adding replicas increases read capacity linearly. Updating the application to use the Reader endpoint for reads (and the Cluster endpoint for writes) is a targeted change that doesn't require changes to application logic. The Aurora Reader endpoint load-balances connections automatically. Increasing the primary instance class scales both reads and writes but is more expensive and doesn't scale horizontally. Aurora Serverless v2 for replicas provides dynamic scaling but requires changing replica configuration. RDS Proxy pools connections but doesn't distribute read load to replicas.

q: A company runs a containerized application on Amazon ECS. After a recent deployment, the application is producing errors that don't appear in normal application logs. The SysOps team suspects the issue is related to inter-container communication within a task. Which tool helps the team debug network communication between containers in the same ECS task?
options: Deploy AWS X-Ray in the containers and review the service map for failed connections between containers.
Use Amazon Inspector to scan the ECS containers for network configuration issues and generate findings.

Enable ECS Exec on the ECS service to get interactive shell access into individual containers in a running task. Use curl, netstat, or custom diagnostic commands to test connectivity and inspect network state within the container.
Enable VPC Flow Logs on the subnets where ECS tasks are running to capture all container-level network traffic.
explain: ECS Exec uses AWS Systems Manager Session Manager to provide interactive shell access to containers running in an ECS task without requiring SSH or exposing container ports. The SysOps team can run diagnostic commands (curl to test connectivity, netstat to inspect connections, env to check environment variables) directly inside the container to diagnose the issue in real time. VPC Flow Logs capture inter-VPC traffic but containers in the same task share a network namespace, making Flow Logs less useful for intra-task debugging. Inspector performs security vulnerability scanning. X-Ray traces distributed requests but requires SDK instrumentation to detect inter-container issues.

q: A company runs development, staging, and production workloads in separate AWS accounts. The development account has over 200 EC2 instances created by developers for testing, but many are no longer needed. The SysOps team wants to automatically identify and terminate instances that have been running for more than 7 days in the development account to reduce costs. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Require developers to use CloudFormation with a DeletionPolicy of Delete and set a stack expiration period of 7 days.
Create a CloudWatch Events rule that triggers daily to list all EC2 instances and terminate ones with a LaunchTime older than 7 days using a Lambda function.

Create an AWS Config custom rule that detects EC2 instances running for more than 7 days using the LaunchTime attribute. Configure automatic remediation using an SSM Automation document to terminate non-compliant instances after sending an SNS notification.
Use AWS Cost Explorer to identify instances that have been running for more than 7 days and manually terminate them monthly.
explain: An EventBridge (formerly CloudWatch Events) rule with a daily schedule triggers a Lambda function that calls the EC2 DescribeInstances API, filters instances where LaunchTime is older than 7 days, and terminates them. This is a lightweight, cost-effective solution that runs daily and requires no additional AWS services beyond Lambda and EventBridge. AWS Config custom rules are designed for compliance checking against configuration states, not time-based lifecycle actions. Manual Cost Explorer review doesn't provide automation. CloudFormation stack expiration is not a native AWS feature — stacks don't automatically delete after a time period.

q: A company is setting up a new AWS account for a regulated industry workload. The security baseline requires that AWS CloudTrail is enabled in all regions, S3 bucket versioning is enabled for log buckets, and the root account has MFA enabled. The company wants to verify these controls are in place and receive alerts when they become non-compliant. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Enable AWS Config with the cloudtrail-enabled, s3-bucket-versioning-enabled, and root-account-mfa-enabled managed rules. Configure SNS notifications for Config compliance changes.
Deploy AWS Trusted Advisor Business Support and subscribe to email notifications for security-category findings.

Enable AWS Security Hub and activate the CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark standard. Security Hub automatically checks for CloudTrail enabled in all regions, S3 bucket versioning on log buckets, and root MFA status. Configure EventBridge to route Security Hub findings to SNS for alerting.
Manually check each control in the AWS console weekly and send a compliance report to the security team.
explain: The CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark in AWS Security Hub includes checks for all three requirements: CloudTrail enabled in all regions (CIS 2.1), S3 bucket versioning (CIS 2.6 for log bucket access logging, related controls), and root MFA enabled (CIS 1.5). Security Hub continuously evaluates these controls and generates findings when non-compliant. EventBridge routing to SNS provides real-time alerting. Manual weekly checks miss interim violations. AWS Config managed rules for individual controls also work but require separate rule configuration for each control; Security Hub bundles these automatically. Trusted Advisor security checks are not as comprehensive as CIS benchmark controls.

q: A company wants to implement GitOps for its Amazon EKS cluster configuration. Changes to Kubernetes manifests committed to a Git repository should be automatically applied to the EKS cluster without manual kubectl commands. The solution must support rollback to previous Git commits if a deployment fails. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Use AWS Systems Manager Automation to periodically pull from the Git repository and apply Kubernetes manifests to the EKS cluster.
Configure an AWS Lambda function triggered by CodeCommit events to run kubectl apply commands against the EKS cluster.
Create an AWS CodePipeline that is triggered by Git repository changes. Add a CodeBuild stage that runs kubectl apply to apply manifests to the EKS cluster.

Install Argo CD on the Amazon EKS cluster. Configure Argo CD with the Git repository as the source of truth. Argo CD continuously monitors the repository and automatically synchronizes the cluster state with the Git-defined desired state, supporting rollback to any previous commit.
explain: Argo CD is the industry-standard GitOps tool for Kubernetes. It continuously reconciles the cluster state with the desired state defined in Git, automatically applying changes when commits are pushed. Argo CD's rollback capability allows reverting to any previous Git commit with a single operation. It provides a visual dashboard showing sync status, deployment history, and health of all resources. CodePipeline with CodeBuild is a push-based CI/CD approach — it applies changes when triggered but doesn't continuously reconcile drift or provide native GitOps rollback. Systems Manager and Lambda approaches add complexity without GitOps capabilities.

q: A company runs a complex distributed application on AWS with hundreds of microservices. The operations team finds it difficult to understand the relationships and dependencies between services and identify which services are causing cascading failures during incidents. Which AWS solution provides automatic service dependency mapping?
options: Enable AWS X-Ray for all microservices. The X-Ray service map automatically discovers and visualizes all service dependencies and communication patterns based on actual trace data, showing latency, error rates, and request volumes for each service connection.
Create a manual architecture diagram in AWS Systems Manager OpsCenter documenting all service dependencies.
Deploy AWS App Mesh and use the App Mesh console to view the service topology based on virtual node and virtual service configurations.
Use Amazon CloudWatch ServiceLens which integrates X-Ray service maps with CloudWatch metrics and logs to show the health of each service and its connections.
explain: AWS X-Ray automatically discovers service dependencies by analyzing traces from instrumented services. The X-Ray service map visualizes all services and their connections with real traffic data — latency heat maps, error percentages, and request volumes per connection — without any manual configuration of the topology. During incidents, the service map immediately highlights which service is the source of errors and shows the downstream impact on dependent services. CloudWatch ServiceLens (option 3) also uses X-Ray service maps and is valid, but the question asks specifically about automatic dependency mapping — X-Ray is the underlying engine. App Mesh topologies must be manually defined. Manual OpsCenter diagrams are not automatic.

q: A company's application tier running on EC2 instances in private subnets needs to send email notifications using Amazon SES. The EC2 instances do not have internet access. The SysOps administrator needs to enable SES access without adding internet connectivity to the private subnets. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Deploy a NAT gateway in the public subnet to allow EC2 instances to access SES over the internet.

Create an interface VPC endpoint for Amazon SES (com.amazonaws.region.email-smtp) in the private subnet. Update the EC2 security groups to allow outbound SMTP traffic to the VPC endpoint. EC2 instances use the endpoint to send emails through SES without internet access.
Configure Amazon SNS to relay email notifications from EC2 instances to SES without requiring direct SES API access.
Deploy an SMTP relay server on an EC2 instance in the public subnet and configure the private instances to route email through the relay.
explain: Amazon SES provides a VPC interface endpoint for its SMTP interface, allowing EC2 instances in private subnets to send emails using SMTP over a private connection without internet access. The interface endpoint creates a private IP within the VPC that routes SES SMTP traffic through AWS PrivateLink. A NAT gateway routes traffic through the internet, which violates the no-internet requirement. SNS cannot relay to SES on behalf of EC2 applications without custom Lambda integration. An SMTP relay server requires managing an additional EC2 instance and still requires internet access at the relay layer.

q: A company uses Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL with a Multi-AZ deployment. A developer accidentally dropped a critical table containing 6 months of sales data. The table was dropped 3 hours ago and the team just discovered the issue. The company needs to recover the table with as little data loss as possible. Which recovery approach should the SysOps administrator use?
options: Fail over the Multi-AZ standby instance to primary and recover the table from the standby. The standby contains a copy of the database at the time of failover.

Use RDS Point-in-Time Restore to create a new RDS instance restored to a point 5 minutes before the DROP TABLE statement was executed. Connect to the restored instance, export the recovered table data, and import it into the production database.
Restore the most recent RDS automated backup to a new instance and export the table from the backup. The backup may be up to 24 hours old.
Contact AWS Support to recover the dropped table from internal RDS storage backups.
explain: RDS Point-in-Time Restore creates a new DB instance restored to any second within the retention period. Restoring to 5 minutes before the DROP TABLE statement recovers the table with minimal data loss (only 5 minutes of transactions). The restored instance is created in addition to the production database (not replacing it), allowing the team to export the recovered table using pg_dump and import it into production without downtime. The most recent automated backup is at most 24 hours old — losing 3+ hours of data plus the backup age. Multi-AZ standby mirrors the primary synchronously, so the DROP TABLE was immediately replicated to the standby. AWS Support cannot recover individual dropped tables.

q: A company recently enabled AWS Cost Anomaly Detection and received an alert that costs in a specific AWS account increased by $50,000 above the expected amount over the past week. The SysOps administrator needs to identify the root cause. Which steps should the administrator take to investigate?
options: Contact AWS Support and provide the anomaly alert ID for AWS to investigate the root cause.
Review the EC2 console in the affected account to look for new instances that may be causing the cost increase.
Check the AWS CloudTrail logs for all API calls in the affected account to find who created resources.

Open the Cost Anomaly Detection finding in the AWS Billing console. Review the root cause analysis which breaks down the anomaly by service, usage type, and account. Use AWS Cost Explorer to drill into the specific service with anomalous costs and filter by resource tags and time period to identify specific resources causing the spike.
explain: AWS Cost Anomaly Detection findings include a root cause analysis that identifies which services, usage types, and accounts contributed most to the anomaly. Starting with the Cost Anomaly Detection finding provides the breakdown of which service drove the increase. Cost Explorer drill-down with service filters and time-range filtering allows identifying specific resources (by instance ID, bucket name, etc.) and their cost contribution. CloudTrail API logs identify who created resources but don't directly show cost breakdown. Manually reviewing the EC2 console misses other services. AWS Support should be involved only if internal investigation cannot identify the cause.

q: A company uses AWS Config to monitor compliance across its AWS accounts. The security team wants Config to automatically record configuration changes for all supported AWS resource types including resources in new regions as AWS adds support. The configuration recorder must be future-proof. Which configuration meets these requirements?
options: Enable AWS Config with a resource scope limited to the 10 most critical resource types to reduce Config costs.

Configure the AWS Config configuration recorder to record all resource types by setting allSupported to true and includeGlobalResourceTypes to true. Config automatically records all currently supported types and extends to newly supported types as AWS adds them.
Enumerate all currently supported AWS Config resource types and configure the configuration recorder to record that specific list. Update the list manually as new resource types become available.
Configure Config to record only resources with specific tags to focus on critical resources and reduce recording costs.
explain: Setting allSupported: true in the Config configuration recorder automatically includes all currently supported AWS resource types and automatically extends to new resource types as AWS adds Config support for them. This is the future-proof configuration that requires no maintenance. includeGlobalResourceTypes: true adds IAM users, roles, and policies which are global resources. Manually enumerating resource types requires constant updates as AWS adds support for new types. Limiting to 10 resource types misses coverage and doesn't meet the all-resources requirement. Tag-based recording reduces costs but creates compliance gaps for untagged resources.

q: A company uses AWS CodeBuild to build and test its application before deployment. The build process requires access to a private npm package registry hosted on an EC2 instance in a VPC. CodeBuild runs in the default (outside VPC) configuration and cannot reach the private registry. Which solution enables CodeBuild to access the private npm registry?
options: Expose the private npm registry on the EC2 instance to the internet with a public IP address and configure a CodeBuild environment variable with the registry URL.

Configure the CodeBuild project to use a VPC configuration that places the build environment inside the specified VPC. Ensure the CodeBuild security group allows outbound access to the EC2 instance hosting the private npm registry.
Use AWS Transfer Family to create an SFTP endpoint in the VPC that CodeBuild can use to download npm packages from the private registry.
Set up a NAT gateway in the VPC and configure routing so the private EC2 instance is accessible through the NAT gateway from the internet.
explain: AWS CodeBuild supports VPC configuration that places the build compute environment inside a specified VPC, subnet, and security group. With VPC configuration, the CodeBuild build environment gets a private IP address within the VPC and can communicate with other resources in the VPC, including the private npm registry on the EC2 instance. The CodeBuild security group must allow outbound access to the EC2 instance's port (typically 4873 for Verdaccio/private npm). A NAT gateway is for outbound internet access from VPC resources, not for making VPC resources accessible from CodeBuild. Exposing the registry to the internet creates a security risk. AWS Transfer Family is for file transfer, not npm registry access.

q: A company's production application generates 50 GB of logs per day in Amazon CloudWatch Logs. Retaining all logs for 1 year is becoming expensive. The security and compliance team requires that critical error logs and audit logs must be retained for 1 year, but routine INFO and DEBUG logs can be deleted after 7 days. Which solution implements tiered log retention with the LEAST operational overhead?
options: Set a 365-day retention policy on all CloudWatch Logs log groups to ensure compliance and accept the higher storage cost.
Use a Lambda function triggered daily to parse log groups and delete INFO/DEBUG log streams older than 7 days while retaining ERROR log streams.

Configure separate CloudWatch Logs log groups for different log levels: one for ERROR/AUDIT logs with a 365-day retention policy and one for INFO/DEBUG logs with a 7-day retention policy. Update the application logging configuration to route log statements to the appropriate log group based on log level.

Create a CloudWatch Logs subscription filter that sends all logs to Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose, which routes ERROR logs to S3 with long-term storage and filters out INFO/DEBUG logs after 7 days using S3 Lifecycle policies.
explain: Separating log groups by severity level is the cleanest approach for tiered retention. CloudWatch Logs supports per-log-group retention policies from 1 day to 10 years. Updating the application to route logs to separate groups by severity (e.g., myapp-errors and myapp-debug) with different retention policies requires only a logging configuration change. This is fully managed by CloudWatch with no ongoing infrastructure. A single 365-day retention policy for all logs is the simplest but most expensive option. Kinesis Firehose with S3 routing adds infrastructure complexity. Daily Lambda parsing of log streams is complex and error-prone.

q: A company deploys an API on Amazon API Gateway backed by AWS Lambda. During load testing, the team discovers that the API returns 429 Too Many Requests errors when concurrent request volume exceeds 500 requests per second. The company wants to increase the API throughput limit. Which configuration change resolves the 429 errors?
options: Configure API Gateway as a regional endpoint instead of edge-optimized to increase the request rate limit.
Use API Gateway WebSocket API instead of REST API to support higher concurrent connections.
Enable API Gateway caching at the stage level to reduce the number of requests reaching the Lambda backend.

Submit a service quota increase request to AWS Support to increase the API Gateway regional request rate limit above the default 10,000 RPS per Region. At the API or stage level, configure a usage plan with higher throttling limits. Also increase the Lambda reserved concurrency limit.
explain: API Gateway has default account-level throttle limits of 10,000 RPS with a burst of 5,000. Individual APIs have stage-level throttle limits that may be lower. Submitting a quota increase request addresses the account-level limit, while usage plan throttling addresses per-API limits. Lambda reserved concurrency also needs to be sufficient to handle the increased throughput. API Gateway caching helps for repeated identical requests but doesn't increase the rate limit. Regional vs edge-optimized endpoint type affects routing, not rate limits. WebSocket APIs handle persistent connections, not REST API throughput.

q: A company runs a web application on Amazon EC2 in us-east-1. Users in Asia report high latency when accessing the application. The company wants to reduce latency for Asian users without deploying a full application stack in another region. Which solution reduces latency for global users with minimal infrastructure changes?
options: Create an Amazon CloudFront distribution with the EC2 application as a custom origin. CloudFront caches static content at edge locations in Asia and proxies dynamic requests to the us-east-1 origin over AWS's optimized backbone network, reducing latency for all content types.
Use AWS Global Accelerator to route Asian users to the us-east-1 application over the AWS global network instead of the public internet.
Deploy additional EC2 instances in ap-southeast-1 running the same application and use Route 53 geolocation routing to direct Asian users to the closest region.
Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration for the application's static assets to serve them from edge locations closer to Asian users.
explain: Amazon CloudFront reduces latency through two mechanisms: (1) static content (HTML, CSS, JS, images) is cached at edge locations in Asia, serving requests from the nearest PoP without crossing the ocean, and (2) dynamic requests are routed through the AWS global network backbone from the Asian edge to the us-east-1 origin, which is faster than public internet routing. This requires only creating a CloudFront distribution — no application deployment in Asia. Deploying EC2 in Asia requires full application stack management. S3 Transfer Acceleration only applies to S3 uploads. Global Accelerator improves dynamic request routing over AWS backbone but doesn't cache static content.

q: A company uses Amazon DynamoDB to store session data for a web application. The application is currently in one AWS Region. As the company expands globally, users in other regions experience high latency when reading session data. The company wants to reduce session read latency for global users while maintaining strong consistency for session writes in the home region. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Migrate session storage from DynamoDB to Amazon ElastiCache for Redis with cross-region replication enabled.
Deploy separate DynamoDB tables in each region and use application-level session synchronization between regions.
Enable DynamoDB DAX in each region with a DynamoDB table per region to cache session reads locally.

Enable DynamoDB global tables with replicas in each target region. Configure the application in each region to read from the local DynamoDB replica using eventually consistent reads. Session writes still occur in the home region with strong consistency.
explain: DynamoDB global tables provide multi-region active-active replication with automatic synchronization. Applications in each region can read session data from the local replica with sub-millisecond latency (eventually consistent reads). Writes in the home region are replicated to all replicas within seconds. This provides low-latency reads globally while maintaining a single authoritative write path. Application-level synchronization requires custom replication code and is complex to maintain. DynamoDB DAX improves in-region read latency but doesn't replicate data across regions. ElastiCache with cross-region replication is possible but adds infrastructure complexity and doesn't integrate with DynamoDB's consistency model.

q: A SysOps administrator needs to update an AWS CloudFormation stack that includes an Amazon SQS queue. The update changes the queue name, which requires replacing the SQS resource (CloudFormation must create a new queue and delete the old one). Messages currently in the old queue must not be lost during the update. Which approach should the administrator take before executing the stack update?
options: Create a CloudFormation change set to preview the update, then execute the update and manually copy messages from the old queue to the new queue after the update completes.
Add a CloudFormation DeletionPolicy of Retain on the SQS queue resource so the old queue is not deleted, then manually delete it after migrating messages.
Execute the CloudFormation stack update immediately. CloudFormation automatically migrates messages from the old SQS queue to the new queue during resource replacement.

Before executing the stack update, drain all messages from the current SQS queue by processing them or moving them to a temporary queue. After all messages are processed or moved, execute the CloudFormation stack update which will create the new queue with the new name and delete the old empty queue.
explain: CloudFormation resource replacement for SQS queues creates the new queue and deletes the old one without migrating messages. To prevent message loss, the administrator must first ensure the old queue is empty before the update. This means either processing all pending messages or moving them to a temporary holding location. After the old queue is drained, the CloudFormation stack update can proceed safely. CloudFormation does not automatically migrate SQS messages during replacement. Creating a change set previews the replacement but executing it still requires pre-migration. DeletionPolicy Retain prevents deletion but requires manually deleting the old queue later, not preventing message loss during the replacement window.

q: A company wants to implement automated security response for AWS accounts in their organization. When Amazon GuardDuty generates a high-severity finding for an EC2 instance (such as cryptocurrency mining or malware communication), the response system must automatically isolate the instance by moving it to a quarantine security group within 5 minutes. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Configure Amazon GuardDuty to automatically move EC2 instances to a quarantine security group when high-severity findings are generated.
Enable AWS Security Hub with automated response and remediation (SOAR) playbooks to isolate EC2 instances when GuardDuty high-severity findings are detected.

Create an Amazon EventBridge rule that matches GuardDuty findings with severity >= 7 and resource type EC2. Configure the rule to trigger an AWS Lambda function that retrieves the instance ID from the finding, modifies the instance's security group to a pre-created quarantine security group (no inbound/outbound rules except for forensics access), and tags the instance as Quarantined.
Create an AWS Systems Manager Automation runbook that runs daily to review GuardDuty findings and quarantine affected EC2 instances.
explain: EventBridge receives GuardDuty findings in near real time (within seconds). Filtering for severity >= 7 (high) and EC2 resource type targets the specific scenario. The Lambda function uses the GuardDuty finding's instanceId to call ec2:ModifyInstanceAttribute to replace the instance's security group with a pre-configured quarantine security group that blocks all traffic. This automated response completes well within the 5-minute requirement. GuardDuty does not natively quarantine instances. A daily SSM runbook doesn't meet the 5-minute response requirement. Security Hub SOAR can trigger automated responses but requires additional configuration compared to the direct EventBridge → Lambda pattern.

q: A company is reviewing its AWS architecture for cost optimization opportunities. The solutions architect identifies that many Amazon RDS for MySQL databases in development accounts have been running Multi-AZ for reliability, but the development databases don't require high availability. The company wants to reduce RDS costs in development while maintaining production Multi-AZ. Which approach reduces costs for development databases?
options: Downgrade all RDS instances (both development and production) to Single-AZ to reduce costs across the board.
Replace the development RDS instances with Amazon DynamoDB to eliminate RDS costs entirely.
Purchase Reserved Instances for the development RDS instances to receive a discount on Multi-AZ pricing.

Modify the development RDS instances to disable Multi-AZ by setting the MultiAZ parameter to false. This eliminates the cost of the standby instance, reducing the RDS cost by approximately 50% for those instances.
explain: Multi-AZ RDS deployments charge approximately double the single-AZ price because a standby instance runs continuously. Development databases typically don't need Multi-AZ since availability isn't critical and data can be regenerated. Disabling Multi-AZ for development instances reduces RDS costs by ~50% for those instances with no impact on production. This is a safe optimization for non-critical environments. Disabling Multi-AZ in production violates availability requirements. Replacing with DynamoDB requires significant application migration effort. Reserved Instances for development Multi-AZ still pay for both instances (primary + standby) at a discount but don't achieve the 50% savings from removing standby.

q: A company stores financial transaction logs in Amazon S3. The audit team needs to query these logs to answer ad-hoc compliance questions, such as finding all transactions above $10,000 from a specific date range. The logs are in Parquet format partitioned by year and month. The solution must minimize query costs and time. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Load the transaction logs into Amazon Redshift Spectrum to query the S3 data using Redshift SQL. Configure a Redshift cluster sized for the audit team's query workload.
Use Amazon EMR with Apache Spark SQL to query the Parquet files in S3. Launch a cluster on demand for audit queries and terminate after use.

Use Amazon Athena to query the Parquet files directly in S3. Create an Athena table with partition projection configured for year and month. The audit team can run SQL queries directly against S3 without loading data into a database. Use partition pruning in WHERE clauses to minimize data scanned.
Use AWS Glue ETL to load the Parquet files into Amazon RDS for MySQL and run SQL queries on the relational database.
explain: Amazon Athena is purpose-built for interactive SQL queries on S3 data without any infrastructure management. Parquet format is natively supported and provides columnar storage for efficient queries. Partition projection eliminates Glue Data Catalog partition registration overhead and automatically generates partition locations for year/month prefixes. Using partition filters in WHERE clauses (WHERE year='2024' AND month='03') limits data scanned to relevant partitions, minimizing both query time and cost (Athena charges per TB scanned). Redshift requires provisioning a cluster. EMR has cluster startup latency and management overhead. Glue ETL loading into RDS loses the benefit of S3-native querying.

q: A company runs Amazon ECS tasks on AWS Fargate. Some tasks are failing due to insufficient memory — the container process is being OOM-killed. The team is not sure which specific tasks are running out of memory or how close other tasks are to their memory limits. Which solution provides visibility into Fargate task memory usage?
options: Enable VPC Flow Logs to monitor network traffic from Fargate tasks and correlate high traffic with memory issues.

Enable Amazon ECS Container Insights for the cluster. Container Insights automatically collects MemoryUtilized and MemoryReserved metrics for all Fargate tasks. Create CloudWatch alarms to alert when task memory utilization exceeds 80% of the configured memory limit.
Install the Amazon CloudWatch agent as a sidecar container in each ECS task definition to collect OS-level memory metrics and publish them to CloudWatch.
Use AWS Compute Optimizer to analyze Fargate task resource utilization and receive right-sizing recommendations.
explain: Amazon ECS Container Insights provides pre-built container-level metrics for Fargate tasks including MemoryUtilized (actual memory used) and MemoryReserved (configured memory limit) without any additional configuration within the task definition. These metrics are available in CloudWatch under the ECS/ContainerInsights namespace per task definition, service, and cluster. CloudWatch alarms on MemoryUtilized/MemoryReserved percentage identify tasks approaching OOM. A CloudWatch agent sidecar requires modifying every task definition and shares the task's memory limit. Compute Optimizer provides right-sizing recommendations but doesn't provide real-time per-task memory monitoring. VPC Flow Logs capture network metadata, not memory metrics.

q: A company uses AWS IAM roles for service-to-service authentication between its microservices. A new compliance requirement mandates that all inter-service API calls must be logged with the source service identity. The company uses Amazon ECS with task roles for authentication. The solution must not require application code changes. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Deploy an AWS App Mesh service mesh to intercept and log all inter-service traffic with service identity information.
Add custom HTTP headers to all inter-service API calls in the application code to identify the calling service and log them in Amazon CloudWatch Logs.

Enable AWS CloudTrail data events for API Gateway and enable CloudTrail management events for all AWS services. CloudTrail automatically logs the IAM role ARN (from the task role) in the userIdentity field for every API call, providing the source service identity without any code changes.
Enable AWS X-Ray on all ECS tasks to trace inter-service calls and log the source service identity in X-Ray segments.
explain: AWS CloudTrail automatically captures the IAM identity for every AWS API call in the userIdentity field, including the task role ARN for ECS tasks. For inter-service calls made using AWS SDK (which use IAM roles for authentication), CloudTrail logs the calling service's role ARN without any code changes. Enabling management events and relevant data events ensures all service-to-service API calls are captured with complete identity information. Custom headers require application code changes. App Mesh intercepts and logs network traffic but doesn't provide IAM identity mapping. X-Ray traces requests but focuses on performance, not complete API call logging with IAM identity for compliance.

q: A company runs a Java application on Amazon EC2. The application server takes 8 minutes to initialize — loading configuration, warming caches, and establishing database connection pools. During Auto Scaling scale-out events, new instances are added to the ALB target group immediately after launch, receiving production traffic before initialization completes, causing errors. Which solution ensures new instances only receive traffic after they are fully initialized?
options: Increase the EC2 instance type to reduce the application initialization time below the default ALB health check evaluation period.
Configure the application to set a custom HTTP header after initialization. Create an ALB listener rule that requires this header before routing traffic.

Configure the EC2 Auto Scaling group to use a warm pool with the pool instances in a Stopped state. Alternatively, configure the ALB target group health check to use a custom health check endpoint that the application exposes only after full initialization, and set the health check grace period to 600 seconds to match the initialization time.
Use Amazon Route 53 health checks to verify the application is running before adding instances to DNS routing.
explain: The ALB target group health check with a custom application health endpoint is the correct solution. The application exposes a /health endpoint that returns 200 OK only after all initialization is complete (caches loaded, connection pools established). The ALB only routes traffic to instances passing health checks, so the instance is not in service until initialization completes. Setting the health check grace period to 600 seconds (10 minutes) prevents the health check from failing the instance during the expected 8-minute startup. Using a larger instance type reduces initialization time but doesn't guarantee it completes before the ALB routes traffic. Route 53 health checks work for DNS-based routing, not ALB target group management. Custom HTTP headers as routing criteria is not an ALB capability.

q: A company wants to implement cost allocation reporting that shows AWS costs attributed to individual application features. Each feature team tags all their AWS resources with a Feature tag. However, some AWS resources like Lambda invocations and CloudFront requests don't support resource-level tagging. The company needs these untaggable costs attributed to the correct feature. Which approach provides the most accurate cost attribution?
options: Accept that costs from untaggable services cannot be attributed to features and only report on tagged resource costs.
Create separate AWS accounts for each feature team to provide complete cost isolation without requiring resource tagging.
Enable AWS Compute Savings Plans which automatically optimize Lambda costs and group them by compute usage regardless of tagging.

Use AWS Cost Categories to create rules that attribute costs from untaggable services (Lambda, CloudFront) to feature categories based on account, region, or service usage patterns. Combine with tag-based cost allocation for resources that support tagging to get comprehensive feature-level cost reports in Cost Explorer.
explain: AWS Cost Categories provide a flexible way to group and allocate costs based on rules that can include accounts, tags, services, regions, and charge types. For untaggable services like Lambda invocations and CloudFront requests, Cost Categories can create rules based on the account the service runs in (if using account-per-feature) or service+region combinations to approximate feature attribution. Combining with tag-based rules provides the most complete picture. Accepting gaps in cost attribution leaves significant costs unallocated. Savings Plans optimize cost but don't provide attribution. Separate accounts provide perfect isolation but add significant operational overhead for many feature teams.

q: A company uses Amazon CloudFront to deliver a global web application.
