During a recent origin server outage that lasted 45 minutes, CloudFront served expired cached content for some URLs but returned 503 errors for others because the cached content had already expired. The company wants to serve stale cached content for all URLs during origin outages, maximizing availability. Which CloudFront configuration achieves this?
options: Enable CloudFront Origin Shield to add a centralized caching tier that maintains cached content longer during origin failures.
Use Amazon S3 as a static website origin for CloudFront with a failover routing policy in Route 53 to serve cached content during outages.
Increase the CloudFront default TTL from 24 hours to 7 days so that cached content is less likely to expire during outages.

Enable CloudFront Origin Failover with a second origin. Alternatively, configure the CloudFront cache behavior to enable the Serve stale content while revalidating option (stale-while-revalidate) and Serve stale content on error (stale-if-error) with a long error caching TTL to serve cached content even when the origin returns errors or is unavailable.
explain: CloudFront's stale-if-error behavior controls how long CloudFront serves cached content when the origin returns an error. When configured with a long error caching TTL (e.g., 86400 seconds = 24 hours), CloudFront serves stale cached content for any URL that has been previously cached, rather than returning errors to users. Origin Failover with a secondary origin provides an active failover path. Combining both provides maximum availability. Increasing default TTL helps for content that hasn't expired yet but doesn't help for content that has already expired. Origin Shield adds a caching layer but subject to the same TTL expirations. S3 static failover only works for static content.

q: A company runs Amazon EKS workloads that have variable compute requirements. The team runs a mix of batch jobs (flexible timing, can tolerate interruptions) and web services (must be always available). The company wants to optimize compute costs for this mixed workload. Which combination of EC2 purchasing options is MOST cost-effective?
options: Use all Spot Instances for the EKS cluster to maximize cost savings. Implement aggressive pod retry policies to handle Spot interruptions for web services.

Use On-Demand Instances or Reserved Instances (covered by Compute Savings Plans) for the web service pods to ensure always-on availability. Use Spot Instances for batch job pods. Configure the EKS cluster with node groups for each type and use Kubernetes pod scheduling with node selectors or tolerations to route workloads to appropriate nodes.
Use all On-Demand Instances for the EKS cluster to ensure consistent availability for all workloads.
Use Reserved Instances for all EKS nodes with a 3-year commitment to achieve maximum savings.
explain: Separating web services and batch jobs onto different node groups with different purchasing options is the standard EKS cost optimization pattern. Web services run on Compute Savings Plans or On-Demand nodes ensuring continuous availability. Batch jobs run on Spot Instance node groups with up to 90% savings — interruptions cause job restart (tolerable per the requirements). Kubernetes node selectors and tolerations route pods to appropriate node groups. Using all Spot Instances for web services creates availability risk during Spot interruptions. All On-Demand is the highest cost option. A 3-year RI commitment for variable workloads locks capital unnecessarily.

q: A company uses AWS CloudTrail and stores logs in an Amazon S3 bucket. The security team wants to run complex SQL queries against CloudTrail logs to detect security events, such as privilege escalation attempts, cross-account role assumptions, and IAM policy changes. The solution must support queries on historical data going back 2 years. Which solution enables this with the LEAST infrastructure management?
options: Use AWS Lake Formation to create a data lake with the CloudTrail logs and query using Amazon Redshift Spectrum.
Write custom AWS Lambda functions that parse CloudTrail JSON files from S3 and store results in DynamoDB for querying.

Create an AWS Glue Data Catalog table pointing to the CloudTrail S3 bucket. Use Amazon Athena to run SQL queries against CloudTrail logs directly in S3 without loading data elsewhere. Partition the Glue table by account, region, and date for efficient historical queries.
Load 2 years of CloudTrail logs into Amazon OpenSearch Service and use OpenSearch queries to detect security events.
explain: Amazon Athena with an AWS Glue Data Catalog table is the standard serverless approach for querying CloudTrail logs in S3. CloudTrail delivers logs to S3 in a predictable partition structure (AWSLogs/accountId/CloudTrail/region/year/month/day), which Glue tables can leverage for partition pruning. Athena queries CloudTrail JSON directly without data loading, supports complex SQL for security analysis, and has no infrastructure to manage. Historical data access depends only on S3 retention, not database size. OpenSearch requires provisioning and scaling a cluster. Lake Formation with Redshift Spectrum adds access governance infrastructure. Custom Lambda requires significant development and maintenance.

q: A company runs a PHP application on AWS Elastic Beanstalk using the PHP platform. After a platform version upgrade by AWS, the application started failing health checks. The SysOps administrator needs to roll back to the previous platform version immediately to restore service. Which action resolves the issue?
options: In the Elastic Beanstalk console, navigate to the environment's Configuration, select Platform, and change the platform version back to the previous working version using the Update platform version option. Elastic Beanstalk performs a rolling update to redeploy the application on the previous platform version.
Terminate the Elastic Beanstalk environment and create a new environment with the previous platform version from the application's saved configuration.
Deploy the application to a new Elastic Beanstalk environment with the previous platform version and update DNS to point to the new environment.
SSH into the EC2 instances and manually downgrade the PHP version to match the previous platform version.
explain: Elastic Beanstalk supports changing the platform version for an existing environment through the console or CLI. The administrator can select the previous platform version from the platform version history and Elastic Beanstalk performs an in-place update (using the configured deployment policy) to redeploy all instances with the previous platform version while keeping the same environment, URL, and resources. This is the fastest path to rollback without DNS changes. Terminating and recreating the environment takes longer and changes the environment endpoint URL. Manual SSH PHP downgrade is not supported or maintainable in Beanstalk-managed environments. Creating a new environment requires DNS updates and additional configuration.

q: A company has deployed Amazon EC2 instances that host a time-sensitive financial application. The application requires deterministic, consistent network performance with no packet loss and low jitter between instances. Standard EC2 networking is insufficient for the application's requirements. Which EC2 networking feature should the SysOps administrator enable?
options: Enable Elastic Fabric Adapter (EFA) on the EC2 instances that support it. EFA provides OS-bypass networking that allows the application to communicate directly with the network hardware, bypassing the operating system kernel and TCP/IP stack to achieve ultra-low latency, high throughput, and near-zero jitter.
Place the EC2 instances in a cluster placement group to reduce physical network distance and improve communication latency.
Assign Elastic IP addresses to the EC2 instances to ensure stable, consistent network identifiers and improve routing consistency.
Enable Enhanced Networking with the Elastic Network Adapter (ENA) on the EC2 instances to increase network throughput and reduce latency.
explain: Elastic Fabric Adapter (EFA) provides OS-bypass networking — the application communicates directly with the network interface hardware without going through the OS TCP/IP stack. This eliminates OS scheduling jitter, context switching overhead, and TCP protocol overhead, achieving deterministic, ultra-low latency communication with near-zero jitter. EFA is required for HPC and latency-sensitive financial applications that cannot tolerate network variability. Enhanced Networking with ENA improves throughput and reduces latency versus standard networking but still uses the OS networking stack. Elastic IPs provide stable addressing, not performance improvement. Cluster placement groups reduce physical distance but don't provide OS-bypass networking.

q: A company runs a critical application that uses Amazon SQS for message queuing. The application requires that each message is processed exactly once. The operations team has found that due to network issues, some messages are occasionally processed twice when the consumer restarts after a failure before deleting the processed message from SQS. Which solution ensures exactly-once message processing?
options: Increase the SQS visibility timeout to a value much longer than the consumer processing time to prevent messages from becoming visible again during consumer restarts.

Switch from an SQS Standard queue to an SQS FIFO queue. FIFO queues with content-based deduplication enabled provide exactly-once processing by detecting and discarding duplicate messages within the 5-minute deduplication window. Ensure consumer application deletes messages immediately after successful processing.
Add application-level deduplication by storing processed message IDs in Amazon DynamoDB with a TTL. Before processing each message, check the DynamoDB table for the message ID.
Use Amazon SNS FIFO with SQS FIFO subscriptions to guarantee exactly-once delivery through the full messaging pipeline.
explain: SQS FIFO queues provide exactly-once processing guarantees during the 5-minute deduplication window, but this may not cover the scenario where a consumer restarts after processing but before deletion if the restart takes longer than the visibility timeout. Application-level deduplication using DynamoDB provides a more robust exactly-once guarantee: storing the message ID (from SQS MessageId) in DynamoDB with a conditional write before processing ensures idempotent processing — if the consumer restarts and reprocesses the message, the DynamoDB conditional write fails, indicating the message was already processed. FIFO queue deduplication handles duplicate sends, not duplicate processing due to consumer failures. Increasing visibility timeout reduces but doesn't eliminate duplicate processing.

q: A company wants to implement a centralized secrets management solution for 50 microservices running on Amazon ECS. Each microservice should only access its own secrets, and the access must be audited. Secrets must be automatically rotated every 30 days. The solution must minimize application code changes. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Store secrets in AWS Secrets Manager and grant all 50 microservices access to all secrets using a shared ECS task role to simplify management.

Store all secrets in AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store as SecureString parameters and grant all microservices full access to Parameter Store. Write a custom rotation Lambda function that updates all secrets every 30 days.

Store all microservice secrets in AWS Secrets Manager using a naming convention (e.g., /service-name/secret-name). Create a separate ECS task role for each microservice with an IAM policy that allows secretsmanager:GetSecretValue only for ARNs matching the service's prefix. Configure ECS to inject secrets as environment variables using the secrets field in the task definition. Enable rotation with a 30-day schedule on each secret.
Create a HashiCorp Vault cluster on Amazon EC2 and configure all microservices to retrieve secrets from Vault using service-specific policies.
explain: AWS Secrets Manager with ECS native secrets injection provides minimal code changes — ECS automatically retrieves secrets from Secrets Manager and injects them as environment variables (using the secrets field in task definition), requiring no SDK calls in the application. Individual task roles per microservice with resource-level IAM policies on Secrets Manager ARNs enforce least privilege. CloudTrail automatically audits all Secrets Manager API calls. Built-in rotation with 30-day schedules handles automatic secret rotation. Shared task roles violate least privilege. Parameter Store with full access violates least privilege. HashiCorp Vault adds significant infrastructure management.

q: A company is building a new data pipeline on AWS. The pipeline ingests streaming data from IoT devices using Amazon Kinesis Data Streams, processes it with AWS Lambda, and stores results in Amazon S3. The data engineering team wants to minimize costs while maintaining sub-minute processing latency. Which Kinesis Data Streams configuration minimizes costs for a variable workload that peaks during business hours and is nearly idle overnight?
options: Use Kinesis Data Streams on-demand mode instead of provisioned mode. On-demand mode automatically scales based on actual throughput, eliminating the need to provision and pay for unused shard capacity during overnight idle periods. On-demand charges per actual data records ingested and retrieved.
Replace Kinesis Data Streams with Amazon SQS for the IoT data ingestion to eliminate Kinesis shard costs.
Use Kinesis Data Streams provisioned mode with 1 shard sized for peak load. Kinesis shards are cheap and simplify capacity management.
Use Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose instead of Kinesis Data Streams to eliminate shard-based pricing entirely.
explain: Kinesis Data Streams on-demand mode eliminates shard capacity planning and charges based on actual data throughput (per GB ingested and retrieved) rather than per-shard-hour. During overnight idle periods, costs drop to near zero when there is no data. During business hour peaks, on-demand automatically scales to handle the load without manual shard adjustment. Provisioned mode with shards sized for peak incurs full shard costs 24/7 even when idle. SQS doesn't provide ordered streaming or Kinesis's replay capability. Kinesis Data Firehose delivers to data stores (S3, Redshift) but doesn't support custom Lambda processing with sub-minute latency in the same way.

q: A company's SysOps team manages an AWS environment where developers frequently change EC2 security group rules without going through the change management process. The team wants to automatically detect unauthorized security group changes and revert them to the approved configuration within 15 minutes. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Enable AWS Config with the restricted-ssh and vpc-sg-open-only-to-authorized-ports managed rules. Create an EventBridge rule on Config compliance change events. Trigger an AWS Lambda function that calls the ec2:AuthorizeSecurityGroupIngress and ec2:RevokeSecurityGroupIngress APIs to restore the approved security group configuration from a Parameter Store-stored baseline.
Monitor EC2 security group changes using AWS CloudTrail and send daily email reports of changes for the change management team to review.
Create an IAM policy that denies ec2:AuthorizeSecurityGroupIngress for developer IAM roles to prevent unauthorized changes.
Use AWS Service Catalog to manage approved security group configurations and prevent developers from modifying security groups directly.
explain: AWS Config continuously evaluates security group configurations against defined rules. When a security group becomes non-compliant, Config generates a compliance change event in near real time. EventBridge routes this to Lambda, which reads the approved configuration from Parameter Store and uses EC2 API calls to revert the security group to the baseline. This entire chain typically completes within 2-5 minutes, well within the 15-minute requirement. Daily email reports are reactive, not automated remediation. Service Catalog controls provisioning but not ongoing modifications to existing resources. Denying ec2:AuthorizeSecurityGroupIngress is a preventive control but doesn't handle automated remediation.

q: A company runs AWS Lambda functions that connect to an Amazon RDS PostgreSQL database. During peak traffic, the number of Lambda concurrent executions reaches 500, causing the RDS database to run out of connections (max_connections: 200). Lambda function invocations start failing with 'too many connections' errors. Which solution resolves the connection exhaustion with the LEAST code changes?
options: Increase the RDS instance type to a larger size that supports more connections.
Reduce Lambda Reserved Concurrency to 200 to match the database connection limit.

Deploy Amazon RDS Proxy between the Lambda functions and the RDS database. Configure Lambda functions to connect to the RDS Proxy endpoint. RDS Proxy maintains a connection pool and multiplexes thousands of Lambda connections into a smaller set of actual database connections, eliminating connection exhaustion.
Implement connection pooling within the Lambda function code by reusing the database connection object between invocations within the same execution environment.
explain: Amazon RDS Proxy maintains a persistent connection pool to the RDS database and multiplexes many Lambda connections into a smaller set of actual database connections. RDS Proxy can handle thousands of Lambda connections while maintaining only the connections the database can support. Updating Lambda to use the RDS Proxy endpoint is a minimal code change (only the connection string changes). Increasing instance type increases max_connections but doesn't scale proportionally with Lambda concurrency. Lambda connection reuse within execution environments reduces connections per Lambda container but doesn't solve the problem when 500 concurrent executions each hold a connection. Limiting Lambda concurrency reduces throughput during peak.

q: A company uses AWS CloudFormation for all infrastructure. A SysOps administrator needs to track when CloudFormation stacks are created, updated, or deleted, and who performed each action, for a 90-day audit trail. The solution must not require any additional services beyond what is already used. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Set up an Amazon EventBridge rule that matches CloudFormation stack events and stores them in Amazon DynamoDB with a 90-day TTL.
Create CloudWatch Logs metric filters on CloudFormation stack status change events and store the filtered logs for 90 days.
Enable AWS Config to record CloudFormation stack resource changes and use the Config timeline to review stack configuration history.

AWS CloudTrail already captures all CloudFormation API calls (CreateStack, UpdateStack, DeleteStack) including the IAM identity of the caller in the event history. Enable CloudTrail log file validation and configure the trail to deliver logs to S3 with a 90-day lifecycle or retention policy to maintain the audit trail.
explain: AWS CloudTrail automatically captures all CloudFormation management API calls — CreateStack, UpdateStack, DeleteStack, and more — including the userIdentity (IAM user or role), source IP, and timestamp for each action. This provides the complete audit trail without additional services. CloudTrail event history is available for 90 days by default in the console, and configuring a trail to deliver to S3 with an appropriate retention policy extends this indefinitely. AWS Config records resource configuration states but not who performed actions. CloudWatch metric filters require the API calls to be logged to CloudWatch Logs first. EventBridge + DynamoDB requires custom setup and code.

q: A company's security policy requires that all AWS Lambda function environment variables containing sensitive values (API keys, database passwords) must be encrypted with a customer-managed KMS key, not the default Lambda service key. The company has 200 Lambda functions and wants to verify compliance and receive alerts for any function that uses the default encryption. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Use AWS Security Hub with Lambda-specific security standards to detect functions using default encryption for environment variables.

Create an AWS Config custom rule using a Lambda function that checks each Lambda function's KMSKeyArn attribute to verify it is set to an approved customer-managed KMS key ARN. Configure automatic remediation with an SNS alert for non-compliant functions. Run the Config rule in all AWS accounts.
Enable Amazon Inspector to scan Lambda functions for encryption configuration issues and generate findings for functions not using CMK encryption.
Write a daily Lambda function that uses the AWS SDK to list all Lambda functions and check their KMS key configuration, sending SNS notifications for non-compliant functions.
explain: AWS Config custom rules using Lambda evaluations can check any resource attribute accessible through the AWS API. The Lambda function in the Config rule calls GetFunctionConfiguration for each Lambda function and checks whether the KMSKeyArn attribute is set to an approved customer-managed KMS key. Config evaluates all existing functions and evaluates new/modified functions as they change. SNS notifications for non-compliant functions provide the alerting requirement. Amazon Inspector focuses on software vulnerabilities and network exposure, not encryption configuration. Security Hub doesn't have specific Lambda KMS encryption standards. Daily Lambda polling doesn't provide real-time compliance detection.

q: A company uses AWS CloudFormation to deploy a multi-tier web application. The stack includes an ALB, Auto Scaling group, RDS database, and several Lambda functions. During a stack update, one of the Lambda functions failed to update due to a packaging error. CloudFormation automatically rolled back the entire stack, but the rollback also failed because the RDS database schema had already been migrated and the old Lambda version is incompatible with the new schema. Which approach should the SysOps administrator take to resolve the stuck stack?
options: Delete the CloudFormation stack and redeploy from scratch using the original template and database backup from before the update.
Contact AWS Support to force the CloudFormation stack rollback to complete.

In the CloudFormation console, use the Continue Rolling Back option and mark the failed Lambda resource as Skip to skip the Lambda resource during rollback. Then manually restore the Lambda function to the working state compatible with the new database schema.
Use the CloudFormation Drift Detection feature to identify the specific resource causing the rollback failure and manually fix it.
explain: When a CloudFormation rollback fails, the stack enters ROLLBACK_FAILED state. The ContinueRollBack API (or console option) allows specifying resources to skip during rollback. Skipping the Lambda function allows the rest of the stack's rollback to complete. After the stack returns to a stable state, the SysOps administrator manually updates the Lambda function to a version compatible with the new (post-migration) database schema, accepting that a forward-fix is needed rather than a true rollback. Deleting the stack would destroy all resources including the updated database. AWS Support cannot fix application-level conflicts. Drift Detection identifies configuration differences from the template but doesn't resolve rollback failures.

q: A company uses Amazon API Gateway with multiple APIs. The security team wants to ensure that all API requests are inspected for common web attacks (SQL injection, cross-site scripting) and that requests exceeding a rate of 1,000 requests per minute per IP address are automatically blocked. The solution must apply to all APIs with minimal per-API configuration. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Enable Amazon Shield Advanced on the API Gateway endpoints to protect against web attacks and apply rate limiting.

Create an AWS WAF Web ACL with AWS Managed Rules for Common Threat Protection (AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet) and a rate-based rule with a limit of 1,000 requests per 5 minutes per IP. Associate the Web ACL with the API Gateway stage. Create a WAF rule group with these rules and apply it to all existing and new API Gateway stages.
Configure API Gateway usage plans with throttling limits of 1,000 requests per minute per API key and require all API consumers to use API keys.
Create Lambda authorizers for each API that inspect requests for SQL injection patterns and return 403 for requests from IP addresses exceeding rate limits.
explain: AWS WAF Web ACLs can be associated with API Gateway stages and apply to all methods in that stage. AWS Managed Rules for Common Threats (AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet) provide pre-built rules for SQL injection, XSS, and other common attacks maintained by AWS. Rate-based rules track request rates per IP and automatically block when the threshold is exceeded. Creating a reusable rule group and associating the Web ACL with each API Gateway stage minimizes per-API configuration. API Gateway usage plans with API keys only throttle clients with known API keys and don't protect against anonymous attack traffic. Shield Advanced handles volumetric DDoS, not application-layer attacks. Lambda authorizers require custom code maintenance for every API.

q: A company's AWS bill shows unexpected charges for data transfer between an Amazon EC2 instance and an Amazon S3 bucket in the same AWS Region. Investigation shows the EC2 instance is accessing S3 using the public S3 endpoint (s3.amazonaws.com), routing traffic through the internet and incurring data transfer charges. The company wants to eliminate these S3 data transfer costs. Which solution eliminates the charges with the LEAST effort?
options: Create a gateway VPC endpoint for Amazon S3 in the VPC. Update the VPC route tables to route S3 traffic through the VPC endpoint. EC2 instances in the VPC will access S3 through the endpoint at no data transfer cost, and all S3 traffic remains within the AWS network.
Move the S3 bucket to the same Availability Zone as the EC2 instance to eliminate cross-AZ charges.
Compress data before transferring it from EC2 to S3 to reduce the volume of data transferred and lower costs.
Use Amazon CloudFront in front of the S3 bucket to cache content closer to the EC2 instance and reduce data transfer.
explain: A gateway VPC endpoint for S3 is free to create and eliminates data transfer charges between EC2 instances in the VPC and S3 in the same region. Traffic routes through the AWS internal network rather than through the public internet, avoiding internet data transfer charges. Only a route table update is required — no application changes are needed since the S3 API endpoint DNS name resolves to private addresses through the VPC endpoint. S3 buckets are a global namespace and don't have AZ-specific placement. CloudFront adds cost for serving S3 content and doesn't eliminate EC2-to-S3 transfer charges. Compression reduces data volume but doesn't eliminate the per-GB data transfer charge.

q: A company runs a critical application on Amazon EC2. During peak load testing, the application suffers from noisy neighbor issues — other workloads on the same physical host are consuming resources that degrade the application's performance. The company wants to guarantee dedicated hardware resources for the application without sharing with any other AWS customers. Which EC2 option meets this requirement?
options: Launch EC2 instances on Dedicated Hosts. Dedicated Hosts provide an entire physical server dedicated to a single customer, ensuring no sharing with other AWS customers. The company can use existing per-socket, per-core, or per-VM software licenses and guarantee full hardware resources.
Launch EC2 instances in a cluster placement group to ensure all instances run on the same physical rack for predictable network performance.
Enable EC2 Enhanced Networking on the instances to dedicate network resources and prevent bandwidth sharing with other instances.
Use EC2 Dedicated Instances to launch the application on hardware dedicated to the company's AWS account, preventing sharing with other AWS customers.
explain: Both Dedicated Hosts and Dedicated Instances provide hardware isolation from other AWS customers. However, Dedicated Hosts provide a specific physical server (with known socket and core counts) that is dedicated to the customer — enabling per-socket, per-core, and per-VM license compliance and full hardware resource guarantees. Dedicated Instances provide hardware isolation but the specific physical server may change between instance starts. Both prevent noisy neighbor issues, but Dedicated Hosts provide the strongest hardware guarantee. Cluster placement groups optimize network performance between instances, not hardware isolation. Enhanced Networking improves network performance but doesn't isolate compute resources.

q: A company runs workloads in multiple AWS accounts using AWS Organizations. The SysOps team wants to create a standardized monitoring baseline across all accounts — specifically ensuring CloudWatch alarms are configured for EC2 CPU utilization, RDS connection count, and Lambda error rates in every account. New accounts should automatically receive these alarms. Which solution achieves this with the LEAST ongoing maintenance?
options: Write a Lambda function triggered by Organizations CreateAccount events to deploy CloudWatch alarms in each new account. Manually deploy alarms to existing accounts using the AWS CLI.

Create an AWS CloudFormation template that defines the standard CloudWatch alarms. Deploy the template to all existing accounts using CloudFormation StackSets with automatic deployment enabled for the organization. New accounts added to the target OUs automatically receive the alarm configuration.
Use AWS Systems Manager State Manager to apply CloudWatch alarm configuration documents to all EC2 instances across accounts.
Create CloudWatch alarms manually in each of the 50 accounts and document the process for future account onboarding.
explain: CloudFormation StackSets with Organizations integration and automatic deployment set to ENABLED automatically deploys the stack (containing CloudWatch alarms) to all existing accounts in the target OUs and to any new accounts added to those OUs. This ensures consistent monitoring baseline with zero ongoing maintenance — new accounts receive alarms automatically without any manual action. Lambda triggered by CreateAccount handles new accounts but requires manual deployment to existing ones. State Manager manages EC2 software state, not account-level CloudWatch alarm configuration. Manual alarm creation doesn't scale and creates inconsistent baselines.

q: A SysOps administrator must ensure that all AWS Lambda function invocation errors across 15 microservices are captured and alert the on-call team within 2 minutes of occurrence. The team uses Amazon CloudWatch for observability. Which solution meets these requirements with the LEAST operational overhead?
options: Deploy a custom Lambda function that queries CloudWatch Logs Insights every minute for errors across all functions and sends an SNS notification when errors are found
Create a CloudWatch Dashboard with Lambda error metrics, configure a CloudWatch Synthetics canary to check the dashboard every minute, and trigger an SNS notification on failure
Enable AWS X-Ray tracing on all Lambda functions, create X-Ray groups for error traces, and configure X-Ray Insights notifications to the on-call team
Create a CloudWatch Metric Filter on /aws/lambda/* log groups for error patterns, create a CloudWatch Alarm with a 1-minute period, and configure an SNS topic to notify the on-call team
explain: CloudWatch Metric Filters on Lambda log groups can detect error patterns in real-time and trigger CloudWatch Alarms within 1 minute, which then notify via SNS — this is the native, lowest-overhead solution. X-Ray Insights are useful for tracing but are not designed for real-time alerting within 2 minutes. CloudWatch Synthetics monitors endpoints, not application errors. A custom polling Lambda adds unnecessary complexity and operational burden.

q: A company runs a stateful web application on Amazon EC2 instances behind an Application Load Balancer. During a planned maintenance window, the operations team needs to drain connections from one instance, perform OS patching, and return it to service — all without impacting active user sessions. What is the correct sequence of actions?
options: Enable connection draining on the ALB, terminate the instance, launch a patched AMI replacement, and register the new instance with the target group

Set the instance's state to 'draining' in the ALB target group, wait for existing connections to complete, stop the instance, apply patches, start the instance, and re-register it with the target group
Take an AMI snapshot of the instance, stop it, apply patches, start it, and invalidate the ALB's session cache
Remove the instance from the target group, immediately stop it, apply patches, start it, and add it back to the target group
explain: The correct approach is to put the target into 'draining' state, which causes the ALB to stop sending new requests while allowing in-flight requests to complete up to the deregistration delay timeout (default 300 seconds). After draining completes, the instance can be stopped safely, patched, restarted, and re-registered. Immediately stopping without draining would drop active connections. Terminating the instance loses the original instance. There is no ALB session cache to invalidate.

q: A financial services company requires that all Amazon S3 buckets in their AWS account block public access and enforce encryption at rest. A SysOps administrator must implement a preventive control that automatically remediates any non-compliant bucket within 5 minutes of creation. Which approach meets this requirement?
options: Create an AWS Config rule for S3 bucket public access and encryption, attach an AWS Systems Manager Automation remediation action to apply the settings automatically on non-compliant resources
Create an SCP in AWS Organizations that denies s3:CreateBucket unless the request includes encryption and public access block parameters
Enable Amazon Macie on the account to detect public buckets and trigger a Lambda function via EventBridge to apply the required settings
Use AWS Security Hub with the CIS AWS Foundations benchmark to detect non-compliant buckets and manually remediate within the SLA window
explain: AWS Config with automatic SSM Automation remediation provides both detection and automatic remediation — Config evaluates new buckets and triggers remediation within minutes. Macie focuses on sensitive data discovery, not bucket policy enforcement, and adding Lambda introduces more complexity. Security Hub with CIS benchmark requires manual remediation, which cannot meet the 5-minute SLA. An SCP can prevent creation without the parameters but cannot remediate buckets created before the SCP was applied or by root, and cannot fix existing non-compliant buckets.

q: An e-commerce company uses AWS CodeDeploy to deploy application updates to a fleet of 200 Amazon EC2 instances. During a recent deployment, a bug was introduced that caused 15% of instances to fail their health checks. The deployment rolled back automatically but left 30 instances in a failed state. How should the SysOps administrator restore the fleet to a healthy state?
options: Re-deploy the last known-good application revision using CodeDeploy targeting only the failed instances using a deployment group filter, then verify health checks pass
SSH into each of the 30 failed instances, manually run the CodeDeploy agent installation script, and restart the CodeDeploy agent service
Terminate the 30 failed instances, allow Auto Scaling to replace them with fresh instances, and wait for the new instances to pull the last successful deployment
Create a new deployment group targeting only the 30 failed instances, deploy the current (buggy) revision to reset agent state, then deploy the good revision
explain: The correct recovery is to re-deploy the last known-good revision to only the affected instances. CodeDeploy tracks revisions and allows targeted redeployment to specific instances, making this the fastest and lowest-overhead path to recovery. Terminating and replacing instances via Auto Scaling would work but is slower and wastes existing capacity. Manually running the agent script does not redeploy the application. Deploying the buggy revision first is unnecessary and risky.

q: A company has two VPCs in the same AWS Region: VPC-A (10.0.0.0/16) hosting a production database and VPC-B (10.1.0.0/16) hosting application servers. The application servers must reach the database on port 5432 only. The security team requires that no other traffic flow between the VPCs. What is the MOST operationally efficient solution?
options: Create a VPC peering connection between VPC-A and VPC-B, update route tables to add specific routes for the database subnet CIDR only, and configure security groups to allow only port 5432 from the application server security group
Deploy an AWS Transit Gateway, attach both VPCs, create Transit Gateway route tables that allow only 10.1.0.0/16 → 10.0.0.0/16 on port 5432, and deny all other routes
Deploy a Network Load Balancer in VPC-A for the database, create a VPC endpoint service, and create an interface VPC endpoint in VPC-B to access the database privately
Establish a Site-to-Site VPN between VPC-A and VPC-B with route filters limiting traffic to the database subnet and port 5432
explain: VPC peering with route table entries scoped to the database subnet CIDR and security groups allowing only port 5432 from the app server security group is the simplest, lowest-cost solution for this use case. The security group configuration ensures no other traffic flows between the VPCs. PrivateLink (NLB + endpoint service) is valid but adds cost and complexity for a direct VPC-to-VPC scenario. Transit Gateway is over-engineered for two VPCs and adds per-attachment and per-GB charges. Site-to-Site VPN is designed for on-premises connectivity, not VPC-to-VPC.

q: A company runs a batch processing workload on Amazon EC2 that processes data every night from 10 PM to 6 AM. The workload is fault-tolerant and can resume from checkpoints if interrupted. The current setup uses On-Demand instances at a cost of $800/month. The operations team wants to reduce costs by at least 60%. Which solution achieves this?
options: Use EC2 Savings Plans covering 100% of the batch compute usage to achieve maximum discount
Switch to AWS Fargate Spot for the batch workload and configure ECS task retry logic on task failures
Purchase 1-year No Upfront Reserved Instances for the batch processing fleet to lock in a lower hourly rate
Replace On-Demand instances with Spot Instances using an EC2 Auto Scaling group with a mixed instances policy, configure checkpointing so the workload can resume after Spot interruptions
explain: Spot Instances offer up to 90% savings compared to On-Demand, easily exceeding the 60% target. Because the workload is fault-tolerant and uses checkpointing, Spot interruptions (with 2-minute warning) can be handled gracefully. A mixed instances policy across multiple instance types and Availability Zones reduces interruption risk. Reserved Instances provide ~30-40% savings but require 1-year commitment and are less effective for intermittent 8-hour nightly workloads. Fargate Spot is for containers, not EC2 workloads. Savings Plans covering 100% of usage would still be limited to ~66% savings maximum.

q: A SysOps administrator needs to track the number of failed SSH login attempts across a fleet of 500 Amazon EC2 Linux instances and alert the security team when any single instance exceeds 10 failed attempts within a 5-minute window. Which solution implements this with MINIMAL custom development?
options: Use Amazon Inspector to continuously assess all instances for SSH brute-force patterns and configure Inspector findings to trigger SNS notifications

Install the CloudWatch Agent on all instances with a configuration that collects /var/log/secure, create a CloudWatch Metric Filter to count authentication failure patterns, and create a CloudWatch Alarm with a 5-minute period threshold of 10
Deploy an AWS WAF rule to block IPs after 10 failed SSH attempts and configure WAF logs to send alerts to the security team
Enable AWS CloudTrail and create an EventBridge rule that triggers a Lambda function to parse CloudTrail SSH events and publish a custom metric to CloudWatch
explain: The CloudWatch Agent can tail /var/log/secure (which logs SSH authentication failures on Linux) and stream it to CloudWatch Logs. A Metric Filter then counts lines matching 'Failed password' patterns and publishes a metric. A CloudWatch Alarm set to 5-minute periods with threshold of 10 triggers SNS notification — this is the native solution requiring no custom Lambda code. CloudTrail captures AWS API calls, not OS-level authentication events. Amazon Inspector performs vulnerability scanning, not real-time log analysis for brute-force detection. AWS WAF operates at the HTTP layer, not the SSH/TCP layer.

q: A company's Amazon RDS Multi-AZ MySQL instance experiences a failover that takes 90 seconds, causing application errors because the application caches the database endpoint IP address. After the failover, the application cannot connect to the new primary instance. How should the SysOps administrator fix the application connection issue with the LEAST code changes?
options: Update the application to use the RDS endpoint DNS name (not the IP address) and reduce the DNS TTL cache in the application's JVM or connection pool settings
Place a Network Load Balancer in front of RDS and have the application connect to the NLB endpoint, updating NLB target groups after failover
Replace the RDS Multi-AZ instance with an Aurora Multi-AZ cluster, which uses a cluster endpoint that automatically routes to the current primary
Configure an Elastic IP address on the RDS instance and update the application to use the Elastic IP, then remap the Elastic IP after failover
explain: The root cause is DNS caching — the application caches the IP behind the RDS endpoint DNS name, so after failover to the new primary (at a different IP), the cached IP no longer works. The fix is to ensure the application resolves the DNS name fresh on reconnection by reducing DNS TTL caching in the JVM (via `networkaddress.cache.ttl=0` or similar) and using the RDS endpoint DNS name. This requires minimal code change. Aurora is a valid alternative but requires migrating the entire database. RDS instances do not support Elastic IPs. An NLB in front of RDS is an anti-pattern and adds latency and cost.

q: A company needs to ensure that all IAM users in their AWS account use MFA for console sign-in. Existing users without MFA should be prevented from accessing any AWS services until they enroll. The solution must enforce this without disrupting users who already have MFA enabled. What is the recommended approach?
options: Enable Amazon Cognito as an identity provider for the AWS Management Console, requiring MFA at the Cognito level before federation to IAM
Attach an IAM policy to all users (or a group) that denies all actions except iam:CreateVirtualMFADevice, iam:EnableMFADevice, and iam:GetUser when the condition aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent is false
Create a Service Control Policy that denies all actions unless aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent is true and attach it to the root of the AWS Organizations OU
Enable AWS Config rule 'iam-user-mfa-enabled', configure automatic remediation to disable non-compliant user credentials until MFA is added
explain: The standard AWS pattern for enforcing MFA enrollment is a deny-all IAM policy conditioned on `aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent = false`, with explicit Allow for the specific IAM actions needed to enroll MFA (CreateVirtualMFADevice, EnableMFADevice, GetUser). Users with MFA already enrolled will have `aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent = true` in their session and the deny won't apply. AWS Config can detect non-compliance but automatic remediation that disables credentials is disruptive. An SCP would affect all principals including service roles. Cognito federation is complex and not a standard approach for IAM user MFA enforcement.

q: A company uses AWS CloudFormation to manage infrastructure. After a stack update, the update failed and CloudFormation rolled back, but three Amazon EC2 instances remain in a 'UPDATE_ROLLBACK_FAILED' state. The team cannot delete or update the stack. How should the SysOps administrator recover the stack?
options: Manually terminate the three failed EC2 instances from the EC2 console, then trigger a CloudFormation stack update to replace them
Use the CloudFormation ContinueUpdateRollback API with the SkipResources parameter to skip the three problematic EC2 instances and complete the rollback, then manually fix or replace those instances
Use AWS Systems Manager Automation to run the AWS-RestartEC2Instance document on the three failed instances, which will resolve the CloudFormation rollback state
Delete the entire CloudFormation stack using the retain resources option, then recreate the stack from scratch with the corrected template
explain: When a CloudFormation stack is in UPDATE_ROLLBACK_FAILED state, the correct recovery is ContinueUpdateRollback with SkipResources to skip the resources that are blocking the rollback. This allows CloudFormation to complete the rollback for all other resources. The skipped resources must then be manually remediated (fixed or deleted) outside of CloudFormation, and the stack can be updated normally afterward. Deleting the stack would destroy all managed resources. Restarting EC2 instances via SSM does not affect CloudFormation's stack state. Manually terminating instances could cause the stack to reference non-existent resources.

q: A company's AWS bill increased by 40% last month. The SysOps administrator discovers that 200 GB of Amazon S3 data retrieval costs from S3 Glacier is driving the increase. The data is retrieved on average once per quarter by the analytics team. What change would MOST reduce these retrieval costs going forward?
options: Move the data from S3 Glacier to S3 Glacier Deep Archive to reduce storage costs, while keeping the same Expedited retrieval tier
Create an S3 Lifecycle policy to move data from Glacier back to S3 Standard-IA to eliminate retrieval fees
Switch the retrieval type from Expedited to Bulk retrieval for Glacier objects, accepting a 5-12 hour retrieval time instead of 1-5 minutes
Enable S3 Intelligent-Tiering on the bucket so data automatically moves between access tiers based on retrieval patterns
explain: Glacier Bulk retrieval costs $0.0025/GB versus Expedited at $0.03/GB — a 12x cost reduction. Since the analytics team only retrieves data quarterly and can plan ahead, the 5-12 hour bulk retrieval window is acceptable. Glacier Deep Archive has even lower storage costs but higher retrieval costs for non-Deep-Archive tiers, and retrievals from Deep Archive are even slower — this doesn't solve the retrieval cost problem. S3 Intelligent-Tiering doesn't manage Glacier retrieval costs. Moving data back to S3 Standard-IA eliminates Glacier retrieval fees but dramatically increases ongoing storage costs (Standard-IA at $0.0125/GB/month vs Glacier at $0.004/GB/month).

q: A company's web application on Amazon EC2 is receiving intermittent HTTP 504 Gateway Timeout errors from the Application Load Balancer. The EC2 instances are healthy and responding within 200ms for most requests, but some requests that involve database queries take up to 65 seconds. What is the MOST likely cause and fix?
options: The ALB target group health check interval is too short, causing the ALB to mark instances as unhealthy during long-running requests; increase the health check interval to 120 seconds
The database is in a different Availability Zone than the EC2 instances, causing network latency that exceeds the ALB timeout; move the database to the same AZ
The EC2 instance security group is blocking return traffic on high-numbered ports; add an outbound rule to allow all traffic from the EC2 instances
The ALB idle timeout is set to 60 seconds (default), which is less than the 65-second database query time; increase the ALB idle timeout to at least 70 seconds
explain: The ALB idle timeout defaults to 60 seconds. If a request takes longer than 60 seconds without any data being sent back, the ALB closes the connection and returns a 504. Database queries taking 65 seconds exceed this timeout. Increasing the ALB idle timeout to 70+ seconds allows the long-running requests to complete. Security group blocking return traffic would cause consistent failures for all requests, not just slow ones. Health check intervals don't affect in-flight request handling. Cross-AZ latency is typically <1ms and cannot explain a 65-second query time.
